topics.title: Fundamentals in Computer Vision: Camera Calibration - Pinhole Camera Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals in Computer Vision: Camera Calibration - Pinhole Camera Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Pinhole Camera Model** is the fundamental geometric model of image formation in computer vision. It describes the mathematical relationship between the coordinates of a 3D point in the real world and its 2D projection onto the image plane, assuming the camera behaves as an ideal pinhole (aperture of infinitesimal size) with no lens-based distortion. The model is defined by two sets of parameters:

- **Intrinsic Parameters** (Internal camera geometry): Focal length $f$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, and skew coefficient $\gamma$.
- **Extrinsic Parameters** (Camera pose in world): Rotation matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ and translation vector $T \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Camera calibration is the process of estimating the intrinsic and extrinsic parameters (and distortion coefficients) of a given camera using known calibration targets (e.g., a checkerboard pattern). This is the **first and most critical step** in any computer vision pipeline such as structure-from-motion, SLAM, stereo vision, and 3D reconstruction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **darkened room (camera obscura)** with a single tiny pinhole on one wall. Outside the room, light rays from a tree, a person, and a car travel in straight lines, pass through this one tiny hole, and project an **inverted image** of the outside world on the opposite wall inside the room.

This is exactly what a digital camera does:
- The **pinhole** = the aperture of the camera.
- The **back wall** = the image sensor (CCD/CMOS).
- The **inverted image** = the digital photograph (we just flip it digitally for convenience).

**Why does this matter?** Because if we know the exact geometry of this "dark room" (the focal length, where the pinhole is, and how the camera is oriented in 3D space), we can **mathematically reverse-engineer** any 2D pixel in a photograph back to a 3D ray in the real world. This reverse mapping is the foundation of 3D vision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The standard image formation model assumes the image plane is placed at distance $f$ (the focal length) in **front of the optical center** (virtual image plane) rather than behind it, to avoid dealing with the inverted image algebraically. This is a mathematical convenience, not a physical one.

---

### 1.3 Physical Setup & Coordinate Systems

A pinhole camera model involves **four coordinate systems** that must be carefully distinguished:

1. **World Coordinate System $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$:** The global 3D reference frame of the real scene.
2. **Camera Coordinate System $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$:** Origin at the optical center (pinhole), $Z_c$ axis pointing along the optical axis.
3. **Image Plane Coordinate System $(x, y)$:** Continuous 2D coordinates on the image plane (in mm or meters).
4. **Pixel Coordinate System $(u, v)$:** Discrete integer coordinates on the digital image (in pixels), with origin at the top-left corner.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **principal point** $(c_x, c_y)$ with the origin of the image. The principal point is the point where the optical axis intersects the image plane, and in an ideal sensor it is at the geometric center of the image. Real sensors often have $(c_x, c_y) \neq (W/2, H/2)$ due to manufacturing imperfections.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Projection Pipeline

The complete pinhole projection pipeline follows **four sequential transformation steps**:

1. **World → Camera Transformation (Extrinsic):** Convert world coordinates to camera coordinates using rotation $R$ and translation $T$.

   $$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = R \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + T$$

2. **Perspective Projection (Intrinsic - Linear Part):** Project the 3D camera coordinates onto the 2D image plane using similar triangles.

   $$x = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

3. **Image → Pixel Conversion (Intrinsic - Affine Part):** Convert metric image coordinates to pixel coordinates using scaling factors $s_x, s_y$ and principal point offsets $c_x, c_y$.

   $$u = s_x \cdot x + c_x, \quad v = s_y \cdot y + c_y$$

4. **Distortion Correction (Optional, Real Cameras):** Apply radial and tangential distortion models to account for real lens imperfections.

   $$x_{distorted} = x \cdot (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + 2p_1 xy + p_2(r^2 + 2x^2)$$

   $$y_{distorted} = y \cdot (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + p_1(r^2 + 2y^2) + 2p_2 xy$$

### 2.2 Why & How of Each Step

- **Step 1 (Extrinsic):** A camera in the real world is never aligned with the world axes. We need to express where the camera is and which way it is pointing. $R$ rotates world axes to camera axes, and $T$ translates the origin to the camera's optical center.
- **Step 2 (Projection):** This is the **heart of the pinhole model** — the projective division by $Z_c$ (perspective foreshortening). It encodes the "Why?" of perspective: distant objects appear smaller.
- **Step 3 (Pixelization):** The image plane is a continuous mathematical surface, but a digital image is a discrete grid. This step introduces the sensor's pixel density and offset.
- **Step 4 (Distortion):** The ideal pinhole model assumes zero lens distortion, but real cameras use lenses. Calibration must estimate and remove lens-induced errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Intrinsic parameters are **constant for a given camera** (they describe the camera itself), while extrinsic parameters **change every time the camera moves** (they describe the camera's pose). Calibration primarily estimates intrinsic parameters; extrinsics are typically estimated per-frame in applications like SLAM.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Description** | **Units / Type** | **Notes** |
|:-----------|:----------------|:-----------------|:----------|
| $f$ | Focal length | millimeters (mm) or pixels | Distance from pinhole to image plane |
| $c_x, c_y$ | Principal point coordinates | pixels | $(c_x, c_y) = (u, v)$ of optical axis intersection |
| $s_x, s_y$ | Pixel scaling factors | pixels/mm | Inverse of pixel pitch |
| $\gamma$ | Skew coefficient | dimensionless | Often $\gamma = 0$ for modern sensors |
| $R$ | Rotation matrix | $3 \times 3$ matrix, $R^T R = I$ | $\det(R) = +1$ |
| $T$ | Translation vector | $3 \times 1$ vector | World origin in camera coordinates |
| $K$ | Intrinsic matrix | $3 \times 3$ matrix | Upper triangular |
| $P$ | Camera projection matrix | $3 \times 4$ matrix | $P = K [R \mid T]$ |
| $k_1, k_2, k_3$ | Radial distortion coefficients | dimensionless | Increasing powers of $r^2$ |
| $p_1, p_2$ | Tangential distortion coefficients | dimensionless | From lens decentering |
| $r^2$ | Radial distance squared | $\text{pixels}^2$ | $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ |
| $\lambda$ | Homogeneous scale factor | scalar | Arbitrary; preserves projection |
| $\tilde{X}$ | Homogeneous coordinate of $X$ | vector | $\tilde{X} = [X; 1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When the problem statement gives a "calibrated camera" with $K$ known, you do **not** need to recompute $K$. When the focal length and image dimensions are given, you can usually assume $c_x = W/2$ and $c_y = H/2$ unless stated otherwise.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pinhole camera model and its calibration are foundational to:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Camera-based obstacle detection, lane detection, and depth estimation all rely on calibrated intrinsic parameters to map pixel offsets to real-world metric distances.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Apps like Pokémon GO and ARKit-based applications need to know the camera's pose in 3D space to overlay virtual objects correctly. Extrinsic calibration is critical here.
- **Medical Imaging:** Endoscopes and laparoscopes use calibrated pinhole models to perform 3D measurements inside the human body.
- **Industrial Metrology & Quality Inspection:** Calibrated cameras enable sub-millimeter 3D measurements for defect detection.
- **Photogrammetry:** Creating 3D maps from aerial photographs (used in Google Earth, surveying, agriculture).
- **Robotics (Visual SLAM):** Robots use calibrated cameras for simultaneous localization and mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> In real engineering practice, the most widely used calibration method is **Zhang's Method (2000)**, which uses a planar checkerboard pattern viewed from multiple orientations. It only requires the user to print a checkerboard, move it in front of the camera, and the algorithm estimates all intrinsic, extrinsic, and distortion parameters using a closed-form solution followed by nonlinear refinement.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Full Camera Projection Equation

**Objective:** Derive the closed-form equation that maps a 3D world point to a 2D pixel coordinate.

**Given:** A 3D point $P_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$ in the world coordinate system, and a calibrated camera with intrinsic matrix $K$, rotation $R$, and translation $T$.

**Step 1: World to Camera Transformation**

We first transform world coordinates to camera coordinates. The homogeneous form is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R & T \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Explanation:* The $4 \times 4$ matrix on the right is the **extrinsic matrix** $[R \mid T]$ augmented with a homogeneous bottom row $[0, 0, 0, 1]$ to preserve the homogeneous structure.

**Step 2: Perspective Projection onto the Image Plane**

Using similar triangles in the pinhole geometry, the image plane coordinates (in metric units) are:

$$x = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

*Explanation:* A point at distance $Z_c$ in front of the camera projects to a position that scales linearly with the depth. The "perspective divide" by $Z_c$ is the key source of **foreshortening** — points farther away appear smaller.

**Step 3: Conversion to Pixel Coordinates**

The pixel coordinates are obtained by:

$$u = s_x \cdot x + c_x, \quad v = s_y \cdot y + c_y$$

where $s_x, s_y$ are the number of pixels per millimeter (inverse of pixel pitch), and $c_x, c_y$ is the principal point in pixel coordinates.

**Step 4: Combined Intrinsic Matrix**

Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$u = f \cdot s_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x = \alpha \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$$

$$v = f \cdot s_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y = \beta \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

where $\alpha = f \cdot s_x$ and $\beta = f \cdot s_y$ are the focal lengths expressed in pixel units (along $u$ and $v$ axes respectively). Including a possible skew $\gamma$, the intrinsic matrix is:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & \beta & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Full Camera Projection in Homogeneous Form**

The complete projection can now be written as a single matrix equation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} R & T \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = P \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $P = K [R \mid T]$ is the $3 \times 4$ **camera projection matrix** and "$\sim$" denotes equality up to a nonzero scale factor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverse Projection (2D Pixel to 3D Ray)

**Objective:** Given a pixel $(u, v)$ and the camera's intrinsic matrix $K$, find the direction of the 3D ray passing through that pixel.

**Step 1: Convert Pixel to Normalized Camera Coordinates**

The inverse of the upper-triangular intrinsic matrix is:

$$K^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{\alpha} & \dfrac{-\gamma}{\alpha \beta} & \dfrac{\gamma c_y - \beta c_x}{\alpha \beta} \\ 0 & \dfrac{1}{\beta} & \dfrac{-c_y}{\beta} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Ray Direction in Camera Coordinates**

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = K^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This gives a 3D point in the camera's coordinate system that lies on the **principal axis line** (a ray from the origin).

**Step 3: Account for Camera Pose in World Coordinates**

$$\text{Ray}_{\text{world}}(d) = C + d \cdot R^T \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}$$

where $C = -R^T T$ is the camera center in world coordinates and $d$ is a free scalar parameter (depth).

*Explanation:* Since a single 2D pixel cannot recover absolute depth (the "depth ambiguity"), we recover only the **direction** of the ray. To get a 3D point we need additional information — either stereo cameras, structure from motion, or depth sensors (LiDAR, RGB-D).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Closed-Form Solution for Extrinsic Parameters (Given 3D-2D Correspondences)

**Objective:** Estimate $R$ and $T$ given known intrinsic matrix $K$ and $N \geq 3$ point correspondences $(X_w^{(i)}, u^{(i)})$.

**Step 1:** Define the **homography** $H = K [r_1 \mid r_2 \mid T]$ for the special case where all world points lie on a plane $Z_w = 0$ (Zhang's Method setup). Here $r_1, r_2$ are the first two columns of $R$.

**Step 2:** Since $K$ is known, the camera extrinsics are extracted as:

$$r_1 = \lambda K^{-1} h_1, \quad r_2 = \lambda K^{-1} h_2, \quad T = \lambda K^{-1} h_3$$

where $\lambda = 1 / \Vert K^{-1} h_1 \Vert$ is a normalization constant, and $h_i$ are the columns of the homography $H$.

**Step 3:** The third column is recovered using the orthonormality constraint of rotation matrices:

$$r_3 = r_1 \times r_2$$

**Step 4:** Because of noise, the recovered $R = [r_1 \mid r_2 \mid r_3]$ may not be a perfect rotation matrix. A **Procrustes projection** is applied:

$$R_{optimal} = U V^T \quad \text{from SVD of } R = U \Sigma V^T$$

*Explanation:* The Procrustes step finds the closest valid rotation matrix (in Frobenius norm) to the noisy estimate. This is a standard post-processing step in Zhang's calibration algorithm.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Table

| **Transformation Stage** | **Input** | **Output** | **Equation** | **Matrix Size** |
|:------------------------|:----------|:-----------|:-------------|:----------------|
| World → Camera | $P_w$ | $P_c$ | $P_c = R P_w + T$ | $3 \times 1$ |
| Perspective Projection | $P_c$ | $(x, y)$ | $x = f X_c / Z_c$ | $2 \times 1$ |
| Image → Pixel | $(x, y)$ | $(u, v)$ | $u = \alpha x + c_x$ | $2 \times 1$ |
| Combined (Full Projection) | $P_w$ | $(u, v)$ | $\tilde{p} \sim K[R \mid T] \tilde{P}_w$ | $3 \times 4$ matrix $P$ |
| Inverse Projection | $(u, v)$ | Ray in 3D | $P_c = K^{-1} \tilde{p}$ | $3 \times 3$ inverse $K^{-1}$ |
| Distortion Model | $(x, y)$ | $(x_d, y_d)$ | Radial + Tangential polynomial | scalar equations |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "$\sim$" symbol** in the homogeneous projection equation means "equal up to a scale factor." This is because homogeneous coordinates are invariant to scalar multiplication — only the **direction** of the resulting vector matters, not its magnitude. The actual pixel coordinates are obtained by dividing the first two components by the third.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Pinhole Camera Geometry & Image Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["World Point P_w = X_w, Y_w, Z_w"]
    B["Camera Frame Transform"]
    C["Camera Point P_c = X_c, Y_c, Z_c"]
    D["Perspective Division by Z_c"]
    E["Image Plane Point x, y in mm"]
    F["Pixel Conversion with Intrinsic K"]
    G["Pixel Coordinate u, v in pixels"]
    H["Apply Distortion k1, k2, p1, p2"]
    I["Final Image Pixel Output"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I

    subgraph EXTRINSIC["Extrinsic Block R, T"]
        B
    end

    subgraph INTRINSIC["Intrinsic Block K = alpha, beta, gamma, cx, cy"]
        D
        F
    end

    subgraph DISTORTION["Distortion Block k1, k2, p1, p2"]
        H
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Zhang's Checkerboard Calibration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Capture N >= 5 Checkerboard Images from Different Angles"]
    B["Detect Corners in Each Image using cv2.findChessboardCorners"]
    C["Refine Corners to Subpixel Accuracy"]
    D["Set up 2D-3D Correspondences with Known Square Size"]
    E["Estimate Homography H for Each View via DLT"]
    F["Solve Intrinsic Matrix K using Orthogonality Constraint"]
    G["Extract Extrinsics R, T per View"]
    H["Estimate Distortion Coefficients k1, k2, p1, p2"]
    I["Nonlinear Refinement using Levenberg-Marquardt"]
    J["Final Calibration Parameters K, D, R, T"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Camera Calibration System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["Calibration Target Checkerboard or Grid"]
        I2["Image Sequence from Camera"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1["Feature Detection Module"]
        P2["Correspondence Matching"]
        P3["Linear Solver Closed Form"]
        P4["Nonlinear Optimizer LM"]
        P5["Distortion Estimator"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["Intrinsic Matrix K 3x3"]
        O2["Distortion Vector D 5 or 8 elements"]
        O3["Extrinsic Poses R, T per View"]
        O4["Reprojection Error Metric"]
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        AP1["3D Reconstruction"]
        AP2["AR Overlay"]
        AP3["Stereo Vision"]
        AP4["Visual Odometry"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    P5 --> O4
    O1 --> AP1
    O2 --> AP2
    O3 --> AP3
    O4 --> AP4
```

### 4.4 Coordinate System Transformation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["World Frame X_w, Y_w, Z_w"]
    B["Camera Frame X_c, Y_c, Z_c"]
    C["Image Frame x, y in mm"]
    D["Pixel Frame u, v in pixels"]

    A -- R, T --> B
    B -- Pinhole Projection f --> C
    C -- s_x, s_y, c_x, c_y --> D

    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#B0E0E6
    style C fill:#98FB98
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each colored box represents a coordinate system, and the arrow labels indicate the parameters used in the transformation. The **Y-axis** in the pinhole diagram points **downward** in the image plane convention used in OpenCV (this is the standard image coordinate convention with origin at the top-left).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define the **Pinhole Camera Model**. List and explain the **intrinsic parameters** of a camera with the standard intrinsic matrix $K$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Pinhole Camera Model** is the fundamental geometric model that describes how a 3D scene is projected onto a 2D image plane through an infinitesimally small aperture, assuming no lens distortion. **(1 mark)**

The **intrinsic parameters** describe the internal geometry of the camera and are represented by the **camera intrinsic matrix** $K$:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $f_x, f_y$ = Focal length in pixel units along $u$ and $v$ axes. **(0.5 marks)**
- $c_x, c_y$ = Principal point coordinates in pixel space (where the optical axis pierces the image plane). **(0.5 marks)**
- $\gamma$ = Skew coefficient between sensor axes; equals 0 for most modern sensors. **(0.5 marks)**
- $K$ is an **upper-triangular $3 \times 3$ matrix** with 5 degrees of freedom. **(0.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

Distinguish between **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** camera parameters with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Intrinsic Parameters** | **Extrinsic Parameters** |
|:-----------|:-------------------------|:--------------------------|
| Definition | Describe the camera's internal geometry | Describe the camera's pose in the world |
| Components | Focal length $f$, principal point $c_x, c_y$, skew $\gamma$ | Rotation matrix $R$, Translation vector $T$ |
| Dependency | Fixed for a given camera | Change with every camera movement |
| Matrix Form | Intrinsic matrix $K$ ($3 \times 3$) | Extrinsic matrix $[R \mid T]$ ($3 \times 4$) |
| Example | A 50mm lens with $f = 50$ mm | Camera placed 2m above the ground, tilted at 30° |

**Examples:** **(1 mark)**
- **Intrinsic example:** Two identical DSLR cameras have the same intrinsic parameters (assuming same sensor and lens).
- **Extrinsic example:** If the same DSLR is moved 90° around a tripod, only the extrinsic parameters change.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse $f$ (focal length) with the principal point. $f$ is the **distance** from the optical center to the image plane, while $(c_x, c_y)$ is the **location** of the principal point on the image plane in pixel units.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the **complete camera projection matrix** $P$ that maps a 3D world point $P_w$ to its 2D image pixel $(u, v)$, clearly explaining the role of intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. State the homogeneous equation and explain the significance of the homogeneous scale factor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A camera has focal length $f = 8$ mm, pixel size $s = 0.005$ mm/pixel, and principal point at the center of a $1920 \times 1080$ image. A 3D world point $P_w = (1.0, 0.5, 5.0)$ m is observed by a camera at world position $(0, 0, 0)$ with no rotation. Find the **pixel coordinates** of the projection. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A.(a):** **(7 Marks)**

The complete projection requires three sequential transformations: **(0.5 marks for stating the steps)**

**Step 1: World to Camera (Extrinsic)** — **(1.5 marks)**

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = R \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix} + T$$

In homogeneous form, the extrinsic transformation is expressed as the $3 \times 4$ matrix $[R \mid T]$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Perspective Projection** — **(1.5 marks)**

Using similar triangles from the pinhole geometry:

$$x = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

The division by $Z_c$ encodes the **perspective foreshortening effect**. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3: Image to Pixel (Intrinsic)** — **(1.5 marks)**

$$u = f \cdot s_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = f \cdot s_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

In matrix form, the intrinsic matrix is $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Full Homogeneous Equation** — **(1 mark)**

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} R & T \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = P \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The symbol "$\sim$" means equality up to a non-zero scale factor. The **homogeneous scale factor** is essential because homogeneous coordinates are invariant to scalar multiplication — only the **direction** of the resulting 3-vector matters. We always divide by the third component to recover the true $(u, v)$:

$$u = \frac{P_{11} X_w + P_{12} Y_w + P_{13} Z_w + P_{14}}{P_{31} X_w + P_{32} Y_w + P_{33} Z_w + P_{34}}, \quad v = \frac{P_{21} X_w + P_{22} Y_w + P_{23} Z_w + P_{24}}{P_{31} X_w + P_{32} Y_w + P_{33} Z_w + P_{34}}$$

---

**Solution to Q.A.(b):** **(7 Marks)**

**Given:** $f = 8$ mm, pixel size $s = 0.005$ mm/pixel, $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$, $P_w = (1.0, 0.5, 5.0)$ m, $R = I$, $T = 0$.

**Step 1: Compute intrinsic parameters in pixel units** **(1 mark)**

$$f_x = f_y = \frac{f}{s} = \frac{8}{0.005} = 1600 \text{ pixels}$$

$$c_x = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{1920}{2} = 960 \text{ pixels}$$

$$c_y = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{1080}{2} = 540 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2: Compute the intrinsic matrix** **(0.5 marks)**

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 1600 & 0 & 960 \\ 0 & 1600 & 540 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Apply the extrinsic transformation** **(1 mark)**

Since $R = I$ and $T = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 5.0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Convert to mm and apply the projection** **(2 marks)**

Converting to mm: $X_c = 1000$ mm, $Y_c = 500$ mm, $Z_c = 5000$ mm.

$$u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x = 1600 \cdot \frac{1000}{5000} + 960 = 1600 \cdot 0.2 + 960 = 320 + 960 = 1280$$

$$v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y = 1600 \cdot \frac{500}{5000} + 540 = 1600 \cdot 0.1 + 540 = 160 + 540 = 700$$

**Step 5: Final Answer and Validation** **(1 mark)**

The pixel coordinates are:

$$\boxed{(u, v) = (1280, 700) \text{ pixels}}$$

**Validation:** The image dimensions are $1920 \times 1080$. Since $0 \leq 1280 \leq 1920$ and $0 \leq 700 \leq 1080$, the projection lies within the image bounds, which is consistent with the world point being in front of the camera. **(0.5 marks)**

**Unit conversion check:** $f$ was given in mm and pixel size in mm/pixel, so the division $f/s$ correctly yields pixels. If the world point is in meters, we must convert to mm before applying the projection. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **radial and tangential lens distortion** models used in camera calibration. Write the mathematical equations and describe the physical origin of each type of distortion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Zhang's Planar Checkerboard Calibration Method** in detail. List the steps involved, the mathematical formulation, and the role of the homography matrix in the calibration process. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B.(a):** **(7 Marks)**

Real cameras use lenses to gather more light (compared to a pinhole), but lenses introduce **geometric distortions** that must be modeled and corrected. **(0.5 marks)**

**Radial Distortion** — **(3 marks)**

Radial distortion is caused by the **radial curvature of the lens** — light rays bend more at the edges than at the center. The correction model is:

$$x_{distorted} = x \cdot (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

$$y_{distorted} = y \cdot (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ is the squared radial distance from the principal point, and $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are the radial distortion coefficients.

**Types of radial distortion:** **(1 mark)**
- **Barrel distortion** ($k_1 < 0$): Image magnification **decreases** with distance from center. Common in wide-angle lenses. Edges appear "pulled in" like a barrel.
- **Pincushion distortion** ($k_1 > 0$): Image magnification **increases** with distance from center. Common in telephoto lenses. Edges appear "pinched in."

**Tangential Distortion** — **(2.5 marks)**

Tangential distortion arises when the **lens is not perfectly aligned** with the image sensor plane (decentering error). The correction model is:

$$x_{distorted} = x + 2 p_1 xy + p_2 (r^2 + 2x^2)$$

$$y_{distorted} = y + p_1 (r^2 + 2y^2) + 2 p_2 xy$$

where $p_1, p_2$ are the tangential distortion coefficients. These terms model the **slight tilting** of the lens relative to the sensor, which shifts points perpendicular to the radial direction.

**Summary:** The complete distortion model has 5 parameters: $k_1, k_2, k_3$ (radial) and $p_1, p_2$ (tangential). Most calibration routines use only $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2$ (4 parameters) and add $k_3$ only for very wide-angle lenses.

---

**Solution to Q.B.(b):** **(7 Marks)**

**Zhang's Method (2000)** is the **most widely used** camera calibration technique. It requires only a **flat checkerboard pattern** and a camera — no expensive 3D calibration rigs. **(0.5 marks)**

**Steps:** **(3 marks for steps, 4 marks for formulation)**

**Step 1: Capture Multiple Views** — Capture $N \geq 5$ images of a planar checkerboard pattern, moving it to different orientations and positions in front of the camera.

**Step 2: Detect Corners** — Detect the checkerboard corners in each image using algorithms like `findChessboardCorners` and refine to subpixel accuracy.

**Step 3: Define Correspondences** — For each view $i$, we have 2D-3D correspondences: world points on the plane $Z_w = 0$ and detected pixel locations $(u^{(i)}, v^{(i)})$.

**Step 4: Estimate Homography $H$** — For each view, compute the $3 \times 3$ homography $H_i$ using the Direct Linear Transformation (DLT) algorithm. The homography relates the planar world points to image pixels:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim H \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Extract Intrinsic Matrix $K$** — Since $H = K [r_1 \mid r_2 \mid T]$ and the columns $r_1, r_2$ of $R$ are orthonormal, we can derive the constraint:

$$h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0, \quad h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_1 = h_2^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2$$

Setting $B = K^{-T} K^{-1}$ (a symmetric $3 \times 3$ matrix with 6 unknowns), these become **linear equations** in the entries of $B$. With $\geq 3$ views, $B$ is solved, and $K$ is recovered via Cholesky decomposition of $B^{-1}$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 6: Extract Extrinsics** — Once $K$ is known, for each view:

$$\lambda = 1 / \Vert K^{-1} h_1 \Vert, \quad r_1 = \lambda K^{-1} h_1, \quad r_2 = \lambda K^{-1} h_2, \quad r_3 = r_1 \times r_2, \quad T = \lambda K^{-1} h_3$$

**Step 7: Estimate Distortion** — Minimize the **reprojection error** to estimate distortion coefficients $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \Vert p_{ij} - \hat{p}_{ij}(K, R_i, T_i, k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2) \Vert^2$$

**Step 8: Nonlinear Refinement** — Apply the **Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm** to jointly refine all parameters by minimizing the total reprojection error. **(1 mark)**

**Role of Homography:** The homography $H$ is the **bridge** between the 2D image and the 3D planar pattern. It encodes the perspective projection of a flat surface. Since the checkerboard is planar ($Z_w = 0$), the general $3 \times 4$ projection matrix $P$ reduces to a $3 \times 3$ homography $H$, simplifying the math significantly. **(0.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing mm, cm, m, and pixels in the projection equation. Always **convert to a consistent unit system** before applying $f \cdot X_c / Z_c$. If $f$ is in mm, $X_c, Y_c, Z_c$ must also be in mm.
> 2. **Forgetting the principal point:** Many students write $u = f_x \cdot X_c / Z_c$ **without** the $+ c_x$ term. The projection does not pass through the origin of the pixel coordinate system — it passes through the principal point.
> 3. **Confusing focal length in mm vs. pixels:** $f$ (in mm) ≠ $f_x$ (in pixels). The conversion is $f_x = f / s_x$, where $s_x$ is the pixel pitch in mm/pixel.
> 4. **Skipping the perspective divide:** In homogeneous coordinates, the pixel coordinates are obtained by **dividing** by the third component (the $W$ coordinate). Forgetting this gives incorrect results.
> 5. **Treating $R$ as a generic $3 \times 3$ matrix:** $R$ is a **rotation matrix** — its columns are orthonormal, and $\det(R) = +1$. Forgetting these constraints gives non-physical solutions.
> 6. **Mixing up the sign convention for $T$:** The extrinsic equation $P_c = R P_w + T$ means $T$ is the **world origin in camera coordinates**, not the camera position in world coordinates. The camera position is $C = -R^T T$.
> 7. **Forgetting distortion in numerical problems:** If a question explicitly mentions "real camera" or "with distortion," you **must** include the distortion model. If it says "ideal pinhole camera," you can omit it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Pinhole Camera Model** = ideal projection through an infinitesimally small aperture; no lens distortion; projects an inverted real image (or upright virtual image if image plane is in front).
- ⭐ **Four coordinate systems:** World $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ → Camera $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ → Image $(x, y)$ in mm → Pixel $(u, v)$.
- ⭐ **Intrinsic matrix $K$** is a $3 \times 3$ upper-triangular matrix with **5 degrees of freedom**: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$.
- ⭐ **Extrinsic matrix $[R \mid T]$** is a $3 \times 4$ matrix with **6 degrees of freedom** (3 for rotation, 3 for translation).
- ⭐ **Full projection matrix $P$** is a $3 \times 4$ matrix: $P = K [R \mid T]$.
- ⭐ **Homogeneous coordinates** allow the projection to be a single matrix multiply, but you **must divide by the third component** to recover $(u, v)$.
- ⭐ **Focal length $f$** is the distance from the optical center to the image plane (in mm) or its pixel equivalent $f_x = f/s_x$.
- ⭐ **Principal point $(c_x, c_y)$** is the intersection of the optical axis with the image plane, in pixel coordinates.
- ⭐ **Rotation matrix $R$** must satisfy $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$ (orthonormality constraints).
- ⭐ **Translation vector $T$** in the standard convention represents the world origin's coordinates in the camera frame, so the camera center in world coordinates is $C = -R^T T$.
- ⭐ **Radial distortion** is parameterized by $k_1, k_2, k_3$ and is a function of $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.
- ⭐ **Tangential distortion** is parameterized by $p_1, p_2$ and arises from lens decentering.
- ⭐ **Barrel distortion** ($k_1 < 0$) → wide-angle lenses; **Pincushion distortion** ($k_1 > 0$) → telephoto lenses.
- ⭐ **Zhang's Method** uses a planar checkerboard and requires $\geq 5$ views from different orientations.
- ⭐ **Homography $H$** is a $3 \times 3$ matrix that maps planar world points $(Z_w = 0)$ to image pixels.
- ⭐ **Reprojection error** is the gold-standard metric for calibration quality — should be **$< 0.5$ pixels** for a well-calibrated camera.
- ⭐ **The pinhole model cannot recover absolute scale from a single image** — we always need additional information (stereo, known object size, IMU, etc.) to get metric 3D reconstruction.
- ⭐ **Standard calibration order:** 1) Detect features → 2) Estimate homography per view → 3) Solve $K$ using orthogonality → 4) Extract extrinsics → 5) Estimate distortion → 6) Nonlinear refinement (Levenberg-Marquardt).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Image Features - Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometric Image Features - Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In Computer Vision, a **Geometric Image Feature** refers to a localized structure within an image that carries meaningful geometric information, such as points, edges, lines, or **curves**. Specifically, a **curve** is mathematically defined as a one-dimensional (1D) geometric primitive embedded in a 2D image plane, represented either implicitly as $f(x, y) = 0$ or parametrically as $(x(t), y(t))$, where $t$ is the parameter along the curve's arc length or domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** Curves are detected by linking contiguous edge pixels that share similar local geometric properties (orientation, curvature). They are considered higher-level features than isolated edge points because they encode *spatial coherence* and *shape information*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sketching a portrait. Before you can recognize a face, your brain first identifies the **outline** — the curves that form the jaw, the arc of an eyebrow, the contour of a nose. The image itself is just a 2D array of brightness values (pixels). A *curve* in this context is the digital equivalent of that outline — a connected chain of edge pixels that, when grouped together, reveals the shape of an object.

**Plain English Interpretation:**
- An **edge point** = "the brightness suddenly changes here."
- A **curve** = "a smooth chain of such points that together describe a boundary or a line in the scene."

Without curves, a vision system sees only noise and isolated dots. With curves, it begins to **understand shape and structure** — the foundation of object recognition, lane detection in autonomous vehicles, and medical image segmentation.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Image Plane Coordinates $(x, y)$**: measured in **pixels (px)**.
- **Gradient Magnitude $\vert \nabla I \vert$**: dimensionless intensity change per pixel.
- **Curvature $\kappa$**: measured in **pixels⁻¹**.
- **Hough Parameter $\rho$**: perpendicular distance from origin to the line, measured in **pixels**.
- **Hough Parameter $\theta$**: angle of the normal vector, measured in **radians (rad)** in the range $[0, \pi)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Module 1, the primary focus is on detecting and representing *parametric curves* (lines, circles, ellipses) using the **Hough Transform** and *robust fitting* using **RANSAC**.

## 1.4 Why Curves Matter in Computer Vision

Curves are crucial for:
1. **Object boundary extraction** (segmentation).
2. **Shape matching and recognition**.
3. **3D reconstruction** (line correspondences across views).
4. **Lane/road detection** in self-driving cars.
5. **Medical imaging** (vessel boundaries, organ contours).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Major Paradigms for Curve Extraction

There are two fundamental approaches to detecting curves in images:

### Paradigm A: Bottom-Up (Edge Linking)
1. Detect edge points using Canny/Sobel filters.
2. Group edge pixels into chains using local similarity (gradient orientation, proximity).
3. Fit analytic curves to these chains.

### Paradigm B: Top-Down (Hough Transform / Voting)
1. Define a parametric model (line, circle, ellipse).
2. Each edge pixel "votes" for all model parameters consistent with it.
3. The parameter combination with the most votes is the detected curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hough Transform** is **non-iterative** and **robust to noise and occlusions**, making it the most exam-relevant curve detection technique for KTU.

## 2.2 Parametric Representation of Common Curves

A curve in the image plane can be written as:

$$
\mathbf{C}(t) = (x(t), y(t))
$$

For specific geometric primitives:

| Curve Type | Parametric Form | Implicit Form | Parameters |
|------------|----------------|---------------|------------|
| **Straight Line** | $x = x_0 + at, \; y = y_0 + bt$ | $ax + by + c = 0$ | $a, b, c$ |
| **Hough Line** | — | $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$ | $\rho, \theta$ |
| **Circle** | $x = x_c + r\cos t, \; y = y_c + r\sin t$ | $(x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 = r^2$ | $x_c, y_c, r$ |
| **Ellipse** | $x = x_c + a\cos t, \; y = y_c + b\sin t$ | $\frac{(x-x_c)^2}{a^2} + \frac{(y-y_c)^2}{b^2} = 1$ | $x_c, y_c, a, b, \phi$ |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Hough Transform

The Hough Transform converts a *global pattern detection problem* (finding a line through many points) into a *local peak detection problem* in a parameter space.

**Steps:**
1. **Quantize** the parameter space $(\rho, \theta)$ into an accumulator array $A[\rho, \theta]$ initialized to zero.
2. **For each edge pixel** $(x_i, y_i)$:
   - For every quantized $\theta_k \in [0, \pi)$:
   - Compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta_k + y_i \sin\theta_k$.
   - Increment $A[\rho, \theta]$.
3. **Find local maxima** in $A$. Each maximum corresponds to a line in the image.

**Why does this work?**
Each edge point lies on infinitely many lines, parameterized by all possible $(\rho, \theta)$. In parameter space, these form a *sinusoidal curve*. If multiple points lie on the same line, their sinusoidal curves **intersect** at a common $(\rho, \theta)$ — producing a peak in the accumulator.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Range |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$ | Normal form of a line | $\rho \in [0, \rho_{max}]$, $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ |
| $(x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 = r^2$ | Circle equation | $r > 0$ |
| $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | Hough transform mapping | pixels, rad |
| $d = \frac{\vert ax_0 + by_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ | Perpendicular distance from point to line $ax+by+c=0$ | pixels |
| $\kappa = \frac{\vert x' y'' - y' x'' \vert}{(x'^2 + y'^2)^{3/2}}$ | Curvature of parametric curve | pixels⁻¹ |
| $N_{votes}(\rho, \theta) = \sum_{(x_i,y_i) \in E} \mathbb{1}\left[ (x_i\cos\theta + y_i\sin\theta) = \rho \right]$ | Hough accumulator count | dimensionless |
| $k = \frac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^n)}$ | RANSAC iterations for confidence $p$ | iterations |
| $L = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - (ax_i + b))^2$ | Least squares line fitting error | squared pixels |
| $\begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \end{bmatrix} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form LS solution | — |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU numerical problems, remember the **sign convention**: the perpendicular distance $\rho$ is always taken as **non-negative** by choosing $\theta \in [0, \pi)$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hough Line Transform** → Used in lane detection (autonomous vehicles, ADAS).
- **Hough Circle Transform** → Used in iris/pupil detection (biometrics), coin counting (vending machines), and traffic sign recognition.
- **RANSAC curve fitting** → Used in 3D point cloud processing, SLAM, and structure-from-motion for fitting primitives to noisy LiDAR data.
- **Active Contours (Snakes)** → Used in medical image segmentation (tumor boundary extraction).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Form Least-Squares Line Fitting

**Problem:** Given $N$ edge points $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$, find the line $y = ax + b$ that minimizes the sum of squared vertical residuals.

**Step 1: Define the cost function.**

The total squared error between observed $y_i$ and predicted $y = ax_i + b$ is:

$$
L(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - (a x_i + b) \right)^2
$$

**Step 2: Take partial derivatives and set to zero (necessary conditions for minimum).**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial a} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left( y_i - a x_i - b \right) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - a x_i - b \right) = 0
$$

**Step 3: Expand and simplify.**

From the second equation:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = a \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i + N b
$$

$$
b = \bar{y} - a \bar{x}
$$

where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i$ and $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum y_i$ are the sample means.

**Step 4: Substitute into the first equation and solve for $a$.**

$$
\sum x_i y_i = a \sum x_i^2 + b \sum x_i
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = a \sum x_i^2 + (\bar{y} - a \bar{x}) \sum x_i
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = a \sum x_i^2 + \bar{y} N \bar{x} - a N \bar{x}^2
$$

$$
a \left( \sum x_i^2 - N \bar{x}^2 \right) = \sum x_i y_i - N \bar{x} \bar{y}
$$

**Step 5: Final closed-form solution.**

$$
a = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

$$
b = \bar{y} - a \bar{x}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** solution. For curves where errors are present in both $x$ and $y$, **Total Least Squares (TLS)** is preferred — it minimizes the *perpendicular* distance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hough Transform Mapping for a Line

**Problem:** Show that a single point $(x_0, y_0)$ in image space corresponds to a sinusoid in the $(\rho, \theta)$ parameter space.

**Step 1: Equation of a general line through $(x_0, y_0)$.**

A line passing through $(x_0, y_0)$ with normal angle $\theta$ satisfies:

$$
x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta = \rho
$$

**Step 2: Treat $\rho$ as a function of $\theta$ (since $x_0, y_0$ are fixed).**

$$
\rho(\theta) = x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3: Recognize the sinusoidal form.**

Using the identity $A\cos\theta + B\sin\theta = R \cos(\theta - \phi)$ where $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$:

$$
\rho(\theta) = \sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2} \, \cos\!\left( \theta - \arctan\!\frac{y_0}{x_0} \right)
$$

This is a **cosine wave** in $\theta$ with amplitude $\sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2}$ and phase $\arctan(y_0/x_0)$. Hence, a point maps to a sinusoid.

**Step 4: Proof of coincidence for collinear points.**

If two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ lie on the same line, then there exists a single $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ satisfying:

$$
x_1 \cos\theta^* + y_1 \sin\theta^* = \rho^*
$$

$$
x_2 \cos\theta^* + y_2 \sin\theta^* = \rho^*
$$

Therefore, both sinusoidal curves **intersect at** $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ in Hough space. The Hough Transform exploits this coincidence by counting how many such curves pass through each cell — the cell with the highest count corresponds to the most likely line.

## 3.3 Derivation: RANSAC Iteration Count

**Problem:** Determine the minimum number of iterations $k$ required for RANSAC to find the correct model with probability $p$ (typically $p = 0.99$).

**Step 1: Let $w$ be the inlier ratio (fraction of data points consistent with the true model).**

The probability that a randomly chosen point is an inlier is $w$, and an outlier is $1 - w$.

**Step 2: For a model requiring $n$ points to be fitted, the probability that all $n$ sampled points are inliers is $w^n$.**

**Step 3: The probability that one iteration *fails* (i.e., at least one outlier is sampled) is $1 - w^n$.**

**Step 4: The probability that all $k$ iterations fail is $(1 - w^n)^k$.**

**Step 5: We require that the probability of *at least one success* equals $p$:**

$$
1 - (1 - w^n)^k = p
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $k$:**

$$
(1 - w^n)^k = 1 - p
$$

$$
k \ln(1 - w^n) = \ln(1 - p)
$$

$$
k = \frac{\ln(1 - p)}{\ln(1 - w^n)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU 14-mark problems**. Memorize the final boxed formula.

## 3.4 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Example:** Three edge points $(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 4)$ — fit a line using OLS.

**Step 1: Compute the means.**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2+3+4}{3} = 3
$$

**Step 2: Compute numerator and denominator for $a$.**

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = (1-2)(2-3) + (2-2)(3-3) + (3-2)(4-3) = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2
$$

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = (-1)^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 = 2
$$

**Step 3: Compute slope and intercept.**

$$
a = \frac{2}{2} = 1, \quad b = 3 - (1)(2) = 1
$$

**Step 4: Final fitted line.**

$$
y = x + 1
$$

This is consistent with the collinear points, as expected.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hough Transform Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Grayscale Conversion]
    B --> C[Edge Detection e.g. Canny]
    C --> D[Edge Pixel Set]
    D --> E[Initialize Accumulator Array A rho theta to Zero]
    E --> F[For Each Edge Pixel x_i y_i]
    F --> G[For Each theta in 0 to pi step delta theta]
    G --> H[Compute rho = x cos theta + y sin theta]
    H --> I[Increment A rho theta]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Done All theta]
    J --> F
    F --> K[Done All Pixels]
    K --> L[Find Local Maxima in A]
    L --> M[Threshold Peaks]
    M --> N[Output Detected Curves]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mermaid flowchart** above visualizes the **vote-and-accumulate** logic of the Hough Transform — the cornerstone algorithm for KTU curve detection problems.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: RANSAC Robust Curve Fitting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Edge Points with Outliers] --> B[Set iteration k to 0]
    B --> C[Randomly Sample n Points]
    C --> D[Fit Candidate Model to n Points]
    D --> E[Compute Residuals for ALL Points]
    E --> F[Count Inliers within Threshold t]
    F --> G{Inlier Count greater than Best?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update Best Model and Best Inlier Set]
    G -- No --> I[Discard Candidate]
    H --> J[Increment k]
    I --> J
    J --> K{k greater than k_max or converged?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Refit Model using ALL Inliers]
    L --> M[Output Robust Curve]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Curve Feature Extraction Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Image Pixels] --> B[Low Level Edges via Canny or Sobel]
    B --> C[Mid Level Curves via Edge Linking or Hough]
    C --> D[High Level Objects via Curve Matching]
    D --> E[Semantic Recognition e.g. lane car face tumor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **3-level hierarchy** is the standard way KTU examiners expect students to frame their answers. Always start with "edges → curves → objects" when describing feature extraction pipelines.

## 4.4 Schematic: Sinusoidal Mapping in Hough Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ImageSpace[Image Space xy]
        P1[Point 1 at x1 y1]
        P2[Point 2 at x2 y2]
        P3[Point 3 at x3 y3]
    end
    subgraph HoughSpace[Hough Space rho theta]
        S1[Sinusoid from P1]
        S2[Sinusoid from P2]
        S3[Sinusoid from P3]
        I[Intersection Peak rho asterisk theta asterisk]
    end
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S2
    P3 --> S3
    S1 --> I
    S2 --> I
    S3 --> I
```

The intersection point $I$ at $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ represents the **detected line** that passes through all three image-space points.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a curve in the context of image features. Differentiate between an edge point and a curve.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **curve** in image processing is a connected 1D geometric primitive in the 2D image plane, formed by linking contiguous edge pixels that share similar local orientation or curvature. It is represented parametrically as $(x(t), y(t))$ or implicitly as $f(x, y) = 0$.

| Feature | Edge Point | Curve |
|---------|-----------|-------|
| Dimensionality | 0D (single pixel) | 1D (chain of pixels) |
| Information | Local intensity discontinuity | Global shape/boundary |
| Detection | Gradient filter (Sobel, Canny) | Edge linking / Hough transform |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tabular differentiation, 1 mark for example.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is the accumulator array in the Hough Transform? Why is the Hough Transform robust to noise?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **accumulator array** $A[\rho, \theta]$ is a 2D histogram in the Hough parameter space where each cell counts the number of edge pixels consistent with a particular $(\rho, \theta)$ line hypothesis. **(1 mark)**

The Hough Transform is robust to noise because:
1. **Voting redundancy** — A single noisy pixel contributes votes to many cells, but only the *correct* cell receives votes from all true inlier points, creating a strong consensus peak. **(1 mark)**
2. **Local maxima are stable** — A few isolated outlier points cannot create a peak comparable to a true line supported by many inliers. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
Explain the **Hough Transform** algorithm for detecting straight lines in an image. Derive the equation that maps an image-space point to a sinusoid in Hough space.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Problem Setup** [1 mark]
Detecting lines in an image is difficult because of the infinite possibilities for slope and intercept. The Hough Transform reformulates the problem as **peak detection in a 2D parameter space**.

**Step 2: Normal Form of a Line** [1 mark]
A line in the image can be written in *normal form*:

$$
x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho
$$

where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the line, and $\theta$ is the angle of the normal vector.

**Step 3: Point-to-Sinusoid Mapping** [2 marks]
For a fixed point $(x_0, y_0)$, treat $\rho$ as a function of $\theta$:

$$
\rho(\theta) = x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $A\cos\theta + B\sin\theta = \sqrt{A^2+B^2}\cos(\theta - \phi)$:

$$
\rho(\theta) = \sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2} \, \cos\!\left( \theta - \arctan\frac{y_0}{x_0} \right)
$$

This is a **sinusoidal curve** in the $(\rho, \theta)$ plane.

**Step 4: Algorithm Steps** [2 marks]
1. Initialize accumulator $A[\rho, \theta] = 0$.
2. For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:
   - For each $\theta_k = k \cdot \Delta\theta$ where $k = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$:
     - Compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta_k + y_i \sin\theta_k$.
     - Increment $A[\rho, \theta_k]$.
3. Local maxima in $A$ correspond to detected lines.

**Step 5: Computational Complexity** [1 mark]
Complexity is $O(N \cdot M)$ where $N$ is the number of edge pixels and $M$ is the number of $\theta$ bins (typically $M \approx 180$ to $360$).

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
Using the Hough Transform, explain how the algorithm extends to **circle detection**. Discuss its computational complexity and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circle Equation** [1 mark]

$$
(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2
$$

Three parameters: center $(a, b)$ and radius $r$.

**Step 2: 3D Hough Space** [1 mark]
The accumulator becomes a 3D array $A[a, b, r]$. Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes along a **cone** in this space — for a fixed $r$, the center $(a, b)$ lies on a circle of radius $r$ around $(x_i, y_i)$.

**Step 3: Algorithm** [2 marks]
1. For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:
   - For each candidate radius $r_k$ in $[r_{min}, r_{max}]$:
     - For each angle $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$:
       - $a = x_i - r_k \cos\phi$
       - $b = y_i - r_k \sin\phi$
       - Increment $A[a, b, r_k]$.
2. Find peaks in $A$ — these are the detected circle centers and radii.

**Step 4: Complexity** [1 mark]
$O(N \cdot R \cdot \Phi)$ where $R$ is the number of radius bins and $\Phi$ is the angular resolution. This is significantly higher than line detection.

**Step 5: Limitations** [2 marks]
1. **Storage:** 3D accumulator is memory-intensive.
2. **Speed:** Cubic growth with parameter count.
3. **Sensitivity to $r$ range:** Must specify the radius range a priori.
4. **False peaks:** Can be mitigated by thresholding and non-maximum suppression.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
What is **RANSAC (Random Sample Consensus)**? Derive the formula for the number of iterations $k$ required to find a model with probability $p$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition** [1 mark]
RANSAC is a **robust estimation algorithm** that fits a mathematical model to data containing a significant proportion of outliers. It uses a *hypothesize-and-verify* strategy with random sampling.

**Step 2: Algorithm Steps** [1 mark]
1. Randomly sample the minimum number of points needed to fit the model (e.g., 2 for a line, 3 for a circle).
2. Fit the candidate model to this minimal sample.
3. Count the number of inliers (points within a threshold distance of the model).
4. Repeat for $k$ iterations, keeping the model with the most inliers.
5. Optionally, refit the model using all inliers of the best model.

**Step 3: Derivation of $k$** [3 marks]
- Let $w$ = inlier ratio, $n$ = sample size, $p$ = desired success probability.
- Probability one sampled point is an inlier = $w$.
- Probability all $n$ points are inliers = $w^n$.
- Probability of *failure* in one iteration = $1 - w^n$.
- Probability of failure in $k$ iterations = $(1 - w^n)^k$.
- Set $(1 - w^n)^k = 1 - p$:

$$
k = \frac{\ln(1 - p)}{\ln(1 - w^n)}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example** [1 mark]
For $w = 0.6$, $n = 2$, $p = 0.99$:

$$
k = \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(1 - 0.36)} = \frac{-4.605}{-0.470} \approx 9.8 \implies k = 10
$$

**Step 5: Advantages over OLS** [1 mark]
RANSAC is robust to outliers, whereas OLS is severely affected by even a single outlier (because squaring magnifies large residuals).

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
Fit a straight line $y = ax + b$ to the following data points using **Ordinary Least Squares**: $(0, 1), (1, 3), (2, 4), (3, 6)$. Show all steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Means** [1 mark]

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{0+1+2+3}{4} = 1.5, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1+3+4+6}{4} = 3.5
$$

**Step 2: Compute Deviations** [1 mark]

| $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $-1.5$ | $-2.5$ | $3.75$ | $2.25$ |
| $-0.5$ | $-0.5$ | $0.25$ | $0.25$ |
| $0.5$ | $0.5$ | $0.25$ | $0.25$ |
| $1.5$ | $2.5$ | $3.75$ | $2.25$ |
| **Sum** | — | **8.0** | **5.0** |

**Step 3: Compute Slope $a$** [1 mark]

$$
a = \frac{8.0}{5.0} = 1.6
$$

**Step 4: Compute Intercept $b$** [1 mark]

$$
b = \bar{y} - a \bar{x} = 3.5 - 1.6 \times 1.5 = 3.5 - 2.4 = 1.1
$$

**Step 5: Final Equation and Verification** [2 marks]

$$
\boxed{y = 1.6 x + 1.1}
$$

**Verification:**
- $x=0$: predicted $y = 1.1$, actual $= 1.0$, residual $= -0.1$
- $x=1$: predicted $y = 2.7$, actual $= 3.0$, residual $= 0.3$
- $x=2$: predicted $y = 4.3$, actual $= 4.0$, residual $= -0.3$
- $x=3$: predicted $y = 5.9$, actual $= 6.0$, residual $= 0.1$

**Total squared error** $= 0.01 + 0.09 + 0.09 + 0.01 = 0.20$.

**Final comment:** The OLS line $y = 1.6x + 1.1$ minimizes the total squared error to $0.20$. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention for $\rho$** — Always state $\rho \geq 0$ and $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ in Hough line problems. (Lose up to 1 mark.)
> 2. **Mixing up OLS and TLS** — OLS minimizes vertical residuals; TLS minimizes perpendicular distance. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. (Lose up to 2 marks.)
> 3. **Skipping the derivation of the RANSAC formula** — You must show $\ln(1-p) / \ln(1-w^n)$ step-by-step. Writing the final formula without derivation earns only partial credit. (Lose up to 3 marks.)
> 4. **Not labeling units** in numerical answers. (Lose 0.5 mark per occurrence.)
> 5. **Confusing edge detection with curve detection** — Edges are pixels; curves are *connected chains* or *fitted analytic models*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **curve** is a 1D geometric feature formed by linking edge pixels; it can be **parametric** $(x(t), y(t))$ or **implicit** $f(x,y) = 0$.
- ✅ The **Hough Transform** is a *voting-based* algorithm that maps image-space points to sinusoidal curves in $(\rho, \theta)$ space and detects curves via **accumulator peak finding**.
- ✅ **Hough line equation:** $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$ with $\theta \in [0, \pi)$.
- ✅ **Hough circle** uses a 3D accumulator $A[a, b, r]$ and is computationally heavier.
- ✅ **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** minimizes *vertical* residuals: $L = \sum(y_i - ax_i - b)^2$.
- ✅ **Closed-form OLS slope:** $a = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$.
- ✅ **RANSAC** is robust to outliers; iteration count: $k = \frac{\ln(1-p)}{\ln(1-w^n)}$.
- ✅ **Curvature** of a parametric curve: $\kappa = \frac{\vert x'y'' - y'x'' \vert}{(x'^2+y'^2)^{3/2}}$.
- ✅ **RANSAC** is preferred over OLS when the outlier ratio $1 - w$ exceeds ~25%.
- ✅ **Edge Linking** is a *bottom-up* alternative; **Hough Transform** is *top-down* (model-driven).
- ✅ **Snakes/Active Contours** (mention only) are energy-minimizing curves that deform to object boundaries — covered in higher modules.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** (e.g., $\rho \geq 0$, integer accumulator quantization) in KTU answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surfaces
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surfaces

# Surfaces in Computer Vision

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Surface** in computer vision is a 2-dimensional manifold embedded in 3-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^3$. Mathematically, it is a continuous mapping from a 2D parameter domain to 3D space, representing the boundary of a 3D object or the visible geometry captured by optical sensors. Surfaces encode depth, shape, orientation, and reflectance properties — serving as the foundational geometric primitive for 3D reconstruction, object recognition, and scene understanding.

### Surface as a Mathematical Object
A surface $\mathcal{S}$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$ can be described by three principal representations:

1. **Explicit Form:** $z = f(x, y)$ — direct height function
2. **Implicit Form:** $F(x, y, z) = 0$ — level-set description
3. **Parametric Form:** $\mathbf{S}(u, v) = (x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v))$ — vector-valued mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PBCMT504 Module 1, the focus on "Surfaces" centers on **surface representation schemes**, **surface normals computation**, **depth map interpretation**, and **surface reconstruction principles** from visual data (images / point clouds).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Clay Sculpture**
> Imagine you are looking at a clay sculpture under a single light source. The **shape of the sculpture** is the *surface*. The **brightness of every patch on it** depends on how that patch is *tilted* relative to the light — patches tilted toward the light look bright, those tilted away look dark. This tilt is what we call the **surface normal**.
>
> - The **silhouette** of the sculpture from your view is a 2D projection.
> - The **3D surface** is the actual geometry that produced that projection.
> - **Computer vision's job** with surfaces is to *reverse-engineer* the 3D clay shape from photographs, light interactions, and depth measurements.
>
> This is why understanding surfaces is foundational: every 3D perception algorithm — from face recognition to autonomous driving — is ultimately reasoning about surfaces.

### Why Surfaces Matter in Computer Vision

| Reason | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **3D Reconstruction** | Build digital twins, CAD models, AR/VR assets |
| **Shape Analysis** | Object recognition, medical imaging (tumor morphology) |
| **Photometric Reasoning** | Relate pixel intensity to geometry via reflectance models |
| **Motion & Tracking** | Surface flow constrains optical flow equations |
| **Rendering & Simulation** | Graphics engines require explicit surface meshes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Surface Representation Schemes — Deep Dive

#### A. Explicit Surface Representation
$$z = f(x, y), \quad (x, y) \in D \subset \mathbb{R}^2$$

- **Pros:** Simple, intuitive, directly compatible with depth maps and height fields.
- **Cons:** Cannot represent vertical or overhanging surfaces (multi-valued $z$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Depth maps** acquired from RGB-D sensors (e.g., Kinect, LiDAR) are explicit surface representations stored as 2D grids $D(i,j)$.

#### B. Implicit Surface Representation
$$F(x, y, z) = 0$$

The surface is the **zero-level set** of a scalar field. Examples:
- Sphere: $F = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - r^2 = 0$
- Plane: $F = ax + by + cz + d = 0$

**Key advantage:** Topological flexibility — can represent arbitrary closed surfaces, holes, and disconnected components.

#### C. Parametric Surface Representation
$$\mathbf{S}(u, v) = \begin{bmatrix} x(u, v) \\ y(u, v) \\ z(u, v) \end{bmatrix}, \quad (u, v) \in \Omega$$

The parameters $(u,v)$ lie in a 2D domain $\Omega$ (often a unit square or disk). Famous parametric forms:

- **Bilinear patch:** $\mathbf{S}(u,v) = (1-u)(1-v)\mathbf{P}_{00} + u(1-v)\mathbf{P}_{10} + (1-u)v\mathbf{P}_{01} + uv\mathbf{P}_{11}$
- **Bézier surface:** Weighted by Bernstein polynomials
- **B-spline surface:** Piecewise polynomial with local control

### 2.2 Surface Normals — The Heart of Surface Geometry

The **surface normal** $\mathbf{n}$ at a point $\mathbf{p}$ on $\mathcal{S}$ is the unit vector perpendicular to the tangent plane at $\mathbf{p}$.

#### Computing Normals from Explicit Form
For $z = f(x,y)$:
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2}} \begin{bmatrix} -f_x \\ -f_y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $f_x = \partial f / \partial x$ and $f_y = \partial f / \partial y$.

#### Computing Normals from Implicit Form
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\nabla F}{\vert \nabla F \vert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}} \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Computing Normals from Parametric Form
Using partial derivatives $\mathbf{S}_u$ and $\mathbf{S}_v$:
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v}{\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cross product order matters!** $\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v$ gives an outward-pointing normal when the parametrization is right-handed. Swapping the order flips the normal direction.

#### Computing Normals from Point Clouds (Discretized Case)
Given three nearby points $\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2, \mathbf{p}_3$ in a point cloud:
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{(\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1) \times (\mathbf{p}_3 - \mathbf{p}_1)}{\vert (\mathbf{p}_2 - \mathbf{p}_1) \times (\mathbf{p}_3 - \mathbf{p}_1) \vert}$$

A more robust method fits a local plane to the $k$-nearest neighbors using **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — the normal is the eigenvector corresponding to the **smallest eigenvalue** of the local covariance matrix.

### 2.3 Surface Curvature

Curvature quantifies how rapidly the surface bends at a point.

- **Normal Curvature $\kappa_n$:** Rate of change of the normal in a given tangent direction.
- **Principal Curvatures $\kappa_1, \kappa_2$:** Max and min normal curvatures (eigenvalues of the shape operator).
- **Gaussian Curvature:** $K = \kappa_1 \cdot \kappa_2$
- **Mean Curvature:** $H = \frac{\kappa_1 + \kappa_2}{2}$

### 2.4 Surface Reconstruction Pipeline (Conceptual Stages)

1. **Acquisition** — Capture depth/point data (stereo, ToF, LiDAR, structured light).
2. **Preprocessing** — Denoise, remove outliers, downsample.
3. **Registration** — Align multiple scans (ICP algorithm).
4. **Surface Fitting** — Triangulation (Delaunay) or implicit fitting (Poisson Surface Reconstruction).
5. **Post-processing** — Smoothing, hole filling, decimation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Explicit surface | $z = f(x,y)$ | Depth maps, terrain modeling |
| 2 | Implicit surface | $F(x,y,z) = 0$ | Level sets, SDFs |
| 3 | Parametric surface | $\mathbf{S}(u,v) \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | Bézier, B-spline patches |
| 4 | Normal from explicit | $\mathbf{n} = \frac{(-f_x, -f_y, 1)}{\sqrt{1+f_x^2+f_y^2}}$ | Height field analysis |
| 5 | Normal from implicit | $\mathbf{n} = \frac{\nabla F}{\vert \nabla F \vert}$ | Level-set methods |
| 6 | Normal from parametric | $\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v}{\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert}$ | Mesh shading |
| 7 | Tangent vectors | $\mathbf{S}_u = \partial \mathbf{S}/\partial u$, $\mathbf{S}_v = \partial \mathbf{S}/\partial v$ | Local frame construction |
| 8 | First fundamental form | $E = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_u$, $F = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_v$, $G = \mathbf{S}_v \cdot \mathbf{S}_v$ | Area, angles on surface |
| 9 | Surface area element | $dA = \sqrt{EG - F^2}\, du\, dv$ | Integration on surfaces |
| 10 | Gaussian curvature | $K = \kappa_1 \kappa_2$ | Shape classification |
| 11 | Mean curvature | $H = (\kappa_1 + \kappa_2)/2$ | Smoothing operators |
| 12 | Lambertian reflectance | $I = \rho (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L})$ | Photometric stereo |
| 13 | Plane equation | $ax+by+cz+d=0$ | Plane fitting |
| 14 | Plane normal | $\mathbf{n} = (a,b,c)/\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}$ | Geometric reasoning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Surface normals extracted from RGB-D data power **photometric stereo** in industrial inspection, **neural radiance fields (NeRF)** in novel-view synthesis, and **iterative closest point (ICP)** alignment in 3D scanning workflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Surface Normal from Explicit Form $z = f(x,y)$

**Step 1: Convert to implicit form.**
Rewriting $z = f(x,y)$ as $F(x,y,z) = f(x,y) - z = 0$.

**Step 2: Compute the gradient.**
The gradient of $F$ is:
$$\nabla F = \left( \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial F}{\partial y}, \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} \right)$$

Computing each component:
- $\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = f_x$
- $\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = f_y$
- $\frac{\partial F}{\partial z} = -1$

**Step 3: Assemble the unnormalized normal.**
$$\nabla F = \begin{bmatrix} f_x \\ f_y \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Normalize to unit length.**
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_x^2 + f_y^2 + 1}} \begin{bmatrix} f_x \\ f_y \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

By convention, we flip the sign so the normal points toward the viewer (positive $z$):
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2}} \begin{bmatrix} -f_x \\ -f_y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Verification with a flat plane.**
For $f(x,y) = 0$ (a flat plane $z=0$): $f_x = 0$, $f_y = 0$.
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+0+0}}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the correct upward-pointing normal for the $xy$-plane. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Surface Area of a Parametric Patch

Given a parametric surface $\mathbf{S}(u,v)$ defined on a region $\Omega$, the area is:
$$A = \iint_{\Omega} \vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert \, du\, dv$$

**Step 1: Construct the parallelogram spanned by tangent vectors.**
At each point, $\mathbf{S}_u$ and $\mathbf{S}_v$ are infinitesimal vectors tangent to the surface. They span an infinitesimal parallelogram.

**Step 2: Compute its area.**
The area of a parallelogram spanned by two 3D vectors is the magnitude of their cross product:
$$dA = \vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert \, du\, dv$$

**Step 3: Introduce the first fundamental form.**
Expanding $\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert^2$ using the vector identity $\vert \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} \vert^2 = \vert \mathbf{a} \vert^2 \vert \mathbf{b} \vert^2 - (\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b})^2$:

Let $E = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_u$, $F = \mathbf{S}_u \cdot \mathbf{S}_v$, $G = \mathbf{S}_v \cdot \mathbf{S}_v$.

$$\vert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \vert^2 = EG - F^2$$

**Step 4: Final form.**
$$A = \iint_{\Omega} \sqrt{EG - F^2} \, du\, dv$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Normal of a Sphere

**Problem:** Compute the outward unit normal of the unit sphere $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$ at the point $\mathbf{p} = (1, 0, 0)$.

**Step 1: Identify the implicit form.**
$F(x,y,z) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - 1 = 0$

**Step 2: Compute the gradient.**
$$\nabla F = \begin{bmatrix} 2x \\ 2y \\ 2z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate at $\mathbf{p} = (1,0,0)$.**
$$\nabla F \big|_{\mathbf{p}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Normalize.**
$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 + 0 + 0}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The outward normal at $(1,0,0)$ points along the $+x$-axis. This matches the radial direction $\mathbf{p} / \vert \mathbf{p} \vert$, confirming the result. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Surface Forms

| Surface | Parametric Form $\mathbf{S}(u,v)$ | Tangent $\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v$ (unnormalized normal) | Area Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plane $z=0$ | $(u, v, 0)$ | $(0, 0, 1)$ | $du\, dv$ |
| Cylinder $x^2+y^2=r^2$ | $(r\cos u, r\sin u, v)$ | $(r\cos u, r\sin u, 0)$ | $r\, du\, dv$ |
| Sphere of radius $R$ | $(R\sin u\cos v, R\sin u\sin v, R\cos u)$ | $(R^2 \sin^2 u \cos v, R^2 \sin^2 u \sin v, R^2 \sin u \cos u)$ | $R^2 \sin u\, du\, dv$ |
| Torus (tube radius $r$, ring $R$) | $((R+r\cos u)\cos v, (R+r\cos u)\sin v, r\sin u)$ | $(r(R+r\cos u)\cos u\cos v, r(R+r\cos u)\cos u\sin v, r(R+r\cos u)\sin u)$ | $r(R+r\cos u)\, du\, dv$ |
| Helicoid | $(u\cos v, u\sin v, cv)$ | $(c\cos v, c\sin v, u)$ via cross product | $\sqrt{u^2 + c^2}\, du\, dv$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The sphere parametrization uses $u$ as polar angle (colatitude) and $v$ as azimuth, so $u \in [0, \pi]$ and $v \in [0, 2\pi]$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Surface Representation Taxonomy (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SURFACE REPRESENTATION"]:::root --> B["EXPLICIT FORM"]
    A --> C["IMPLICIT FORM"]
    A --> D["PARAMETRIC FORM"]
    A --> E["DIGITAL / DISCRETE"]
    B --> B1["Height Field z = f x y"]
```
