## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Euclidean distance between two points in 2D image space. State its significance in feature matching.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Euclidean distance between two image points $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ in a 2D image is defined as:

$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

**Significance in Feature Matching (3 marks distribution):**
1. (1 mark) **Definition** with the correct formula.
2. (1 mark) It measures the **straight-line (as-the-crow-flies) distance**, unlike Manhattan or Chebyshev distances.
3. (1 mark) In **feature matching**, SIFT/SURF/ORB descriptors are compared using nearest-neighbor search with Euclidean distance (or SSD). A smaller $d$ indicates higher feature similarity, enabling correspondence between keypoints in different images.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the dot product of two vectors? Explain its role in determining the angle between two edges in an image.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **dot product** (or inner product) of two vectors $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$ and $\vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is defined as:

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i = \|\vec{a}\| \, \|\vec{b}\| \cos\theta$$

**Role in Edge Angle Determination (3 marks distribution):**
1. (1 mark) **Definition** with the formula.
2. (1 mark) The **angle** between two edge direction vectors is computed as:
$$\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\|\vec{a}\| \, \|\vec{b}\|}\right)$$
3. (1 mark) If $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$, the edges are **perpendicular**; if $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} > 0$, the angle is acute. This is used in **Hough Transform** for shape detection and in **corner detection** algorithms (Harris, Shi-Tomasi).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive Problem on Image Feature Geometry**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Two image keypoints are detected at $K_1 = (120, 85)$ and $K_2 = (200, 160)$ in a 2D image. Compute:
  (i) The Euclidean distance between them.
  (ii) The unit vector pointing from $K_1$ to $K_2$.
  (iii) The midpoint of the line segment $K_1 K_2$.
  (iv) A unit normal vector to the line passing through $K_1$ and $K_2$.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two edge direction vectors are extracted from an image: $\vec{u} = (4, 3)$ and $\vec{v} = (-3, 4)$.
  (i) Compute the dot product $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}$.
  (ii) Find the angle between the two edges in degrees.
  (iii) Determine if the two edges are perpendicular. Justify.
  (iv) Compute the cross product $\vec{u} \times \vec{v}$ (extended to 3D with $z=0$).
   **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Sub-parts (i) to (iv):**

**(i) Euclidean Distance (1.5 marks):**
$$d(K_1, K_2) = \sqrt{(200 - 120)^2 + (160 - 85)^2} = \sqrt{(80)^2 + (75)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{6400 + 5625} = \sqrt{12025} = \sqrt{12025}$$

Computing the square root precisely: $12025 = 25 \times 481$, so $\sqrt{12025} = 5\sqrt{481}$.

Numerically: $\sqrt{481} \approx 21.93$, so $d \approx 5 \times 21.93 = 109.66$ pixels.

**Answer:** $d(K_1, K_2) = 5\sqrt{481} \approx 109.66$ pixels.

**(ii) Unit Vector from $K_1$ to $K_2$ (2 marks):**
The vector from $K_1$ to $K_2$ is:
$$\vec{w} = K_2 - K_1 = (200 - 120, \, 160 - 85) = (80, 75)$$

The magnitude is the same as the distance: $\|\vec{w}\| = 5\sqrt{481} \approx 109.66$.

The unit vector:
$$\hat{w} = \frac{\vec{w}}{\|\vec{w}\|} = \left(\frac{80}{5\sqrt{481}}, \, \frac{75}{5\sqrt{481}}\right) = \left(\frac{16}{\sqrt{481}}, \, \frac{15}{\sqrt{481}}\right)$$

Numerically: $\hat{w} \approx (0.7296, \, 0.6839)$.

**Answer:** $\hat{w} \approx (0.730, \, 0.684)$.

**(iii) Midpoint (1.5 marks):**
$$M = \left(\frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \, \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2}\right) = \left(\frac{120 + 200}{2}, \, \frac{85 + 160}{2}\right) = (160, 122.5)$$

**Answer:** $M = (160, 122.5)$.

**(iv) Unit Normal Vector to Line $K_1 K_2$ (2 marks):**
The line's direction vector is $\vec{w} = (80, 75)$. A normal (perpendicular) vector is obtained by rotating 90°: $\vec{n} = (-75, 80)$ or $(75, -80)$.

Magnitude of $\vec{n}$:
$$\|\vec{n}\| = \sqrt{(-75)^2 + 80^2} = \sqrt{5625 + 6400} = \sqrt{12025} = 5\sqrt{481}$$

Unit normal:
$$\hat{n} = \left(\frac{-75}{5\sqrt{481}}, \, \frac{80}{5\sqrt{481}}\right) = \left(\frac{-15}{\sqrt{481}}, \, \frac{16}{\sqrt{481}}\right) \approx (-0.6839, \, 0.7296)$$

**Answer:** $\hat{n} \approx (-0.684, \, 0.730)$.

---

##### **Part (b) — Sub-parts (i) to (iv):**

**(i) Dot Product (1.5 marks):**
$$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = (4)(-3) + (3)(4) = -12 + 12 = 0$$

**Answer:** $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$.

**(ii) Angle Between Edges (2 marks):**
$$\|\vec{u}\| = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$
$$\|\vec{v}\| = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$
$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\|\vec{u}\| \, \|\vec{v}\|} = \frac{0}{5 \times 5} = 0$$
$$\theta = \arccos(0) = 90°$$

**Answer:** $\theta = 90°$.

**(iii) Perpendicularity Check (1.5 marks):**
**Yes**, the two edges are **perpendicular**. Justification: The dot product $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ implies the angle between them is exactly 90°. Equivalently, $\vec{u} = (4, 3)$ and $\vec{v} = (-3, 4)$ are scalar-rotations of each other: $\vec{v}$ is $\vec{u}$ rotated by 90° counter-clockwise.

**(iv) Cross Product in 3D (2 marks):**
Extend $\vec{u} = (4, 3, 0)$ and $\vec{v} = (-3, 4, 0)$.

$$\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 3 & 0 \\ -3 & 4 & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:
$$= \hat{i}(3 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 4) - \hat{j}(4 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot (-3)) + \hat{k}(4 \cdot 4 - 3 \cdot (-3))$$
$$= \hat{i}(0) - \hat{j}(0) + \hat{k}(16 + 9) = 0\hat{i} - 0\hat{j} + 25\hat{k} = (0, 0, 25)$$

**Answer:** $\vec{u} \times \vec{v} = (0, 0, 25)$.

This is a vector of magnitude 25 pointing along the $+z$ axis (out of the image plane), which makes sense because $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$ lie in the $xy$-plane.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2): Problem on Line Fitting and Point-to-Line Distance**

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** A line in an image is detected with equation $L: 2x - 3y + 6 = 0$.
  (i) Find two points on the line by setting $x = 0$ and $y = 0$.
  (ii) Determine the direction vector and the normal vector of the line.
  (iii) Compute the perpendicular distance from the point $P_0 = (5, 7)$ to this line using the standard formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hough Transform accumulator finds three collinear points: $A = (1, 2)$, $B = (3, 4)$, $C = (7, 8)$.
  (i) Verify if they are collinear using the cross product method.
  (ii) If collinear, find the equation of the line passing through them in the form $ax + by + c = 0$.
  (iii) Compute the Euclidean distance from the origin $(0, 0)$ to this line.
  (iv) If a 4th point $D = (5, 5)$ is added, find the shortest distance from $D$ to the line.
   **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Sub-parts (i) to (iii):**

**(i) Two Points on $L: 2x - 3y + 6 = 0$ (1.5 marks):**
- Setting $x = 0$: $-3y + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 2$. So $P_1 = (0, 2)$.
- Setting $y = 0$: $2x + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -3$. So $P_2 = (-3, 0)$.

**Answer:** $P_1 = (0, 2)$, $P_2 = (-3, 0)$.

**(ii) Direction and Normal Vectors (2.5 marks):**
The general form $ax + by + c = 0$ has coefficients $a = 2$, $b = -3$, $c = 6$.

**Normal vector** (perpendicular to the line):
$$\vec{n} = (a, b) = (2, -3)$$

**Direction vector** (parallel to the line) — perpendicular to $\vec{n}$:
$$\vec{d} = (b, -a) = (-3, -2) \text{ or equivalently } (3, 2)$$

Verification: $\vec{n} \cdot \vec{d} = (2)(3) + (-3)(2) = 6 - 6 = 0$. ✓ (Perpendicular)

**Answer:** $\vec{n} = (2, -3)$, $\vec{d} = (3, 2)$.

**(iii) Distance from $P_0 = (5, 7)$ to Line $L$ (3 marks):**
Using the standard formula:
$$d = \frac{|ax_0 + by_0 + c|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} = \frac{|2(5) + (-3)(7) + 6|}{\sqrt{2^2 + (-3)^2}}$$

Numerator:
$$|2(5) - 3(7) + 6| = |10 - 21 + 6| = |-5| = 5$$

Denominator:
$$\sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

$$d = \frac{5}{\sqrt{13}} = \frac{5\sqrt{13}}{13} \approx 1.387 \text{ units}$$

**Answer:** $d = \frac{5}{\sqrt{13}} \approx 1.387$ pixels.

---

##### **Part (b) — Sub-parts (i) to (iv):**

**(i) Collinearity Check via Cross Product (2 marks):**
For three points to be collinear, the vectors $\vec{AB}$ and $\vec{AC}$ must be parallel, i.e., their cross product must be zero.

$$\vec{AB} = B - A = (3-1, \, 4-2) = (2, 2)$$
$$\vec{AC} = C - A = (7-1, \, 8-2) = (6, 6)$$

Notice that $\vec{AC} = 3 \vec{AB}$, so the points are **collinear**.

Cross product verification (in 3D with $z=0$):
$$\vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 6 & 6 & 0 \end{vmatrix} = \hat{k}(2 \cdot 6 - 2 \cdot 6) = 0\hat{k} = \vec{0}$$

**Answer:** Collinear (cross product is $\vec{0}$). ✓

**(ii) Equation of the Line (2 marks):**
Using two points $A = (1, 2)$ and $B = (3, 4)$:

The slope is:
$$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{4 - 2}{3 - 1} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

Equation: $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$
$$y - 2 = 1 \cdot (x - 1) \Rightarrow y = x + 1 \Rightarrow x - y + 1 = 0$$

So: $a = 1$, $b = -1$, $c = 1$.

**Answer:** $L: x - y + 1 = 0$.

**(iii) Distance from Origin to Line (1.5 marks):**
$$d = \frac{|a(0) + b(0) + c|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} = \frac{|0 + 0 + 1|}{\sqrt{1 + 1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707$$

**Answer:** $d = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ units.

**(iv) Distance from $D = (5, 5)$ to Line (1.5 marks):**
$$d = \frac{|a(5) + b(5) + c|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} = \frac{|5 - 5 + 1|}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{|1|}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707$$

**Answer:** $d = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ units.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** in distance formulas. The point-to-line distance is $d = \frac{|ax_0 + by_0 + c|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ — without the absolute value, you get a *signed* distance, which is wrong unless specifically asked.
>
> 2. **Confusing dot product and cross product formulas.** Dot product gives a *scalar*; cross product gives a *vector*. Examiners deduct 2-3 marks for this confusion.
>
> 3. **Not normalizing vectors** before using them in similarity computations. For cosine similarity, both vectors must have unit norm for the result to be in $[-1, 1]$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always state "pixels" for image distances, "meters" for 3D world distances.
>
> 5. **In rotation problems, using degrees in the matrix when $\theta$ is in radians** (or vice versa). Always convert to radians first: $\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \times \pi / 180$.
>
> 6. **Writing the line equation in inconsistent form.** Standard form is $ax + by + c = 0$. Don't mix it with $y = mx + c$ without converting.
>
> 7. **Sign errors in the cross product determinant.** The expansion order is: $\hat{i}(a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2) - \hat{j}(a_1 b_3 - a_3 b_1) + \hat{k}(a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1)$. The middle term has a **minus sign** — many students miss this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Euclidean Distance (2D/3D):** $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ and $d = \sqrt{\Delta X^2 + \Delta Y^2 + \Delta Z^2}$
- [ ] **Vector Magnitude (L2 norm):** $\lVert \vec{v} \rVert = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \cdots + v_n^2}$
- [ ] **Unit Vector:** $\hat{v} = \vec{v} / \lVert \vec{v} \rVert$ (used in edge orientation, surface normals)
- [ ] **Dot Product:** $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \sum a_i b_i = \lVert \vec{a} \rVert \lVert \vec{b} \rVert \cos\theta$
- [ ] **Cross Product (3D only):** Determinant formula; gives a vector perpendicular to both inputs
- [ ] **Cosine Similarity:** $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\lVert \vec{a} \rVert \lVert \vec{b} \rVert}$ — range $[-1, 1]$, used in feature matching
- [ ] **Line Equation:** $ax + by + c = 0$ with normal $\vec{n} = (a, b)$ and direction $\vec{d} = (-b, a)$
- [ ] **Point-to-Line Distance:** $d = \frac{\vert ax_0 + by_0 + c \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}$ (always non-negative)
- [ ] **Plane Equation (3D):** $Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$
- [ ] **Midpoint:** $M = \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}, \frac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right)$
- [ ] **2D Rotation Matrix:** $\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$
- [ ] **Rotation Matrix Property:** $\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$
- [ ] **Euclidean Transform:** $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{t}$ — preserves distances and angles
- [ ] **Perpendicularity Test:** Vectors are perpendicular iff $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$
- [ ] **Parallelism Test (3D):** Vectors are parallel iff $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{0}$
- [ ] **Collinearity Test:** Three points are collinear iff $\vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} = \vec{0}$
- [ ] **Triangle Area:** $A = \frac{1}{2} \lVert \vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} \rVert$

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU 2024:**
> 1. Always draw a **rough sketch** of points/lines before solving — it catches 50% of sign errors.
> 2. For 14-mark questions, **show all intermediate steps**. KTU examiners award **step marks** liberally.
> 3. When asked "explain the role in CV," always mention a **specific CV application** (edge detection, feature matching, camera calibration) — generic answers lose marks.
> 4. **Unit consistency** is critical: state pixels for 2D, meters for 3D world coordinates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Camera Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometric Camera Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Geometric Camera Parameters** are the set of mathematical descriptors that completely define how a 3-dimensional real-world scene is projected onto a 2-dimensional image plane by an optical imaging system. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PBCMT504)**, these parameters are partitioned into two principal categories:

- **Intrinsic Parameters (Internal):** Properties of the camera itself, independent of its position or orientation in the world. These include focal length $(f, f_x, f_y)$, principal point coordinates $(c_x, c_y)$, pixel skew $(\gamma)$, and lens distortion coefficients $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$.
- **Extrinsic Parameters (External):** Properties describing the rigid transformation (rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$) from a chosen **world coordinate system** to the **camera coordinate system**.

Collectively, these parameters populate the **Camera Projection Matrix** $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$, which mathematically maps a 3D world point to a 2D pixel coordinate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The geometric camera model is the foundational building block for all computer vision pipelines. Mastering intrinsic vs. extrinsic parameters is **mandatory** before moving to stereo vision, structure-from-motion, or SLAM.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are taking a photograph of a friend standing in a room. The photo is flat, but your friend is 3D. Three things determine exactly *where* and *how big* your friend appears in the image:

1. **The Camera Itself (Intrinsics):** Think of this as the "personality" of your specific camera. How strong is its lens (focal length)? Is the lens perfectly centered over the sensor, or is it slightly off to the left (principal point)? Does the lens bend light in weird ways at the edges (distortion)? A high-end DSLR and a cheap pinhole toy camera have different "personalities" — even when placed in the exact same spot, they will produce different images.

2. **Where You Stand and How You Point the Camera (Extrinsics):** This is the camera's "pose" in the room. Are you standing 2 meters or 5 meters from your friend? Are you looking straight at them or from an angle? Rotating and shifting the camera changes the image dramatically, even if the camera itself didn't change.

3. **The Film/Sensor Behind the Lens:** This captures the projection. The relationship between the "size" of objects on this sensor and their actual projected image is governed by the intrinsics.

So in plain English: **Intrinsics = "What kind of camera is this?"** and **Extrinsics = "Where is this camera and which way is it pointing?"** Together, they tell us the complete geometric story of how 3D reality became a 2D photograph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction to Memorize:**
> - Intrinsic parameters $\mathbf{K}$ → do **not** change when the camera moves.
> - Extrinsic parameters $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ → change every time the camera moves.
> - Together: $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K} \cdot [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ (a $3 \times 4$ matrix).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pinhole Camera Model — Conceptual Breakdown

The simplest geometric model of a camera is the **Pinhole Model**, which assumes light passes through an infinitesimally small aperture and inverts the image on a projection plane. Real cameras approximate this with lenses, but the mathematical framework is identical for a first-order analysis.

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **World Frame Definition:** Establish an arbitrary world coordinate system $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^\top$ at a fixed reference point in the scene.
2. **Camera Frame Definition:** Place a 3D coordinate system $\mathbf{X}_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)^\top$ at the optical center (pinhole) of the camera, with the $Z_c$-axis pointing along the optical axis.
3. **Rigid Body Transformation (Extrinsic):** Convert world coordinates to camera coordinates using a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ and translation vector $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$:
   $$\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t}$$
4. **Perspective Projection:** Project the 3D camera-frame point onto the 2D image plane (at distance $f$, the focal length) using similar triangles:
   $$x' = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y' = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$
5. **Affine Sensor Mapping (Intrinsic):** Convert the projected image-plane coordinates $(x', y')$ to discrete pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ using pixel size $(\alpha, \beta)$ and principal point offset $(u_0, v_0)$.

## 2.2 Detailed Treatment of Each Parameter

### 2.2.1 Focal Length $(f, f_x, f_y)$
- Defined as the perpendicular distance from the **optical center** (pinhole) to the **image plane**.
- In physical units (mm): $f$.
- In pixel units: $f_x = f / s_x$ and $f_y = f / s_y$, where $s_x, s_y$ are the effective pixel pitch in mm/pixel along the $x$ and $y$ axes.

### 2.2.2 Principal Point $(c_x, c_y)$
- The pixel coordinate where the **optical axis** intersects the image plane.
- In an ideal sensor perfectly aligned with the lens, this is the image center.
- In practice, manufacturing tolerances shift it, so it must be calibrated.

### 2.2.3 Skew Coefficient $(\gamma)$
- Represents the **non-orthogonality** between the $u$ and $v$ pixel axes on the sensor.
- In modern CMOS sensors, $\gamma \approx 0$, but it is retained in the general model for completeness.

### 2.2.4 Rotation Matrix $\mathbf{R}$ (3 DOF)
- An orthogonal matrix ($\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$, $\det \mathbf{R} = 1$) encoding **pitch, yaw, and roll** of the camera relative to the world frame.
- Parameterized by 3 angles (Euler angles) or by a unit quaternion.

### 2.2.5 Translation Vector $\mathbf{t}$ (3 DOF)
- The 3D position of the camera's optical center expressed in the world coordinate frame.
- Encodes the **3 translational DOF** (X, Y, Z offset).

### 2.2.6 Lens Distortion Coefficients
Real lenses deviate from the pinhole model. The two dominant distortions are:

- **Radial Distortion:** Light rays bend more at the lens edges than the center.
  $$x_{\text{corrected}} = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$
  $$y_{\text{corrected}} = y(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$
  where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.

- **Tangential Distortion:** Caused by the lens not being perfectly parallel to the sensor plane.
  $$x_{\text{corrected}} = x + 2 p_1 x y + p_2(r^2 + 2 x^2)$$
  $$y_{\text{corrected}} = y + p_1(r^2 + 2 y^2) + 2 p_2 x y$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula you must memorize** for KTU board examination questions on Geometric Camera Parameters. Tables use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ in math to preserve formatting.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Remarks |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | World → Camera (Extrinsic) | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t}$ | $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$, $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$ |
| 2 | In Homogeneous Form | $\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^\top & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $4 \times 4$ transformation |
| 3 | Perspective Projection (no intrinsics) | $x' = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}$, $y' = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$ | $f$ in mm; $x', y'$ in mm |
| 4 | Pixel Coordinates (full intrinsic model) | $u = \alpha \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + \gamma \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_x$, $v = \beta \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$ | $(u, v)$ in pixels |
| 5 | Intrinsic Matrix $\mathbf{K}$ | $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Upper-triangular $3 \times 3$ |
| 6 | Full Projection Matrix | $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ | $3 \times 4$ matrix |
| 7 | Full Mapping (World → Pixel) | $\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\lambda = Z_c$ is the depth |
| 8 | Radial Distortion | $x_d = x_u (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$ | $r^2 = x_u^2 + y_u^2$ |
| 9 | Tangential Distortion | $x_d = x_u + 2 p_1 x_u y_u + p_2(r^2 + 2 x_u^2)$ | $(x_u, y_u)$ undistorted |
| 10 | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | Intrinsics: 5–8; Extrinsics: 6 | Total: 11–14 DOF |
| 11 | Orthogonality of $\mathbf{R}$ | $\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ | $\det \mathbf{R} = +1$ |
| 12 | Inverse Depth $\lambda$ | $\lambda = Z_c$ | Scaling factor in homogeneous |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding geometric camera parameters is **not** an academic exercise — it is the cornerstone of:

- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving cars (e.g., Tesla, Waymo) must know the intrinsic and extrinsic parameters of every onboard camera to perform accurate 3D scene reconstruction, obstacle avoidance, and lane detection.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Applications like ARCore (Google) and ARKit (Apple) perform real-time camera calibration to virtually place 3D objects onto flat surfaces.
- **Medical Imaging:** Endoscopes and microscopes are calibrated to measure the size of tumors or cells with micrometer precision.
- **Photogrammetry & 3D Scanning:** Cultural heritage preservation uses calibrated multi-camera rigs to create digital twins of monuments.
- **Structure from Motion (SfM) and SLAM:** Algorithms like ORB-SLAM, COLMAP, and OpenCV's `calibrateCamera` use these parameters as the foundation for reconstructing 3D scenes from 2D image sequences.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From World Coordinates to Pixel Coordinates

We derive the complete mapping $\mathbf{X}_w \in \mathbb{R}^3 \rightarrow (u, v) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ step by step. This is a **guaranteed KTU exam derivation question**.

### Step 1 — Define the World Point

A 3D point in the real world is represented as a column vector:

$$\mathbf{X}_w = \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic:** We choose an arbitrary origin in the scene (e.g., a corner of a calibration checkerboard) and measure all other points relative to it.

### Step 2 — Convert to Homogeneous World Coordinates

To enable affine transformations using matrix multiplication, we augment the point with a 1:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_w = \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic:** Inhomogeneous 3D points cannot be linearly transformed into 2D; the homogeneous trick allows a $3 \times 4$ matrix to act on a 4-vector.

### Step 3 — Apply the Extrinsic Transformation

The world-to-camera transformation is a rigid body motion (rotation + translation):

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic:** The upper-left $3 \times 3$ block is $\mathbf{R}$ (orientation), and the rightmost $3 \times 1$ column is $\mathbf{t}$ (camera position). The bottom row $[0, 0, 0, 1]$ preserves the homogeneous coordinate.

### Step 4 — Apply the Perspective Projection

Using similar triangles, the 3D point in the camera frame is projected onto the 2D image plane (at distance $f$):

$$x_{\text{plane}} = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_{\text{plane}} = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

**Logic:** If $Z_c$ is large, the point is far and the projection is small. If $Z_c$ is small, the point is near and the projection is large. This is the "foreshortening" effect.

### Step 5 — Convert Physical Units to Pixel Units

The image-plane coordinates are in millimeters. To convert to pixel indices:

$$u = \frac{x_{\text{plane}}}{s_x} + c_x = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = \frac{y_{\text{plane}}}{s_y} + c_y = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

**Logic:** The sensor is divided into discrete pixels. $s_x, s_y$ convert mm to pixels; $c_x, c_y$ shift the origin from the lens axis to the top-left pixel. We have defined $f_x = f / s_x$ and $f_y = f / s_y$.

### Step 6 — Construct the Intrinsic Matrix

The previous step can be written in matrix form as:

$$\begin{aligned} Z_c \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** This is the **Intrinsic Matrix** $\mathbf{K}$. It is upper-triangular (5 DOF: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$, where $\gamma$ is the skew term we omitted for simplicity).

### Step 7 — Compose the Full Projection Equation

Substituting Step 3 into Step 6:

$$\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{P} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_w$$

where $\lambda = Z_c$ is the **scaling factor** (depth) that must be divided out to recover the true homogeneous pixel coordinates.

**Logic:** This is the **fundamental equation of geometric camera projection**. It is the single most important formula in this module. Every KTU question ultimately derives from this.

## 3.2 Derivation: Radial Distortion Correction

Real lenses exhibit non-linear distortions. The most common is **barrel/pincushion radial distortion**, modeled as a polynomial in $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.

### Step 1 — Define Undistorted (Ideal) Image Coordinates

The point as it *should* be in an ideal pinhole camera:

$$x_u, y_u \quad \text{where} \quad r_u^2 = x_u^2 + y_u^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Radial Polynomial

The actual (distorted) point is displaced radially outward (or inward):

$$x_d = x_u (1 + k_1 r_u^2 + k_2 r_u^4 + k_3 r_u^6)$$

$$y_d = y_u (1 + k_1 r_u^2 + k_2 r_u^4 + k_3 r_u^6)$$

**Logic:** $k_1 > 0$ causes **barrel distortion** (image bows outward); $k_1 < 0$ causes **pincushion distortion**. Higher-order terms ($k_2, k_3$) model more severe warping at the periphery of the lens.

### Step 3 — Add Tangential Component

If the lens is not perfectly parallel to the sensor, tangential distortion also appears:

$$x_d = x_u + 2 p_1 x_u y_u + p_2(r_u^2 + 2 x_u^2)$$

$$y_d = y_u + p_1(r_u^2 + 2 y_u^2) + 2 p_2 x_u y_u$$

**Logic:** The cross-terms $2 p_1 x_u y_u$ and $p_2(r_u^2 + 2 x_u^2)$ model the asymmetric skew introduced by lens decentering.

### Step 4 — Map to Pixel Coordinates

After distortion correction, the final pixel coordinates are:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_d + c_x, \quad v = f_y \cdot y_d + c_y$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Parameter Summary

| Parameter Category | Symbol | DOF | Estimated via |
|-------------------|--------|-----|---------------|
| Focal length (x-axis) | $f_x$ | 1 | Calibration |
| Focal length (y-axis) | $f_y$ | 1 | Calibration |
| Principal point | $(c_x, c_y)$ | 2 | Calibration |
| Skew | $\gamma$ | 1 | Calibration (usually $\approx 0$) |
| Radial distortion | $(k_1, k_2, k_3)$ | 3 | Calibration |
| Tangential distortion | $(p_1, p_2)$ | 2 | Calibration |
| **Total Intrinsic** | $\mathbf{K}$ + distortion | **8–10** | Calibrated once per camera |
| Rotation | $\mathbf{R}$ | 3 | Pose estimation |
| Translation | $\mathbf{t}$ | 3 | Pose estimation |
| **Total Extrinsic** | $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ | **6** | Recomputed per frame |

**Total DOF in a complete camera model: 14–16**, which is why camera calibration is a non-trivial optimization problem.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A camera has $f_x = 800$ px, $f_y = 800$ px, $c_x = 320$ px, $c_y = 240$ px, and is positioned such that a world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (0, 0, 10)^\top$ m is at the optical axis (so $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$, $\mathbf{t} = (0, 0, 0)^\top$). Find the pixel coordinates $(u, v)$.

**Step 1:** Since $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the intrinsic matrix:

$$Z_c \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3200 \\ 2400 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $Z_c = 10$:

$$u = 320, \quad v = 240$$

**Interpretation:** A point lying on the optical axis projects to the **principal point** $(c_x, c_y) = (320, 240)$, as expected by definition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pinhole Camera Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of the pinhole camera model, from the real-world 3D scene to the discrete 2D pixel grid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[World Coordinate System Xw Yw Zw] --> B[Extrinsic Transformation R and t]
    B --> C[Camera Coordinate System Xc Yc Zc]
    C --> D[Perspective Projection at Focal Plane f]
    D --> E[Image Plane Coordinates x and y in mm]
    E --> F[Lens Distortion Model k1 k2 p1 p2]
    F --> G[Sensor Sampling fx fy cx cy]
    G --> H[Pixel Coordinate System u and v in pixels]
    H --> I[2D Digital Image Output]
    
    subgraph EXT[Extrinsic Parameters]
        B
    end
    
    subgraph INT[Intrinsic Parameters]
        D
        F
        G
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Follow the arrows from the world point (top-left) to the pixel output (bottom-right). The **Extrinsic block** (rotation + translation) sits at the entry to the camera, while the **Intrinsic block** (focal length + distortion + sensor mapping) governs everything inside the camera.

## 4.2 Camera Calibration Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Capture Checkerboard Images from Multiple Angles] --> K[Detect Corner Points in 2D]
    K --> L[Pair 2D Detections with Known 3D World Points]
    L --> M[Set Up Linear Equations of the Form lambda times uv1 equals K R t Xw]
    M --> N[Initialize Intrinsic Matrix K Using Zhangs Method]
    N --> O[Optimize All Parameters to Minimize Reprojection Error]
    O --> P[Output Final K R t and Distortion Coefficients]
    
    Q[OpenCV calibrateCamera function] -.uses.-> M
    R[MATLAB Camera Calibrator App] -.uses.-> M
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- **J — Image Capture:** The user moves a checkerboard (or ChArUco board) in front of the camera, capturing 10–20 images from diverse viewpoints.
- **K — Corner Detection:** An algorithm (e.g., `findChessboardCorners` in OpenCV) automatically locates the inner grid intersections in pixels.
- **L — 3D–2D Pairing:** Because the checkerboard square size is known, each 2D corner is associated with a known 3D world coordinate.
- **M — Equation Setup:** We stack many such correspondences into a large linear system.
- **N — Closed-Form Initialization:** Zhang's method gives an initial guess for $\mathbf{K}$, $\mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{t}$ without distortion.
- **O — Non-Linear Refinement:** Levenberg–Marquardt iteration minimizes $\sum \vert \mathbf{x}_{\text{observed}} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{projected}} \vert^2$.
- **P — Final Output:** The calibrated parameters are saved as `.yaml`, `.xml`, or `.json` for downstream use.

## 4.3 Intrinsic vs Extrinsic — Conceptual Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WORLD[World Frame]
        W1[3D Point Xw Yw Zw]
    end
    
    subgraph EXT_BLOCK[Extrinsic 3 by 4 Matrix R and t]
        E1[Rotation R 3 DOF Pitch Yaw Roll]
        E2[Translation t 3 DOF X Y Z Position]
    end
    
    subgraph INT_BLOCK[Intrinsic 3 by 3 Matrix K plus Distortion]
        I1[Focal Length fx fy]
        I2[Principal Point cx cy]
        I3[Skew gamma]
        I4[Radial Distortion k1 k2 k3]
        I5[Tangential Distortion p1 p2]
    end
    
    subgraph IMAGE[Image Frame]
        IM1[2D Pixel u v]
    end
    
    W1 --> EXT_BLOCK
    EXT_BLOCK --> INT_BLOCK
    INT_BLOCK --> IM1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order matters!** Extrinsic transformation is applied **first** (to enter the camera frame), and intrinsic projection is applied **second** (to land on the sensor). Reversing this order will produce garbage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are concise, definition-style, or short-derivation questions expecting a 3–4 sentence answer or a labeled diagram.

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Define intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters. List any four parameters from each category.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Intrinsic parameters** describe the internal optical and geometric properties of the camera. They are independent of the camera's position or orientation in the world. Four examples: focal length $(f_x, f_y)$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, skew coefficient $(\gamma)$, and lens distortion coefficients $(k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2)$.
- **Extrinsic parameters** describe the pose of the camera in the world coordinate system. Four examples: 3 rotation angles (pitch, yaw, roll) of matrix $\mathbf{R}$ and the 3 translation components $(t_x, t_y, t_z)$ of vector $\mathbf{t}$.
- Together they form the projection matrix $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$? Write its general form and explain each entry.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ converts 3D camera-frame coordinates (in meters) into 2D pixel coordinates. Its general form is:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $f_x, f_y$: focal length in pixel units along the $u$ and $v$ axes.
- $\gamma$: skew coefficient accounting for non-orthogonal pixel axes.
- $c_x, c_y$: principal point (the pixel where the optical axis meets the sensor).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions in Module 1 carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. The following two questions are mutually exclusive; students answer **one**.

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the complete perspective projection equation that maps a 3D world point to a 2D pixel coordinate, clearly identifying the extrinsic and intrinsic stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A camera has the following intrinsic parameters: $f_x = 1200$ px, $f_y = 1200$ px, $c_x = 640$ px, $c_y = 360$ px. The extrinsic parameters are $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}_3$ and $\mathbf{t} = (0, 0, 2)^\top$ m. Find the pixel coordinates of the 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (0.1, 0.2, 5)^\top$ m. Also state the physical interpretation of your result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^\top$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the extrinsic rigid transformation to obtain the camera-frame coordinates:
$$\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use similar triangles to project the camera-frame point onto the image plane at focal length $f$:
$$x_{\text{plane}} = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_{\text{plane}} = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Convert image-plane mm-coordinates to pixel coordinates using $s_x, s_y$ and the principal point:
$$u = \alpha \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = \beta \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Write the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ explicitly:
$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Combine into a single homogeneous equation:
$$\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Identify and label each component — intrinsic, extrinsic, and the depth scaling factor $\lambda = Z_c$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Since $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{t} = (0, 0, 2)^\top$ m, compute camera coordinates:
$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Construct the intrinsic matrix:
$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1200 & 0 & 640 \\ 0 & 1200 & 360 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $\mathbf{K} \mathbf{X}_c$:
$$\mathbf{K} \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 1200 \cdot 0.1 + 640 \\ 1200 \cdot 0.2 + 360 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 120 + 640 \\ 240 + 360 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 760 \\ 600 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Divide by $Z_c = 7$ (perspective divide) to obtain homogeneous pixel coordinates:
$$u = \frac{760}{7} \approx 108.57, \quad v = \frac{600}{7} \approx 85.71$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the result: The world point projects to pixel $(u, v) \approx (108.57, 85.71)$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Interpretation: The point appears in the upper-left quadrant of the image (since $u < c_x$ and $v < c_y$), which is consistent with its 3D position being slightly off-axis in the negative $X_c$ and $Y_c$ directions.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Note that the principal point $(640, 360)$ is the image center; the small offset of the projection from this center reflects the small lateral displacement of the 3D point from the optical axis.

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the pinhole camera model with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the coordinates of a 3D point and its image point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to camera calibration, distinguish between radial and tangential lens distortions. Derive the mathematical model used to correct each, assuming a 3rd-order radial polynomial. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Diagram — Draw a 2D cross-section showing the optical center $O$, the image plane at distance $f$ behind $O$, the principal axis, and a 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ projecting to $(x, y)$ on the image plane. Label the inverted image property.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply similar triangles: a ray from point $(X, Y, Z)$ passing through the optical center $O$ hits the image plane at:
$$x = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Explain that the image is inverted; this is conventionally corrected by placing the image plane in front of the pinhole at distance $f$ (mathematically equivalent for sign conventions).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Generalize to include the principal point offset:
$$u = f_x \frac{X}{Z} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the homogeneous form:
$$\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \mathbf{X}_c$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition and physical origin of each distortion:
- **Radial distortion:** caused by the radial curvature of the lens; light rays bend more at the periphery than at the center, producing barrel or pincushion effects.
- **Tangential distortion:** caused by the lens not being perfectly parallel to the image sensor; produces an asymmetric skew.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Radial distortion model (3rd-order polynomial):
$$x_d = x_u (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$
$$y_d = y_u (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$
where $r^2 = x_u^2 + y_u^2$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Tangential distortion model:
$$x_d = x_u + 2 p_1 x_u y_u + p_2(r^2 + 2 x_u^2)$$
$$y_d = y_u + p_1(r^2 + 2 y_u^2) + 2 p_2 x_u y_u$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Note that the total distortion is the sum of both, and the corrected pixel coordinates are obtained by:
$$u = f_x \cdot x_d + c_x, \quad v = f_y \cdot y_d + c_y$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the perspective divide:** Students often write $\mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}_w$ directly as $(u, v)$ without dividing by $\lambda = Z_c$. This loses **2 full marks** in any derivation question.
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{K}$ with $\mathbf{P}$:** $\mathbf{K}$ is $3 \times 3$ (intrinsic only); $\mathbf{P}$ is $3 \times 4$ (intrinsic + extrinsic). Examiners dock marks for writing the wrong size matrix.
> 3. **Skewing the signs in distortion equations:** The tangential terms have specific coefficient placements (e.g., $2 p_1 x y$ in $x$ but $p_1(r^2 + 2 y^2)$ in $y$). Memorize both formulas side-by-side.
> 4. **Not defining $r^2$:** Always explicitly state $r^2 = x_u^2 + y_u^2$ when using distortion polynomials.
> 5. **Mixing units:** $f$ in mm vs. $f_x$ in pixels — state units in every step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- **Two parameter families:** Intrinsic (camera internals) and Extrinsic (camera pose in the world).
- **Intrinsic Matrix $\mathbf{K}$:** $3 \times 3$, upper-triangular, 5 DOF (with skew) or 4 DOF (without skew, common assumption).
- **Extrinsic Matrix $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$:** $3 \times 4$, encodes 3 rotation + 3 translation DOF.
- **Projection Matrix $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$:** $3 \times 4$, takes a homogeneous 3D world point to a homogeneous 2D pixel point.
- **Master Equation:** $\lambda [u, v, 1]^\top = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}][X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^\top$, with $\lambda = Z_c$ being the depth.
- **Perspective Divide:** Always divide by the third (depth) component to recover true $(u, v)$.
- **Principal Point $(c_x, c_y)$:** Pixel coordinates of the optical axis intersection with the sensor — usually near the image center.
- **Focal Length $f_x, f_y$:** In pixel units, equals physical focal length divided by pixel pitch.
- **Radial Distortion:** Polynomial in $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$; parameters $k_1, k_2, k_3$.
- **Tangential Distortion:** Cross-term polynomial in $x, y$; parameters $p_1, p_2$.
- **Pinhole Model:** The ideal linear model; real cameras add distortion on top of it.
- **Calibration Method:** Zhang's method (checkerboard) is the de-facto standard; OpenCV's `calibrateCamera` returns $\mathbf{K}$, distortion coefficients, $\mathbf{R}$, and $\mathbf{t}$.
- **Orthogonality of $\mathbf{R}$:** $\mathbf{R}^\top \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\det \mathbf{R} = 1$.
- **Units Discipline:** Always state whether $f$ is in mm or pixels; always divide by $Z_c$ at the end.
- **Total DOF:** 14–16 (5–8 intrinsic + 6 extrinsic + 2–5 distortion).
- **Real-World Use:** AR, robotics, autonomous driving, photogrammetry, medical imaging, SLAM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stereopsis - Binocular Camera Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stereopsis - Binocular Camera Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stereopsis** is the biological and computational process of extracting depth information of a 3D scene by simultaneously viewing it from two or more slightly offset viewpoints, mimicking the geometry of the human binocular visual system. In computational vision, **Binocular Camera Geometry** is the mathematical framework that models the geometric relationship between two cameras (a stereo rig) observing the same scene point, and the resulting projective constraints that allow recovery of 3D structure from 2D image correspondences.

The cornerstone of this framework is the **Epipolar Geometry**, which describes the intrinsic projective relationship between two camera views. It is captured by a $3 \times 3$ singular matrix called the **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ (in pixel coordinates) or its specialized form, the **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ (in calibrated normalized coordinates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The focus is on the *geometry* — the algebraic constraints, the concept of the epipole, the epipolar line, the essential matrix, and the practical inversion of these to recover depth (triangulation). Calibration is the bridge from pixel to metric space.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Eye Ruler"

Imagine you close one eye and try to thread a needle — you fumble. Now open both eyes. The instant the second eye adds a *horizontally shifted* view, your brain magically judges how far the needle is. **Stereopsis is the engineering of that magic.**

Think of your two eyes as two cameras with a horizontal offset called the **baseline $B$** (typically $\mathbf{6 \text{ cm}}$ for a human, $\mathbf{5{-}15 \text{ cm}}$ for a stereo rig). A nearby object appears to "jump" more between the two views than a faraway object. This horizontal jump is called the **disparity $d$**, and disparity is *inversely proportional* to depth. That single relationship is the heart of every stereo vision system in robotics, autonomous vehicles, and 3D reconstruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Intuition Rule:** *Closer object → larger disparity. Farther object → smaller disparity. Infinite distance → zero disparity.*

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Focal length $f$** : Distance from the lens to the image sensor, measured in **millimeters** (physical) or **pixels** (digital).
- **Baseline $B$** : Distance between the optical centers of the two cameras, measured in the same world units as depth (e.g., **mm** or **m**).
- **Disparity $d$** : Horizontal pixel-shift between corresponding points, unit is **pixels** ($px$).
- **Depth $Z$** : Perpendicular distance from the camera baseline plane to the 3D point, same unit as $B$.
- **Standard stereo image pairs (KITTI, Middlebury)** use $B \approx 54 \text{ cm}$ to $10 \text{ cm}$ and $f \approx 700 \text{ px}$ to $2000 \text{ px}$.

## 1.4 The Binocular Camera Setup — A Bird's-Eye Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Optical Center $C$** | The pinhole of each camera where all rays converge. |
| **Optical Axis** | The line perpendicular to the image plane passing through $C$. |
| **Baseline** | The line segment $C_L C_R$ joining the two optical centers. |
| **Epipole $e_L$, $e_R$** | The image of one camera's optical center as seen by the other. |
| **Epipolar Plane** | The plane formed by $C_L$, $C_R$, and the 3D world point $P$. |
| **Epipolar Line** | The intersection of the epipolar plane with each image plane. |
| **Corresponding Point** | The pixel in one image that observes the same 3D point as a pixel in the other. |
| **Rectification** | The process of re-projecting both images so that epipolar lines are horizontal and aligned. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Stereopsis Works (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

1. **Two cameras with overlapping fields of view** capture the same 3D scene from a known horizontal offset $B$.
2. A **feature (e.g., corner, edge)** is detected in the left image, and the **same feature** must be located in the right image — this is the **correspondence problem**.
3. The geometric constraint that the two corresponding image points and the two optical centers must lie in a common **epipolar plane** reduces the 2D search for the right-image match to a **1D search along the epipolar line**.
4. After rectification, this 1D search becomes a **purely horizontal scan** along the same row $y = y_L = y_R$.
5. The **disparity** $d = x_L - x_R$ is computed and converted to depth using the **inverse-depth law** $Z = \dfrac{fB}{d}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Geometry

- **Why two views?** A single image is a *projection*, losing the depth dimension. A second view from a translated position recovers the lost dimension via *triangulation*.
- **Why baseline $B > 0$?** If $B = 0$, both cameras coincide and disparity is zero everywhere — no depth information. Larger $B$ ⇒ larger disparities ⇒ better depth resolution, but reduced overlap and more occlusion.
- **Why a $3 \times 3$ matrix?** The epipolar constraint $\mathbf{x}_L^{\top} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_R = 0$ is a single scalar bilinear equation in homogeneous coordinates; a $3 \times 3$ matrix is the most compact way to encode it.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars `|` in the table below** — using $\vert$ and $\mid$ for absolute / conditional syntax to preserve markdown compatibility.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \dfrac{fB}{d}$ | **Inverse-depth law** — depth from disparity | $Z$ in m, $f$ in px, $B$ in m, $d$ in px |
| 2 | $d = x_L - x_R$ | Disparity definition (rectified) | $x_L, x_R$ in px |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{(x_L - c_{xL}) \cdot Z}{f_L}, \quad Y = \dfrac{(y_L - c_{yL}) \cdot Z}{f_L}$ | 3D reconstruction (left-camera frame) | $c_{xL}, c_{yL}$ principal point (px) |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}_L^{\top} \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{x}_R = 0$ | **Epipolar constraint** in normalized coords | $\mathbf{x}$ homogeneous 3-vectors |
| 5 | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$ | Essential matrix from relative pose | $\mathbf{R}$ rotation, $\mathbf{t}$ translation |
| 6 | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{K}_R^{-1}$ | Fundamental matrix using intrinsics | $\mathbf{K}$ $3 \times 3$ intrinsics |
| 7 | $\mathbf{x}_L^{\top} \mathbf{F} \, \mathbf{x}_R = 0$ | **Epipolar constraint** in pixel coords | pixel-coord homogeneous vectors |
| 8 | $\det(\mathbf{F}) = 0$ | Singularity of $\mathbf{F}$ (rank 2) | scalar condition |
| 9 | $7$ DOF for $\mathbf{F}$, $5$ DOF for $\mathbf{E}$ | Degrees of freedom | — |
| 10 | $\mathbf{Q} \begin{bmatrix} x_L \\ y_L \\ d \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ W \end{bmatrix}$ | Disparity-to-3D via **Q-matrix** (reprojection matrix) | $4 \times 4$ reprojection matrix $\mathbf{Q}$ |
| 11 | $Z_{\min} = \dfrac{fB}{d_{\max}}$ | Minimum measurable depth (max detectable disparity) | limited by pixel pitch |
| 12 | $Z_{\max} = \dfrac{fB}{d_{\min}}$ | Maximum depth (limited by $d_{\min} = 1$ px) | far-field cutoff |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., KITTI dataset)**: Stereo rigs estimate obstacle distance $50{-}100$ m ahead.
- **Industrial Robot Bin Picking**: Stereo cameras reconstruct 3D pose of untextured mechanical parts for grasping.
- **Medical Endoscopy**: Stereo endoscopes give surgeons quantitative depth in minimally invasive surgery.
- **Aerial Mapping / Drone Photogrammetry**: Down-looking stereo pairs generate Digital Elevation Models (DEM).
- **AR/VR Headsets**: Stereo passthrough cameras estimate scene geometry for occlusion handling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth from Disparity (The Core Stereo Equation)

**Setup:** Two pinhole cameras with identical focal length $f$, perfectly aligned optical axes, and parallel image planes. The left camera's optical center is at the world origin. The right camera is translated by a baseline $B$ along the $X$-axis. A 3D world point $P$ has world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ where $Z > 0$.

**Goal:** Express $Z$ in terms of measurable image quantities.

**Step 1 — Project $P$ into the left camera.**
Using the standard pinhole projection $x = f \dfrac{X}{Z}$, $y = f \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ (with the principal point at the origin for simplicity), the left image coordinates are:
$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y_L = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Express the 3D point in the right camera's frame.**
Because the right camera's origin is shifted by $(B, 0, 0)$ in the world frame, the point $P$ has coordinates $(X - B, Y, Z)$ relative to the right camera. (Both cameras look down $+Z$ with no rotation between them — the *rectified* configuration.)

**Step 3 — Project $P$ into the right camera.**
$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}, \quad y_R = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the epipolar alignment.**
Since $y_L = y_R = fY/Z$, both image points lie on the **same horizontal scanline**, confirming that rectification makes the epipolar line horizontal.

**Step 5 — Compute disparity.**
$$d = x_L - x_R = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} = f \cdot \frac{B}{Z}$$

**Step 6 — Invert for depth.**
Rearranging $d \cdot Z = fB$:
$$\boxed{\,Z = \frac{fB}{d}\,}$$

**Sanity check:** When $Z \to \infty$, $d \to 0$ ✓. When $Z \to 0$ (point on baseline plane), $d \to \infty$ ✓ (the point cannot be seen by both cameras — it is occluded from one view).

**Step 7 — Recover the lateral coordinates $X$ and $Y$.**
Substituting $Z$ back into the projection equations:
$$X = \frac{x_L \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{x_L \cdot B}{d}, \quad Y = \frac{y_L \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{y_L \cdot B}{d}$$

The $3$D reconstruction in the left-camera frame is therefore:
$$(X, Y, Z) = \left( \frac{B \cdot x_L}{d}, \; \frac{B \cdot y_L}{d}, \; \frac{B \cdot f}{d} \right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Essential Matrix from Camera Pose

**Setup:** Let the world point $P$ be expressed as a homogeneous 4-vector $\mathbf{P}_w = (X, Y, Z, 1)^{\top}$. The left camera's extrinsic matrix is $[\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}]$, and the right camera's extrinsic matrix is $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ (relative to the left).

**Step 1 — Transform $P$ into the left camera frame.**
$$\mathbf{P}_L = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0}^{\top} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{P}_w = (X, Y, Z, 1)^{\top}$$

**Step 2 — Transform $P$ into the right camera frame.**
$$\mathbf{P}_R = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^{\top} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{P}_w = (\mathbf{R}\tilde{\mathbf{P}} + \mathbf{t}, \, 1)^{\top}$$
where $\tilde{\mathbf{P}} = (X, Y, Z)^{\top}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the coplanarity condition.**
The three vectors $\mathbf{P}_L$, $\mathbf{P}_R$, and $\mathbf{t}$ must be **coplanar** (they lie in the epipolar plane). The coplanarity of three vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$ is expressed as the vanishing scalar triple product:
$$\mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{a}^{\top} [\mathbf{b}]_{\times} \mathbf{c} = 0$$
where $[\mathbf{b}]_{\times}$ is the $3 \times 3$ **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix** of $\mathbf{b}$.

**Step 4 — Set up the triple product.**
Using $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{P}_L$, $\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{t}$, $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{P}_R$:
$$\mathbf{P}_L^{\top} [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{P}_R = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the rotation.**
$\mathbf{P}_R = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{P}_L + \mathbf{t}$, but the cleaner route is to define the **essential matrix** by absorbing the rotation:
$$\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$$
so that the epipolar constraint becomes:
$$\boxed{\,\mathbf{P}_L^{\top} \, \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{P}_R = 0\,}$$

where $\mathbf{P}_L, \mathbf{P}_R$ are the **normalized image coordinates** (i.e., multiplied by $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ to remove intrinsics).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: From Essential to Fundamental Matrix

**Step 1 — Define normalized (calibrated) coordinates.**
For pixel coordinates $\mathbf{u}_L = (u_L, v_L, 1)^{\top}$ and $\mathbf{u}_R = (u_R, v_R, 1)^{\top}$:
$$\mathbf{x}_L = \mathbf{K}_L^{-1} \mathbf{u}_L, \quad \mathbf{x}_R = \mathbf{K}_R^{-1} \mathbf{u}_R$$

**Step 2 — Plug into the essential constraint.**
$$\mathbf{u}_L^{\top} \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \, \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{K}_R^{-1} \mathbf{u}_R = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define the fundamental matrix.**
$$\boxed{\,\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{K}_R^{-1} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{K}_R^{-1}\,}$$
and the pixel-coord epipolar constraint is:
$$\mathbf{u}_L^{\top} \, \mathbf{F} \, \mathbf{u}_R = 0$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Epipolar Line Equation from $\mathbf{F}$

For a given left-image point $\mathbf{u}_L$, the set of right-image points $\mathbf{u}_R$ satisfying $\mathbf{u}_L^{\top} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{u}_R = 0$ lies on a line. In row-vector form, define the line coefficients in the right image:
$$\mathbf{l}_R = \mathbf{F}^{\top} \mathbf{u}_L$$
The equation of the epipolar line in the right image is then:
$$\mathbf{l}_R^{\top} \mathbf{u}_R = 0$$
i.e., $a_R u_R + b_R v_R + c_R = 0$ where $(a_R, b_R, c_R)^{\top} = \mathbf{F}^{\top} \mathbf{u}_L$.

**Symmetrically**, for a given right-image point $\mathbf{u}_R$:
$$\mathbf{l}_L = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{u}_R, \quad \mathbf{l}_L^{\top} \mathbf{u}_L = 0$$

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A rectified stereo rig has $f = 800 \text{ px}$, $B = 0.12 \text{ m}$. A feature detected at $x_L = 412$ px, $x_R = 388$ px (with $y_L = y_R = 256$ px). Find the depth $Z$ and the 3D coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$. Assume principal point at $(400, 300)$ px.

**Step 1 — Disparity.**
$$d = x_L - x_R = 412 - 388 = 24 \text{ px}$$

**Step 2 — Depth.**
$$Z = \frac{fB}{d} = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{24} = \frac{96}{24} = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Lateral coordinates (offset from principal point).**
$$\Delta x = x_L - c_x = 412 - 400 = 12 \text{ px}, \quad \Delta y = y_L - c_y = 256 - 300 = -44 \text{ px}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $X$ and $Y$.**
$$X = \frac{\Delta x \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{12 \times 4.0}{800} = 0.06 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ cm}$$
$$Y = \frac{\Delta y \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{-44 \times 4.0}{800} = -0.22 \text{ m} = -22 \text{ cm}$$

**Result:** The 3D point is at $\mathbf{(0.06, -0.22, 4.0) \text{ m}}$ relative to the left camera's optical center.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binocular Camera Geometry — Top-Down View

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **block-level functional architecture flow** of the binocular stereo geometry, including the rectified image-plane projection and the epipolar constraint propagation. It is a faithful representation of the standard textbook top-down stereo rig.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WorldSpace["WORLD 3D SPACE"]
        Pt["P (X, Y, Z)"]
    end

    subgraph LeftCam["LEFT CAMERA FRAME"]
        CL["Optical Center C_L"]
        IL["Image Plane (Left)"]
        pL["Image Point p_L = (x_L, y_L)"]
    end

    subgraph RightCam["RIGHT CAMERA FRAME"]
        CR["Optical Center C_R"]
        IR["Image Plane (Right)"]
        pR["Image Point p_R = (x_R, y_R)"]
    end

    subgraph EpiPlane["EPIPOLAR GEOMETRY"]
        EpiL["Epipole e_L (left)"]
        EpiR["Epipole e_R (right)"]
        LineL["Epipolar Line l_L"]
        LineR["Epipolar Line l_R"]
        Fmat["F or E Matrix"]
    end

    subgraph Recover["DEPTH RECOVERY"]
        Disp["Disparity d = x_L - x_R"]
        Depth["Depth Z = fB / d"]
        P3D["3D Point (X, Y, Z)"]
    end

    Pt --> CL
    Pt --> CR
    CL --> pL
    CR --> pR
    pL --> EpiL
    pR --> EpiR
    EpiL --> LineL
    EpiR --> LineR
    Fmat --> LineL
    Fmat --> LineR
    pL --> Disp
    pR --> Disp
    Disp --> Depth
    Depth --> P3D
```

## 4.2 Functional Pipeline — Correspondence to Triangulation

This diagram depicts the **end-to-end functional pipeline** from raw stereo image capture to 3D point cloud generation, which is the operation students will see in the KTU lab component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Capture Left Image I_L"] --> C["Feature Detection (Harris / SIFT / ORB)"]
    B["Capture Right Image I_R"] --> C
    C --> D["Rectification (Stereo Rectify)"]
    D --> E["Epipolar Constraint: x_L^T F x_R = 0"]
    E --> F["1D Scan along Epipolar Line"]
    F --> G["Stereo Matching (NCC / SSD / SGM)"]
    G --> H["Disparity Map d(u,v)"]
    H --> I["Inverse Depth: Z = fB / d"]
    I --> J["Back-project to 3D (X, Y, Z)"]
    J --> K["Point Cloud / Depth Map Output"]
```

## 4.3 Geometry Coordinate System — Pinhole Projective Model

The following Mermaid graph illustrates the algebraic dependency chain from world point to image pixel, highlighting the role of the intrinsic and extrinsic matrices used in deriving the essential and fundamental matrices.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W["World Point P_w (4x1)"] --> E1["Left Extrinsic [I | 0]"]
    W --> E2["Right Extrinsic [R | t]"]
    E1 --> N1["Camera Coords P_L (3x1)"]
    E2 --> N2["Camera Coords P_R (3x1)"]
    N1 --> K1["Intrinsic K_L (3x3)"]
    N2 --> K2["Intrinsic K_R (3x3)"]
    K1 --> Px1["Pixel p_L (3x1 hom.)"]
    K2 --> Px2["Pixel p_R (3x1 hom.)"]
    Px1 --> EpiC["Epipolar Constraint x_L^T E x_R = 0"]
    Px2 --> EpiC
    Px1 --> FundC["Pixel Constraint u_L^T F u_R = 0"]
    Px2 --> FundC
    EpiC --> Emat["E = [t]_x R"]
    FundC --> Fmat["F = K_L^-T E K_R^-1"]
```
