# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]
**Define stereopsis and explain the concept of disparity in binocular vision.**

**Model Answer:**
Stereopsis is the process of perceiving depth by viewing a scene from two slightly different viewpoints simultaneously, analogous to how the human eyes work. In computational stereo vision, two cameras separated by a horizontal **baseline $B$** capture images of the same scene. **Disparity $d$** is defined as the horizontal pixel-shift between the projections of the same 3D point in the left and right images: $d = x_L - x_R$ (after rectification). The depth $Z$ of the 3D point is then recovered via the **inverse-depth relation** $Z = fB / d$, where $f$ is the focal length. Closer objects produce larger disparities; farther objects produce smaller disparities.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Dec 2023]
**What is the essential matrix? How is it related to the fundamental matrix?**

**Model Answer:**
The **essential matrix $\mathbf{E}$** is a $3 \times 3$ rank-2 matrix that encodes the epipolar geometry between two **calibrated** cameras in terms of their relative rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$:
$$\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$$
It satisfies the epipolar constraint $\mathbf{x}_L^{\top} \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{x}_R = 0$ for **normalized image coordinates** $\mathbf{x}$. The **fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$** is the corresponding matrix in **pixel (uncalibrated) coordinates**, obtained by incorporating the camera intrinsics:
$$\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \, \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{K}_R^{-1}$$
so that $\mathbf{u}_L^{\top} \mathbf{F} \, \mathbf{u}_R = 0$ where $\mathbf{u}$ are pixel coordinates. $\mathbf{E}$ has **5 DOF**; $\mathbf{F}$ has **7 DOF**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Depth Computation and Epipolar Geometry

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q-B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A rectified stereo camera system has focal length $f = 1000$ pixels, baseline $B = 0.20$ m, and principal point at $(500, 500)$ pixels. For a 3D world point, the left image coordinate is $(u_L, v_L) = (640, 520)$ and the right image coordinate is $(u_R, v_R) = (600, 520)$. Compute the depth $Z$ and the 3D world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the essential matrix and the fundamental matrix. With the help of suitable equations, explain how the fundamental matrix can be estimated from point correspondences. State the algebraic error used in its estimation.

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Verify the epipolar alignment.** $v_L = v_R = 520$ confirms the epipolar line is horizontal (rectified configuration).

**Step 2 — Compute disparity.**
$$d = u_L - u_R = 640 - 600 = 40 \text{ px}$$

**Step 3 — Compute depth.**
$$Z = \frac{fB}{d} = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{40} = \frac{200}{40} = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute offsets from principal point.**
$$\Delta u = u_L - c_x = 640 - 500 = 140 \text{ px}$$
$$\Delta v = v_L - c_y = 520 - 500 = 20 \text{ px}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $X$ and $Y$.**
$$X = \frac{\Delta u \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{140 \times 5.0}{1000} = 0.70 \text{ m}$$
$$Y = \frac{\Delta v \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{20 \times 5.0}{1000} = 0.10 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{(X, Y, Z) = (0.70 \text{ m}, 0.10 \text{ m}, 5.0 \text{ m})}$.

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Definition of Essential Matrix:** The essential matrix $\mathbf{E}$ is a $3 \times 3$ singular matrix with **5 degrees of freedom** that encodes the relative pose between two calibrated cameras:
$$\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}, \quad \text{where } [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
The epipolar constraint in normalized coordinates: $\mathbf{x}_L^{\top} \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{x}_R = 0$.

**Definition of Fundamental Matrix:** The fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$ is the pixel-coordinate equivalent of $\mathbf{E}$, incorporating camera intrinsics:
$$\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \, \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{K}_R^{-1}, \quad \text{constraint: } \mathbf{u}_L^{\top} \mathbf{F} \, \mathbf{u}_R = 0$$

**Estimation from correspondences:** Given $N$ point correspondences $\{\mathbf{u}_L^{(i)}, \mathbf{u}_R^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, stack each correspondence as a row of a measurement matrix and solve the homogeneous linear system:
$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{f} = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{f} = \text{vec}(\mathbf{F}) \in \mathbb{R}^{9}$$
where each row of $\mathbf{A}$ is $(u_{L,i} u_{R,i}, \, u_{L,i} v_{R,i}, \, u_{L,i}, \, v_{L,i} u_{R,i}, \, v_{L,i} v_{R,i}, \, v_{L,i}, \, u_{R,i}, \, v_{R,i}, \, 1)$.

**Algebraic Error (Sampson Distance):** Since $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} = 0$ is over-determined for $N \geq 8$, the solution is the right-singular vector of $\mathbf{A}$ corresponding to the smallest singular value (via **SVD**). The residual — **Sampson distance** — used to assess fit is:
$$d_{\text{Sampson}} = \frac{(\mathbf{u}_L^{\top} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{u}_R)^2}{(\mathbf{F}\mathbf{u}_R)_1^2 + (\mathbf{F}\mathbf{u}_R)_2^2 + (\mathbf{F}^{\top}\mathbf{u}_L)_1^2 + (\mathbf{F}^{\top}\mathbf{u}_L)_2^2}$$
**Note:** The **8-point algorithm** requires $\geq 8$ correspondences; the **normalized 8-point** (Hartley, 1997) pre-normalizes coordinates to improve numerical conditioning.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q-B Alt.]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the fundamental relationship between depth and disparity in a rectified stereo vision system, clearly stating all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a stereo pair, the focal length is $600$ px and the baseline is $0.10$ m. (i) Find the disparity produced by an object at $Z = 3.0$ m. (ii) What is the minimum detectable depth if the maximum measurable disparity is $d_{\max} = 60$ px? (iii) Find the maximum depth for a minimum measurable disparity of $1$ px.

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Both cameras are **pinhole** with equal focal length $f$.
2. Cameras are **perfectly rectified** — parallel optical axes, coplanar image planes, horizontal baseline.
3. Principal points coincide at the origin of each image.
4. The world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ is visible to **both** cameras (no occlusion).

**Derivation:**

Projection into the left camera (origin at $C_L$):
$$x_L = \frac{fX}{Z}, \quad y_L = \frac{fY}{Z}$$

In the right camera's frame, the origin is shifted by $(B, 0, 0)$, so the point is $(X - B, Y, Z)$:
$$x_R = \frac{f(X - B)}{Z}, \quad y_R = \frac{fY}{Z}$$

Disparity:
$$d = x_L - x_R = \frac{fB}{Z}$$

Inverting:
$$\boxed{\,Z = \frac{fB}{d}\,}$$

The lateral coordinates follow as $X = x_L Z / f = B x_L / d$ and $Y = y_L B / d$. This is the **triangulation formula** of stereo vision.

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

Given: $f = 600$ px, $B = 0.10$ m.

**(i) Disparity at $Z = 3.0$ m:**
$$d = \frac{fB}{Z} = \frac{600 \times 0.10}{3.0} = \frac{60}{3.0} = 20 \text{ px}$$

**(ii) Minimum detectable depth ($d_{\max} = 60$ px):**
$$Z_{\min} = \frac{fB}{d_{\max}} = \frac{600 \times 0.10}{60} = \frac{60}{60} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Maximum depth ($d_{\min} = 1$ px):**
$$Z_{\max} = \frac{fB}{d_{\min}} = \frac{600 \times 0.10}{1} = 60 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $f$ must be in pixels, $B$ in world units (m), $d$ in pixels. Mixing them produces a nonsensical $Z$. Always write units in your working.
> 2. **Confusing $d$ direction:** Some texts define $d = x_R - x_L$; check the convention. KTU uses $d = x_L - x_R$.
> 3. **Skipping the principal-point offset:** Using $x_L$ directly without subtracting $c_x$ gives a wrong $X$. The principal point must be subtracted first.
> 4. **Confusing essential and fundamental matrices:** $\mathbf{E}$ is for *calibrated* (normalized) coordinates; $\mathbf{F}$ is for *pixel* coordinates. Writing $\mathbf{u}_L^{\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{u}_R = 0$ is a sure way to lose 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting $\det(\mathbf{F}) = 0$:** Many students forget that $\mathbf{F}$ is rank 2, not rank 3. The SVD post-processing step (forcing the smallest singular value to zero) is essential.
> 6. **Not verifying epipolar alignment:** If $v_L \neq v_R$ in a "rectified" problem, the rectification is faulty — note this in your answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stereopsis** = depth from two views; **baseline $B$** is the horizontal offset between the cameras.
- The **inverse-depth law** $Z = \dfrac{fB}{d}$ is the single most important formula in stereo vision — **memorize it**.
- **Disparity** $d = x_L - x_R$ is measured in **pixels**; depth $Z$ comes out in the **same unit as $B$**.
- A **larger baseline** $B$ ⇒ **better depth resolution** (larger $d$ for the same $Z$) but **smaller overlap** between views.
- **Closer ⇒ larger $d$**; **Farther ⇒ smaller $d$**; **Infinity ⇒ $d = 0$**.
- The **epipolar constraint** reduces the correspondence search from 2D to 1D (along the epipolar line).
- After **rectification**, epipolar lines are **horizontal** and aligned across both images — $y_L = y_R$.
- The **essential matrix** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$ operates on **normalized** coordinates; has **5 DOF**.
- The **fundamental matrix** $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}_R^{-1}$ operates on **pixel** coordinates; has **7 DOF**.
- $\det(\mathbf{F}) = 0$ (rank 2); estimated via the **8-point algorithm** and refined with **Sampson distance**.
- **Minimum depth** $Z_{\min} = fB / d_{\max}$ — limited by image resolution and matching range.
- **Maximum depth** $Z_{\max} = fB / d_{\min}$ — limited by the smallest reliable disparity (typically $d_{\min} = 1$ px).
- Always **subtract the principal point** $c_x, c_y$ from pixel coordinates before back-projection.
- The **3D reconstruction** is $X = Bx_L / d$, $Y = By_L / d$, $Z = fB / d$ (rectified, principal point at origin).
- **Q-matrix** in OpenCV maps disparity directly to $(X, Y, Z, W)$ for efficient dense reconstruction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Epipolar Constraint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Epipolar Constraint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — Epipolar Constraint

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epipolar Constraint** is a geometric relationship that exists between two 2D image points (and their corresponding 3D scene point) when the same physical scene is photographed from two different camera viewpoints. Mathematically, it states that *if `x` and `x'` are the projections of the same 3D point `X` in two views, then these points, the camera centers, and the 3D point all lie on a single common plane known as the **Epipolar Plane**. Consequently, the corresponding image point `x'` in the second view must lie on a specific line (the **epipolar line**) in that view — and this condition can be compactly expressed as the scalar equation **`x'ᵀ F x = 0`**, where `F` is the **Fundamental Matrix** of rank 2.*

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the Epipolar Constraint is treated as the *cornerstone* of **stereo vision**, **structure-from-motion (SfM)**, and **multi-view geometry**. It transforms an otherwise exponential correspondence-search problem into a *1-dimensional line-search* problem — an enormous reduction in computational complexity.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long, dark corridor holding a torch. You see a single object hanging in space (the 3D point `X`). Now, a friend stands at the other end of the corridor and sees the *same* object from a different angle.

- The **object** = the 3D scene point `X`.
- **Your eye** = the first camera center `C`.
- **Your friend's eye** = the second camera center `C'`.
- The **line from your eye to the object** is the *first viewing ray*.
- The **line from your friend's eye to the object** is the *second viewing ray*.

> [!NOTE]
> 🔦 **The "Flashlight" Analogy:** Both you and your friend can independently shine a flashlight along your viewing rays. Where the two light cones *intersect* is where the object is. The **Epipolar Constraint** is the rule that says: *"Whatever angle you tilt your head, the object, your eye, and your friend's eye are always coplanar (lie on the same flat sheet of paper)."* This "sheet of paper" is the **epipolar plane**, and where it cuts your friend's view is the **epipolar line**.

So instead of your friend searching the *entire wall* to find where the object appears, they only need to look along a *single line* on the wall. That is the power of the Epipolar Constraint — it reduces a 2D search to a 1D search.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Camera Center (1st) | `C` | Optical center of the left camera |
| Camera Center (2nd) | `C'` | Optical center of the right camera |
| Baseline | `C–C'` | Line joining the two camera centers |
| Epipole (left) | `e` | Projection of `C'` in the left image |
| Epipole (right) | `e'` | Projection of `C` in the right image |
| Epipolar Plane | `π` | Plane containing `C`, `C'`, and `X` |
| Epipolar Line | `l'` | Intersection of `π` with the right image plane |
| Correspondence | `x ↔ x'` | Pixels in two images of the same 3D point |

---

### 1.4 Why It Matters (Engineering Significance)

- **Stereo Depth Estimation:** The disparity (offset) between `x` and `x'` along conjugate epipolar lines is inversely proportional to depth.
- **3D Reconstruction:** Given `F` and matched correspondences, the 3D point `X` can be triangulated.
- **Feature Matching Speed-Up:** In algorithms like SIFT/SURF + RANSAC, the constraint eliminates **>95%** of false matches.
- **SLAM & Robotics:** Critical for autonomous navigation, drone mapping, and AR overlays.
- **Film & VFX:** Used in *The Matrix* "bullet-time" rigs and modern volumetric video capture.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When answering, always remember that the **epipolar constraint is a co-planarity condition** — it is fundamentally a *geometric* constraint, not a photometric one.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Epipolar Geometry Pipeline

The epipolar constraint is derived from a chain of geometric and projective operations. Below is the structured logical flow that KTU expects students to internalize:

### Step 1 — The World Point
A 3D point `X = (X, Y, Z, 1)ᵀ` exists in the world (Euclidean) coordinate frame.

### Step 2 — First Projection
The first camera, with intrinsic matrix `K` and extrinsic `[R₁ ∣ t₁]`, projects `X` into the left image as:

$$x = K [R_1 \mid t_1] X$$

### Step 3 — Second Projection
Similarly, the second camera projects the same `X` to the right image as:

$$x' = K' [R_2 \mid t_2] X$$

### Step 4 — Relative Pose
Without loss of generality, we set the world frame coincident with the first camera, so `R₁ = I`, `t₁ = 0`, and define the **relative motion**:

$$R = R_2 R_1^{T} = R_2, \quad t = t_2 - t_2$$

This gives a clean two-view relation.

### Step 5 — Normalized Image Coordinates
Removing the intrinsics, we work in *normalized* (calibrated) coordinates:

$$\hat{x} = K^{-1} x, \quad \hat{x}' = K'^{-1} x'$$

### Step 6 — The Coplanarity Condition
The three points `C`, `C'`, `X` are coplanar. The vectors `C'−C`, `X−C`, and `X'−C'` all lie in the same plane. In vector form:

$$(\hat{x}' \times \hat{x}) \cdot t = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cross product `t × x` produces a vector perpendicular to the epipolar plane, and the dot product with `x'` (which lies in the plane) must vanish.

### Step 7 — Skew-Symmetric Formulation
The cross product can be written as a matrix-vector product using the **skew-symmetric matrix** `[t]×`:

$$\hat{x}'^{T} \, [t]_{\times} \, R \, \hat{x} = 0$$

### Step 8 — Define the Essential Matrix
The product `[t]× R` is the **Essential Matrix** `E` (3×3, rank 2, with 5 degrees of freedom).

$$\hat{x}'^{T} E \hat{x} = 0$$

### Step 9 — Re-introduce Intrinsics
Substituting back `K` and `K'`, the constraint in raw pixel coordinates is:

$$x'^{T} K'^{-T} E K^{-1} x = 0$$

### Step 10 — Define the Fundamental Matrix
The 3×3 **Fundamental Matrix** `F` encapsulates *all* intrinsic and extrinsic parameters relating the two views:

$$F = K'^{-T} E K^{-1} = K'^{-T} [t]_{\times} R K^{-1}$$

The constraint reduces to the iconic form:

$$x'^{T} F x = 0$$

This is the **Epipolar Constraint Equation** that KTU board questions are built around.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below — these equations appear in **every** KTU 2024 ESE question paper on this topic.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Notes / Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x'^{T} F x = 0$ | Epipolar constraint in pixels | Scalar equation; `F` is 3×3 |
| 2 | $\hat{x}'^{T} E \hat{x} = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (calibrated) | `E` is 3×3 essential matrix |
| 3 | $F = K'^{-T} E K^{-1}$ | F ↔ E relationship | `K`, `K'` are 3×3 intrinsics |
| 4 | $E = [t]_{\times} R$ | Essential matrix from pose | `R` 3×3, `t` 3×1 |
| 5 | $[t]_{\times} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ | Skew-symmetric translation | Rank 2, `det = 0` |
| 6 | $\text{rank}(F) = 2$ | F is rank deficient | `det(F) = 0` |
| 7 | $\text{rank}(E) = 2$ | E is rank deficient | `det(E) = 0` |
| 8 | $l' = F x$ | Epipolar line in image 2 | `l'` is a 3-vector (line) |
| 9 | $l = F^{T} x'$ | Epipolar line in image 1 | Dual relation |
| 10 | $F e = 0$, $F^{T} e' = 0$ | Epipoles lie on F's null space | e, e' are 3-vectors |
| 11 | $x'^{T} l' = 0$ | Point lies on its epipolar line | Used in 8-point algorithm |
| 12 | $E E^{T} E = \frac{1}{2}\text{tr}(E E^{T}) E$ | Cubic constraint on E | For self-calibration |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Stereo cameras compute depth from `F` for obstacle avoidance (e.g., Tesla, Waymo). |
| **Medical Imaging** | Endoscopic 3D reconstruction from two camera feeds inside the body. |
| **Cultural Heritage** | Photogrammetric 3D scans of monuments from tourist photos (e.g., UNESCO projects). |
| **Space Exploration** | NASA's Mars rovers use epipolar geometry for terrain mapping from stereo pairs. |
| **AR/VR Headsets** | Inside-out tracking relies on epipolar constraints for real-time SLAM. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are **exam-ready**. Each algebraic transition is followed by a justification line. KTU examiners award *method marks* when transitions are explained, not just written.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Epipolar Constraint (x'ᵀ F x = 0)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider two cameras `C` and `C'` with intrinsic matrices `K` and `K'`, and a common 3D scene point `X` (homogeneous). The pinhole projection models are:

$$x = K [I \mid 0] X, \quad x' = K' [R \mid t] X$$

**Logical Justification:** Without loss of generality, we align the world frame with the first camera. The second camera is then expressed in this frame via rotation `R` and translation `t`.

---

### 3.1.2 Inverse Projection (Back-projection)

To recover rays in 3D, we strip away the intrinsics:

$$\hat{x} = K^{-1} x, \quad \hat{x}' = K'^{-1} x'$$

**Logical Justification:** `K` maps 3D camera coordinates to pixel coordinates; its inverse lifts pixels back to rays on the normalized plane `Z = 1`.

The back-projected 3D rays from each camera center through the observed pixels are:

$$X = \lambda \hat{x} \quad \text{(from C)}, \quad X = \lambda' R^{T} \hat{x}' - R^{T} t \quad \text{(from C')}$$

**Logical Justification:** A 3D point along the first ray is just a scalar `λ` times the direction. The second ray uses the inverse transformation `[R ∣ t]⁻¹ = [Rᵀ ∣ -Rᵀ t]`.

---

### 3.1.3 Eliminate the 3D Point `X`

Equating the two ray expressions:

$$\lambda \hat{x} = \lambda' R^{T} \hat{x}' - R^{T} t$$

Pre-multiplying by `R`:

$$\lambda R \hat{x} = \lambda' \hat{x}' - t$$

Rearranging:

$$\lambda' \hat{x}' = \lambda R \hat{x} + t$$

---

### 3.1.4 The Coplanarity / Epipolar Constraint

**Logical Justification:** All three points `C`, `C'`, `X` must be coplanar. A 3D vector `v` lies in the same plane as vectors `a` and `b` if and only if the **scalar triple product** vanishes:

$$v \cdot (a \times b) = 0$$

We set `v = X − C = λ R \hat{x}` and recognize that `t = C' − C` is also in the plane, while the plane is spanned by `t` and `R \hat{x}`. The normal to the epipolar plane is therefore `t × (R \hat{x})`, and the third point's direction (which is `λ R \hat{x}`) lies in the plane, so its dot product with the normal is `0` — but the *second* camera ray `λ' \hat{x}'` also lies in this plane, giving:

$$\hat{x}' \cdot \big( t \times (R \hat{x}) \big) = 0$$

**Logical Justification:** Since `λ' \hat{x}'` is in the plane, any scalar multiple — and hence `\hat{x}'` itself — must satisfy the same coplanarity condition.

---

### 3.1.5 Vector Triple Product → Skew-Symmetric Form

Using the identity `a · (b × c) = (a × b) · c`, and the skew-symmetric formulation `a × b = [a]× b`:

$$\hat{x}'^{T} \, [t]_{\times} \, (R \hat{x}) = 0$$

**Logical Justification:** The skew-symmetric matrix `[t]×` encodes the cross product as a linear operator — this is a standard result in multi-view geometry (Hartley & Zisserman convention).

---

### 3.1.6 Define the Essential Matrix

$$\boxed{E = [t]_{\times} R}$$

Then:

$$\hat{x}'^{T} E \hat{x} = 0$$

**Logical Justification:** `E` is a 3×3 matrix that captures *all* relative pose information (R, t) between the cameras, and it has the property that any pair of calibrated correspondences satisfies this scalar equation.

---

### 3.1.7 Re-inject Intrinsics → Fundamental Matrix

Substitute `\hat{x} = K^{-1} x` and `\hat{x}' = K'^{-1} x'`:

$$(K'^{-1} x')^{T} E (K^{-1} x) = 0$$

$$x'^{T} (K'^{-T} E K^{-1}) x = 0$$

Define the **Fundamental Matrix**:

$$\boxed{F = K'^{-T} E K^{-1}}$$

This yields the canonical epipolar constraint:

$$\boxed{x'^{T} F x = 0}$$

**Logical Justification:** `F` carries *both* intrinsic and extrinsic information. It is the natural epipolar operator in *uncalibrated* pixel coordinates — which is the realistic engineering case (we rarely know K perfectly).

---

## 3.2 Properties of the Fundamental Matrix (Algebraically Derived)

| Property | Derivation Step | Final Form |
|---|---|---|
| Singular | `det(F) = det(K'^{-T}) det([t]×) det(R) det(K^{-1})`. Since `det([t]×) = 0` (skew-symmetric of odd dim with last row dependent), `det(F) = 0`. | rank(F) = 2 |
| 7 DOF | 9 entries of F, but `det(F) = 0` (1 constraint) and *scale* is irrelevant (1 constraint) → 9 − 2 = 7 | DOF(F) = 7 |
| Epipolar line in view 2 | For a given `x` in view 1, the locus of `x'` is `l' = F x`. Proof: any `x'` on the line satisfies `x'ᵀ (F x) = 0`. | `l' = F x` |
| Epipolar line in view 1 | By symmetry: `l = Fᵀ x'`. | `l = Fᵀ x'` |
| Epipoles | `e` satisfies `F e = 0` (left null space of F). `e'` satisfies `Fᵀ e' = 0`. | `Fe = 0, Fᵀ e' = 0` |

---

## 3.3 The 8-Point Algorithm (Bonus Derivation for KTU Full-Mark Answers)

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves asking: *"How is F computed from point correspondences?"* — this is the standard answer.

Given `N ≥ 8` point correspondences `{(x_i, x'_i)}`, the constraint `x'_iᵀ F x_i = 0` is linear in the 9 unknowns of `F = (f_{ij})`. Stacking:

$$\begin{aligned}
(x'_1 u_1,\; x'_1 v_1,\; x'_1,\; y'_1 u_1,\; y'_1 v_1,\; y'_1,\; u_1,\; v_1,\; 1) \cdot \text{vec}(F) &= 0 \\
(x'_2 u_2,\; x'_2 v_2,\; x'_2,\; y'_2 u_2,\; y'_2 v_2,\; y'_2,\; u_2,\; v_2,\; 1) \cdot \text{vec}(F) &= 0 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
(x'_N u_N,\; x'_N v_N,\; x'_N,\; y'_N u_N,\; y'_N v_N,\; y'_N,\; u_N,\; v_N,\; 1) \cdot \text{vec}(F) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

This is `A · f = 0` where `A` is `N×9`. The solution is the **right singular vector** of `A` corresponding to its smallest singular value, obtained via SVD. A final rank-2 enforcement `F' = U \cdot \text{diag}(σ₁, σ₂, 0) · Vᵀ` ensures `det(F') = 0`.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on **actual KTU 2024 Scheme ESE patterns**: 3-mark short-answer (Part A) and 14-mark choice-based long answer (Part B). Each long-answer carries 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the epipolar constraint in two-view geometry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The epipolar constraint states that *if a 3D scene point `X` is projected to image points `x` in the first view and `x'` in the second view, then `x` and `x'` must lie on a common plane — the **epipolar plane** — together with the two camera centers `C` and `C'`.*

In algebraic form, given the 3×3 **Fundamental Matrix** `F`, the constraint is:

$$x'^{T} F x = 0$$

This scalar equation (1 mark) reduces the correspondence search from 2D to 1D (1 mark) and is foundational for stereo matching and 3D reconstruction (1 mark).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Essential Matrix and the Fundamental Matrix.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Essential Matrix `E` | Fundamental Matrix `F` |
|---|---|---|
| Coordinates | Operates on **calibrated** (normalized) image points | Operates on **raw pixel** image points |
| Formula | $E = [t]_{\times} R$ | $F = K'^{-T} E K^{-1}$ |
| Intrinsics | Independent of camera intrinsics | Depends on both camera intrinsic matrices |
| Use case | When cameras are **calibrated** | When cameras are **uncalibrated** (real-world) |

`F` is a generalization of `E` that absorbs the calibration (1 mark), `E` is the special case when both `K = I` (1 mark), and the relation `F = K'^{-T} E K^{-1}` bridges them (1 mark).

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Internal Choice)

### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the epipolar constraint equation `x'ᵀ F x = 0` starting from the pinhole camera projection model. State clearly the geometric meaning of every term. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Projection Models (1 mark):**
Place the world frame at the first camera center. The two projections are:
$$x = K [I \mid 0] X, \quad x' = K' [R \mid t] X$$

**Step 2 — Inverse projection to normalized coords (1 mark):**
$$\hat{x} = K^{-1} x, \quad \hat{x}' = K'^{-1} x'$$

**Step 3 — Coplanarity of `C`, `C'`, `X` (2 marks):**
The vectors `t = C' − C`, `R \hat{x}`, and `\hat{x}'` are coplanar. By the scalar triple product rule:
$$\hat{x}' \cdot \big( t \times (R \hat{x}) \big) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Skew-symmetric reformulation (1 mark):**
Using `a × b = [a]× b`:
$$\hat{x}'^{T} [t]_{\times} R \hat{x} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Define `E` and `F` (1 mark):**
With $E = [t]_{\times} R$ and $F = K'^{-T} E K^{-1}$:
$$x'^{T} F x = 0$$

**Step 6 — Geometric meaning of terms (1 mark):**
- `F` (3×3, rank 2): encodes intrinsic + extrinsic geometry of the two views.
- `x`, `x'`: 2D homogeneous pixel coordinates.
- The equation asserts *coplanarity* of camera centers, scene point, and image points.

---

**(b) Given two cameras with intrinsic matrices `K = K' = diag(800, 800, 1)` (focal length 800 px, principal point at origin, no skew), and relative pose `R = I` (identity), `t = (0.5, 0, 0)ᵀ` meters (baseline 0.5 m along X-axis). Compute the fundamental matrix `F`. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Form `[t]×` (2 marks):**
For $t = (0.5, 0, 0)^{T}$:

$$[t]_{\times} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -0.5 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute `E` (2 marks):**
Since $R = I$:

$$E = [t]_{\times} \cdot I = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -0.5 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute `F` (2 marks):**
With $K = K' = \text{diag}(800, 800, 1)$, we have $K^{-1} = \text{diag}(1/800, 1/800, 1)$ and $K'^{-T} = \text{diag}(1/800, 1/800, 1)$. Therefore:

$$F = \text{diag}(1/800, 1/800, 1) \cdot E \cdot \text{diag}(1/800, 1/800, 1)$$

$$F = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -0.5/800 \\ 0 & 0.5/800 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Final form (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{F = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -6.25 \times 10^{-4} \\ 0 & 6.25 \times 10^{-4} & 0 \end{pmatrix} \; \text{per meter}}$$

The zero first column shows that the **epipole lies on the X-axis** — consistent with a translation along X.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(a) Explain the geometric interpretation of the Fundamental Matrix. Derive the equations `l' = F x` and `l = Fᵀ x'`. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Geometric Interpretation (3 marks):**
The Fundamental Matrix `F` is a 3×3 matrix of rank 2 that maps a 2D image point `x` in view 1 to the **epipolar line** `l'` in view 2 on which its correspondence must lie. It is the algebraic encoding of the epipolar geometry, summarizing *all* projective relationships between the two views without requiring a Euclidean 3D reconstruction.

**Derivation of `l' = F x` (2 marks):**
Starting from $x'^{T} F x = 0$, treat `x` as fixed. The set of all `x'` satisfying the equation is the line:

$$l' = F x, \quad \text{such that} \quad x'^{T} l' = 0$$

**Derivation of `l = Fᵀ x'` (2 marks):**
By symmetry, exchanging the roles of the two views and using $(x'^{T} F x)^{T} = x^{T} F^{T} x' = 0$:

$$l = F^{T} x' \quad \text{with} \quad x^{T} l = 0$$

This dual symmetry is a direct consequence of the constraint's bilinear structure.

---

**(b) Describe the 8-point algorithm for computing the Fundamental Matrix from point correspondences. Why is the rank-2 enforcement step necessary? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stack the Linear Equations (2 marks):**
From $x'^{T} F x = 0$ with $x = (u, v, 1)^{T}$, $x' = (u', v', 1)^{T}$ and $F = (f_{ij})$, we obtain one linear equation per correspondence. Stacking `N ≥ 8` correspondences:

$$A \cdot f = 0, \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 9}$$

where $f = (f_{11}, f_{12}, f_{13}, f_{21}, \dots, f_{33})^{T}$ and the *i*-th row of `A` is $(u'_i u_i, u'_i v_i, u'_i, v'_i u_i, v'_i v_i, v'_i, u_i, v_i, 1)$.

**Step 2 — Solve via SVD (2 marks):**
The solution is the singular vector corresponding to the smallest singular value of `A`, i.e., $f = V_{:,9}$ where $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$. This minimizes `‖A f‖` subject to `‖f‖ = 1`.

**Step 3 — Rank-2 Enforcement (2 marks):**
The estimated `F̂` from SVD generally has full rank (rank 3), but theory demands `rank(F) = 2` because $\det([t]×) = 0$. We project:

$$F = U' \cdot \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, 0) \cdot V'^{T}$$

where $F̂ = U' \Sigma' V'^{T}$ is the SVD of the initial estimate. This forces `det(F) = 0`, placing the epipoles correctly.

**Step 4 — Why it matters (1 mark):**
Without rank-2 enforcement, the epipolar lines will *not* all pass through a common epipole, breaking the geometric consistency that epipolar geometry demands.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting homogeneity:** The equation $x'^{T} F x = 0$ holds in *homogeneous* coordinates — students often plug in raw pixel values without the third coordinate = 1 (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing `F` and `E`:** `E` requires *normalized* coordinates; `F` works in *pixels*. Mixing them is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Wrong sign in `[t]×`:** The skew-symmetric convention $[t]_{\times} = \begin{psmallmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{psmallmatrix}$ — the off-diagonal sign must be `+` on the upper-right, `-` on the lower-left. Many students invert this.
> 4. **Ignoring the rank-2 constraint:** Reporting a rank-3 `F` from SVD *without* the projection step loses full credit on algorithm questions.
> 5. **Skew-symmetric determinant:** Students forget $\det([t]×) = 0$ and try to invert it during derivations — the matrix is singular by construction.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Night-Review Card):**

- ✅ The **epipolar constraint** is fundamentally a **coplanarity condition** among `C`, `C'`, `X`, `x`, `x'`.
- ✅ Canonical form: $x'^{T} F x = 0$ — **in homogeneous pixel coordinates**.
- ✅ Calibrated form: $\hat{x}'^{T} E \hat{x} = 0$ — **in normalized coordinates**.
- ✅ Relation: $F = K'^{-T} E K^{-1}$, and $E = [t]_{\times} R$.
- ✅ `F` has **7 DOF** (9 entries − 1 determinant-zero − 1 scale).
- ✅ `det(F) = 0` ⇒ `rank(F) = 2` ⇒ all epipolar lines concur at an **epipole**.
- ✅ Epipoles: $F e = 0$ (epipole in image 1) and $F^{T} e' = 0$ (epipole in image 2).
- ✅ Epipolar line in image 2: $l' = F x$. Epipolar line in image 1: $l = F^{T} x'$.
- ✅ The **8-point algorithm** computes `F` via SVD on a stacked linear system from `N ≥ 8` correspondences.
- ✅ **Always enforce rank 2** on the estimated `F` by zeroing the smallest singular value.
- ✅ Skew-symmetric rule: $a \times b = [a]_{\times} b$, and $\det([a]_{\times}) = 0$.
- ✅ **Engineering impact:** SLAM, autonomous driving, AR/VR, photogrammetry, medical imaging, space robotics.
- ✅ **KTU keyword triggers:** *"epipolar line"*, *"fundamental matrix"*, *"essential matrix"*, *"rank deficient"*, *"8-point algorithm"*, *"coplanarity"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binocular Reconstruction (Stereo Reconstruction)** is the process of recovering the three-dimensional (3D) geometric structure of a scene by analyzing two or more two-dimensional (2D) images captured from distinct viewpoints, typically using a **stereo camera pair** (binocular system) with a known spatial separation called the **baseline**.

In formal computer vision terms, binocular reconstruction solves the **inverse projection problem**: given two 2D image points $p_L$ and $p_R$ in the left and right cameras respectively (which correspond to the same physical 3D point $P$ in space), the objective is to estimate the 3D coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ of $P$ with respect to a chosen world/camera reference frame.

The two foundational pillars of this technique are:
1. **Epipolar Geometry** — the geometric relationship between two cameras observing the same scene.
2. **Triangulation** — the mathematical technique of back-projecting 2D rays from both cameras to find their intersection in 3D space.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: Human Stereoscopic Vision**

Imagine holding your thumb out at arm's length and alternately closing your left and right eye. You will notice that your thumb appears to "jump" horizontally against the background. This horizontal shift, called **disparity**, is the key signal our brain uses to perceive depth.

Your eyes are essentially a calibrated binocular camera system:
- **Baseline** $B$ ≈ distance between your eyes (≈ **6.3 cm** on average).
- **Focal length** $f$ ≈ the effective focal length of the eye lens.
- **Disparity** $d$ = horizontal pixel shift between left and right retinal images.
- **Depth** $Z$ is inversely proportional to disparity: closer objects → larger disparity; farther objects → smaller disparity.

The binocular reconstruction pipeline in computer vision mimics exactly this biological process — it is essentially *"engineering human depth perception."*

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics Used in Binocular Reconstruction (Industry Standards):**
> - **Baseline** $B$ : typically **5 cm – 30 cm** for indoor rigs, up to **>1 m** for outdoor automotive stereo (e.g., KITTI dataset).
> - **Focal length** $f$ : measured in **pixels** (after intrinsic calibration) or **mm** (physical).
> - **Disparity range** $d$ : usually **0 – 255 pixels** (8-bit disparity map).
> - **Depth accuracy** : depends on $Z^2 / (f \cdot B)$; doubles the baseline → doubles depth accuracy.
> - **Reprojection error** : standard validation metric, typically **< 0.5 pixels** for well-calibrated rigs.

## 1.4 The Core Mathematical Setup

Given two pinhole cameras (left $C_L$ and right $C_R$) with parallel optical axes separated by a baseline $B$, a 3D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to:
- Left image: $x_L = f \cdot X / Z$
- Right image: $x_R = f \cdot (X - B) / Z$

The **disparity** is defined as the horizontal pixel difference:

$$d = x_L - x_R = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}$$

This single equation is the heart of binocular reconstruction — it is the *master equation* every KTU student must memorize.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Stereo Vision Pipeline (5 Logical Stages)

The binocular reconstruction process is executed in a strict sequence. Each stage depends on the correctness of the previous one.

1. **Camera Calibration (Intrinsics + Extrinsics)**
   - Determines focal length $f$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, and lens distortion coefficients.
   - Determines the relative rotation $R$ and translation $t$ between the two cameras.
   - **Why:** Without calibration, pixel coordinates cannot be converted to true ray angles in 3D space.

2. **Image Rectification**
   - Reprojects both images onto a common plane such that corresponding epipolar lines become **horizontal and scanline-aligned**.
   - **Why:** Reduces the correspondence search from a 2D problem to a 1D problem (only along the same row).

3. **Stereo Correspondence (Matching)**
   - For every pixel $(u, v)$ in the left image, find the corresponding pixel $(u - d, v)$ in the right image.
   - **Why:** This is the only step that actually measures the *physical signal* (disparity) from the images.

4. **Disparity Map Computation**
   - Produces a dense (or sparse) map $d(u, v)$ where each pixel stores its disparity value.
   - **Why:** Disparity is the bridge between 2D image coordinates and 3D metric depth.

5. **Triangulation / 3D Reconstruction**
   - Back-projects matched 2D points to 3D rays and finds their intersection.
   - **Why:** This converts the disparity map into the final 3D point cloud or depth map.

## 2.2 The Epipolar Geometry — The Geometric Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Epipolar Geometry** describes the intrinsic geometric relationship between two cameras viewing the same 3D scene from different viewpoints. It constrains where a point in one image can appear in the other.

### Core Epipolar Entities

| Entity | Symbol | Intuitive Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Epipole | $e_L, e_R$ | The image of one camera's center as seen by the other camera |
| Epipolar Plane | $\pi$ | Plane formed by the two camera centers and the 3D point $P$ |
| Epipolar Line | $l_L, l_R$ | The intersection of the epipolar plane with each image plane |
| Baseline | $B$ | The line connecting the two camera optical centers |
| Essential Matrix | $E$ | Encodes rotation and translation between cameras (calibrated) |
| Fundamental Matrix | $F$ | Encodes epipolar geometry in pixel coordinates (uncalibrated) |

### The Epipolar Constraint Equation

The fundamental constraint that any matched pair of image points $(p_L, p_R)$ must satisfy is:

$$p_R^{\top} \cdot F \cdot p_L = 0$$

In homogeneous coordinates. This means: the matched right point $p_R$ must always lie on the epipolar line $l_R = F \cdot p_L$ induced by the left point.

## 2.3 Essential Matrix vs. Fundamental Matrix

| Property | Essential Matrix $E$ | Fundamental Matrix $F$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Coordinates** | Normalized image coordinates | Raw pixel coordinates |
| **Requires calibration?** | Yes (intrinsics known) | No (works uncalibrated) |
| **Formula** | $E = [t]_{\times} \cdot R$ | $F = K^{-\top} \cdot E \cdot K^{-1}$ |
| **Rank** | 2 (with two equal non-zero SVDs) | 2 |
| **DoF** | 5 | 7 |
| **Use case** | Calibrated stereo rigs | General two-view geometry |

Where $[t]_{\times}$ is the **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix** of the translation vector $t$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize These for the Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $d = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}$ | Disparity-Depth relation (rectified) | Core depth computation |
| 2 | $Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | Depth from disparity | Inverse depth map |
| 3 | $X = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}$ | Back-projection (X-coordinate) | 3D reconstruction |
| 4 | $Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$ | Back-projection (Y-coordinate) | 3D reconstruction |
| 5 | $p_R^{\top} \cdot F \cdot p_L = 0$ | Epipolar constraint | Point matching validation |
| 6 | $E = [t]_{\times} \cdot R$ | Essential matrix from extrinsics | Calibration |
| 7 | $F = K^{-\top} \cdot E \cdot K^{-1}$ | Fundamental matrix conversion | Uncalibrated case |
| 8 | $\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta d$ | Depth error (1 pixel disparity error) | Accuracy analysis |
| 9 | $d_{\min} \leq d \leq d_{\max}$ | Disparity search range | Stereo matching |
| 10 | $Z_{\min} = \frac{f \cdot B}{d_{\max}}$, $Z_{\max} = \frac{f \cdot B}{d_{\min}}$ | Depth range bounds | System design |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** In any table or derivation, never use the pipe symbol `$\vert$` notation inside a markdown table. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table parser. (This is enforced in the KTU answer-booklet scanning pipeline.)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Binocular Reconstruction is Used in Industry:**

- **Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., Tesla, Waymo):** Real-time obstacle detection and depth estimation using forward-facing stereo pairs.
- **Robotics (Boston Dynamics, ABB):** Manipulation, SLAM, and 3D scene understanding.
- **Medical Imaging:** Stereo endoscopy for minimally invasive 3D surgery.
- **Augmented Reality (ARKit, ARCore):** Real-time depth-aware scene reconstruction.
- **Photogrammetry & Cartography:** 3D terrain mapping from aerial stereo imagery.
- **Industrial Metrology:** Non-contact dimensional inspection of manufactured parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Disparity-Depth Master Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Consider a perfectly rectified stereo pair. Both cameras have the same focal length $f$, parallel optical axes, and are separated by a baseline $B$ along the X-axis. The right camera's center is at $(B, 0, 0)$ and the left camera's center is at the origin $(0, 0, 0)$. A 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ exists in front of the cameras ($Z > 0$).

**Step 1 — Project $P$ into the left camera's image plane.**

By the pinhole projection model, a 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ projects to a 2D image coordinate $(x, y)$ via:

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y_L = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Textual logic:** The X-coordinate of the image is the focal length times the ratio of the world X-distance to the world depth Z. This is pure similar-triangles geometry from the pinhole model.

**Step 2 — Project $P$ into the right camera's image plane.**

The right camera's coordinate system is shifted by $+B$ along the X-axis. So the point $P$ has coordinates $(X - B, Y, Z)$ *relative to the right camera's center*. Applying the pinhole model again:

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}, \quad y_R = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Textual logic:** We subtract $B$ because the right camera's origin is offset by $B$ in the world X-direction. The Y and Z coordinates are unaffected because the cameras share the same Y-axis and Z-axis (rectified configuration).

**Step 3 — Compute the disparity $d$.**

The disparity is defined as the horizontal displacement of the same 3D point between the two images:

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= x_L - x_R \\[4pt]
&= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{f}{Z} \cdot \big[ X - (X - B) \big] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{f}{Z} \cdot B \\[4pt]
&= \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual logic:** The $X$ terms cancel out beautifully, leaving only the baseline and depth. This is the celebrated result — disparity depends on nothing but depth, focal length, and baseline. The X and Y coordinates of the 3D point do not appear!

**Step 4 — Solve for depth $Z$.**

Inverting the relation to express depth as a function of measured disparity:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Textual logic:** This is the operational form used in software. You measure $d$ in pixels from the images, and as long as you know $f$ (in pixels) and $B$ (in meters or mm), you can compute the metric depth $Z$ of every pixel.

## 3.2 Derivation: 3D Coordinates from Disparity (Back-Projection)

**Step 1 — Start from the disparity relation.**

We have $Z = f B / d$ from Section 3.1. To recover the full 3D coordinate, we need $X$ and $Y$ as well.

**Step 2 — Recover $X$ using the left camera's projection.**

From the pinhole projection in the left camera (in pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ with principal point $(c_x, c_y)$):

$$u = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}$$

**Textual logic:** We isolate $X$ by multiplying both sides by $Z/f$ and subtracting the principal point offset $c_x$.

**Step 3 — Recover $Y$ similarly.**

$$v = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Z = f B / d$ into $X$ and $Y$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{(u - c_x)}{f} \cdot \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot B}{d} \\[4pt]
Y &= \frac{(v - c_y)}{f} \cdot \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot B}{d} \\[4pt]
Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual logic:** Notice how the focal length $f$ cancels out in $X$ and $Y$! This is a beautiful property of a rectified stereo pair — the 3D coordinates depend only on the pixel coordinates, the baseline, and the disparity. The focal length is only needed for $Z$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Depth Error Propagation

**Step 1 — Set up the differential relation.**

Differentiate the depth equation $Z = f B / d$ with respect to $d$:

$$\frac{dZ}{dd} = -\frac{f \cdot B}{d^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B}$$

**Textual logic:** The derivative is negative (depth decreases as disparity increases, as expected) and its magnitude grows quadratically with $Z$.

**Step 2 — Express the absolute depth error for a unit disparity error $\Delta d$.**

$$\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta d$$

**Textual logic:** A 1-pixel error in disparity produces a depth error that scales as $Z^2$. Doubling the depth quadruples the error. This is why far-field stereo is fundamentally harder than near-field stereo.

## 3.4 Derivation: The Epipolar Constraint in Rectified Coordinates

**Step 1 — Recall the rectified geometry.**

After rectification, the corresponding points $(u_L, v)$ and $(u_R, v)$ have the **same vertical coordinate $v$** (scanline-aligned). The only unknown is the horizontal shift $d = u_L - u_R$.

**Step 2 — Write the y-equation equality.**

From the pinhole projection in both cameras:

$$y_L = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} = y_R$$

**Textual logic:** The Y-coordinates of the projections are equal in a rectified system, because both cameras have parallel Y-axes. This is the mathematical statement of "epipolar lines are horizontal."

**Step 3 — Combine with the X-equation difference.**

$$x_L - x_R = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z} = d$$

**Textual logic:** The X-coordinates differ only by the disparity. The full 2D search problem has been reduced to a 1D search along horizontal scanlines — this is the entire purpose of rectification.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Input | Operation | Output | Justification |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Two 2D images | Calibrate cameras | $K_L, K_R, R, t$ | Zhang's method, checkerboard |
| 2 | Calibrated pair | Rectify | Scanline-aligned pair | Hartley algorithm / Bouguet |
| 3 | Rectified pair | Match pixels | Disparity $d(u,v)$ | SGM, block matching, NCC |
| 4 | Disparity map | Triangulate | Depth $Z(u,v)$ | $Z = fB/d$ |
| 5 | Depth map | Back-project | 3D point cloud | $X,Y$ from $u,v,Z$ |
| 6 | Point cloud | Validate | Reprojection error | Quality control metric |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Binocular Stereo Geometry — Top-Down View

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the rectified binocular setup, the two cameras, the baseline, and the back-projection rays converging on a 3D point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCENE["3D SCENE WITH POINT P"]
        P3D["WORLD POINT P X Y Z"]
    end

    subgraph CAML["LEFT CAMERA"]
        CL["OPTICAL CENTER CL at 0,0,0"]
        IL["LEFT IMAGE PLANE xL plane"]
    end

    subgraph CAMR["RIGHT CAMERA"]
        CR["OPTICAL CENTER CR at B,0,0"]
        IR["RIGHT IMAGE PLANE xR plane"]
    end

    BLINE["BASELINE B horizontal separation"]

    P3D -- "ray 1: X over Z" --> IL
    P3D -- "ray 2: X minus B over Z" --> IR
    CL -- "left projection" --> IL
    CR -- "right projection" --> IR
    CL -. "baseline distance B" .-> CR
    IL -- "disparity d = xL minus xR" --> IR

    DEPTH["DEPTH Z = f times B over d"]
    IL --> DEPTH
    IR --> DEPTH
```

## 4.2 The Complete Binocular Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: STEREO IMAGE PAIR IL AND IR"] --> B["STAGE 1: CAMERA CALIBRATION"]
    B --> B1["Intrinsic matrix K"]
    B --> B2["Extrinsic rotation R and translation t"]
    
    B1 --> C["STAGE 2: IMAGE RECTIFICATION"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1["Scanline aligned images"]
    
    C1 --> D["STAGE 3: STEREO MATCHING"]
    D --> D1["Block matching or SGM or NCC"]
    D1 --> D2["DISPARITY MAP d of u,v"]
    
    D2 --> E["STAGE 4: DEPTH COMPUTATION"]
    E --> E1["Z equals f times B over d"]
    E1 --> E2["DEPTH MAP Z of u,v"]
    
    E2 --> F["STAGE 5: 3D BACK PROJECTION"]
    F --> F1["X equals u minus cx times B over d"]
    F --> F2["Y equals v minus cy times B over d"]
    F --> F3["3D POINT CLOUD"]
    
    F3 --> G["STAGE 6: VALIDATION"]
    G --> G1["Reprojection error check"]
    G1 --> H["OUTPUT: METRIC 3D RECONSTRUCTION"]
```

## 4.3 Epipolar Geometry Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P3D2["3D POINT P"] -- "projection ray 1" --> PL["LEFT IMAGE POINT pL"]
    P3D2 -- "projection ray 2" --> PR["RIGHT IMAGE POINT pR"]
    
    PL -- "epipolar line in right image" --> LL["EPIPOLAR LINE lR"]
    PR -- "must lie on this line" --> LL
    
    EL["LEFT EPIPOLE eL"] -. "projection of right camera center" .-> PL
    ER["RIGHT EPIPOLE eR"] -. "projection of left camera center" .-> PR
    
    BASELINE2["BASELINE LINE"] -- "defines epipolar plane" --> P3D2
```

## 4.4 The Correspondence Problem Search Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"IS THE SYSTEM RECTIFIED?"}
    Q1 -- "YES" --> S1["SEARCH ONLY ALONG SAME SCANLINE V"]
    Q1 -- "NO" --> S2["SEARCH ALONG EPIPOLAR LINE L"]
    S1 --> M1["1D SEARCH range d_min to d_max"]
    S2 --> M2["2D CONSTRAINED SEARCH along lR"]
    M1 --> OUT["MATCHED CORRESPONDENCE"]
    M2 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define the following terms with respect to binocular stereo vision: (i) Baseline, (ii) Disparity, (iii) Epipole.**

### Model Answer:

**(i) Baseline:** The baseline $B$ is the physical distance between the optical centers of the two cameras in a stereo vision system. It is one of the most critical design parameters because depth accuracy is directly proportional to the baseline length. A larger baseline yields better depth resolution but reduces the common field of view.

**(ii) Disparity:** Disparity $d$ is defined as the horizontal pixel displacement between the projections of the same 3D world point in the left and right rectified images: $d = x_L - x_R$. It is the fundamental observable signal in stereo vision and is inversely proportional to depth: $d = f B / Z$.

**(iii) Epipole:** The epipole is the projection of one camera's optical center onto the image plane of the other camera. There are two epipoles, $e_L$ and $e_R$. All epipolar lines in an image pass through the epipole. In a rectified stereo pair, the epipoles lie at infinity along the horizontal direction.

> *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for each clear, accurate definition. Mentioning the mathematical symbol is recommended.)*

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **State and explain the epipolar constraint equation. Why is it important in stereo matching?**

### Model Answer:

The epipolar constraint is the foundational geometric constraint in two-view geometry. For a 3D point $P$ projecting to image points $p_L$ in the left camera and $p_R$ in the right camera, the constraint is:

$$p_R^{\top} \cdot F \cdot p_L = 0$$

This equation states that the corresponding right-image point $p_R$ must lie on the epipolar line $l_R = F \cdot p_L$ induced by the left point $p_L$ through the fundamental matrix $F$.

**Importance in stereo matching:**
- It reduces the correspondence search from a **2D problem** (searching the entire right image) to a **1D problem** (searching along a single epipolar line).
- It eliminates thousands of false matches by enforcing geometric consistency.
- It is the mathematical basis for highly efficient algorithms such as rectification, SGM (Semi-Global Matching), and the 8-point algorithm for estimating $F$.

> *(Examiner's note: The equation itself is worth 1 mark. Explanation of why it reduces search dimensionality is worth 2 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the rectified binocular stereo vision setup. Derive the relationship between disparity and depth for a point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ in the scene.**

### Model Solution:

**Diagram Description (Rectified Stereo Setup):**
Draw two parallel cameras with optical axes parallel to the Z-axis. The left camera center is at $C_L = (0, 0, 0)$ and the right camera center is at $C_R = (B, 0, 0)$. Their image planes are at $z = f$ in their respective coordinate systems. Draw a 3D point $P$ in front of the cameras. Show the projection rays from $P$ to the two image planes, forming similar triangles. Mark the projected points $x_L$ and $x_R$ on the two image planes, with $x_L > x_R$ (left point is further right in image coordinates).

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

**(1)** Projection of $P$ onto the left image plane using pinhole model:

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

**(2)** Projection of $P$ onto the right image plane (the right camera is shifted by $+B$ along the X-axis, so $P$'s X-coordinate relative to the right camera is $X - B$):

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}$$

**(3)** The disparity is the horizontal difference between the two projections:

$$d = x_L - x_R = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}$$

**(4)** Simplifying by combining over a common denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{f \cdot X - f \cdot (X - B)}{Z} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{f \cdot X - f \cdot X + f \cdot B}{Z} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

**(5)** Solving for depth $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Conclusion:** The disparity is directly proportional to the baseline and focal length, and inversely proportional to depth. Closer objects produce larger disparities. The relationship is independent of the world coordinates $(X, Y)$ of the point.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A stereo camera system has a baseline $B = 12$ cm and a focal length $f = 800$ pixels. A 3D point is observed with a disparity of 16 pixels.**
**(i) Calculate the depth $Z$ of the point.**
**(ii) If the disparity measurement has an error of $\pm 1$ pixel, calculate the resulting depth uncertainty.**
**(iii) What will be the disparity of an object at $Z = 4$ m?**

### Model Solution:

**Given:** $B = 12$ cm $= 0.12$ m, $f = 800$ pixels, $d = 16$ pixels.

**(i) Depth calculation:**

Using $Z = f B / d$:

$$Z = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{16} = \frac{96}{16} = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Depth uncertainty for $\Delta d = 1$ pixel:**

Using the error propagation formula $\Delta Z = Z^2 / (f B) \cdot \Delta d$:

$$\Delta Z = \frac{(6.0)^2}{800 \times 0.12} \times 1 = \frac{36}{96} \times 1 = 0.375 \text{ m}$$

So the depth estimate is $6.0 \pm 0.375$ m, i.e., $Z \in [5.625, 6.375]$ m.

**(iii) Disparity at $Z = 4$ m:**

Using $d = f B / Z$:

$$d = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{4} = \frac{96}{4} = 24 \text{ pixels}$$

**Verification:** As expected, the closer object (4 m) has larger disparity (24 px) than the farther object (6 m, 16 px). This confirms the inverse relationship.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of epipolar geometry in stereo vision. Define the essential matrix and the fundamental matrix. Derive the relationship between them.**

### Model Solution:

**Concept of Epipolar Geometry:**

Epipolar geometry is the intrinsic projective geometry between two cameras. It does not depend on the scene structure, only on the cameras' internal parameters and relative pose. For any 3D point $P$ projecting to $p_L$ in the left image and $p_R$ in the right image, the five camera centers, the two image points, and the two epipoles all lie in a single common plane called the **epipolar plane**.

The **epipolar line** is the intersection of this epipolar plane with an image plane. Critically, the corresponding point $p_R$ in the right image is guaranteed to lie on the epipolar line $l_R$ determined by $p_L$. This is the **epipolar constraint**, which drastically constrains the correspondence search.

**Essential Matrix $E$:**

The essential matrix $E$ encodes the epipolar geometry in **normalized image coordinates** (i.e., after intrinsic calibration). It is a $3 \times 3$ matrix with rank 2 and 5 degrees of freedom, defined as:

$$E = [t]_{\times} \cdot R$$

where $R$ is the $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix and $t$ is the $3 \times 1$ translation vector between the two cameras, and $[t]_{\times}$ is the skew-symmetric matrix:

$$[t]_{\times} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The essential matrix satisfies: $p_R^{\top} \cdot E \cdot p_L = 0$ for any pair of corresponding points in normalized coordinates.

**Fundamental Matrix $F$:**

The fundamental matrix $F$ encodes the epipolar geometry in **raw pixel coordinates**. It is also $3 \times 3$ with rank 2, but has 7 degrees of freedom, defined as:

$$F = K^{-\top} \cdot E \cdot K^{-1}$$

where $K$ is the camera intrinsic matrix. It satisfies: $p_R^{\top} \cdot F \cdot p_L = 0$ in pixel coordinates.

**Relationship between $E$ and $F$:**

Given the camera intrinsic matrix $K$, the essential matrix in normalized coordinates can be converted to the fundamental matrix in pixel coordinates using $F = K^{-\top} E K^{-1}$. Conversely, $E = K^{\top} F K$. The essential matrix requires calibrated cameras; the fundamental matrix works for uncalibrated setups.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the five stages of the binocular reconstruction pipeline. With an example, explain how a disparity map is converted to a 3D point cloud.**

### Model Solution:

**The Five Stages:**

**(1) Camera Calibration:** Determine the intrinsic parameters (focal length $f$, principal point $c_x, c_y$, distortion coefficients) of each camera and the extrinsic parameters (rotation $R$ and translation $t$ between the two cameras). Typically done with a checkerboard pattern using Zhang's method.

**(2) Image Rectification:** Reproject both images onto a common plane so that epipolar lines are horizontal and aligned with image rows. This reduces correspondence search to 1D.

**(3) Stereo Correspondence / Matching:** For each pixel in the left image, find the corresponding pixel in the right image along the same row. Algorithms include block matching, Semi-Global Matching (SGM), and deep-learning-based matchers.

**(4) Disparity Map Generation:** The output of matching is a disparity map $d(u,v)$ where each pixel has a disparity value. Invalid pixels (occlusions, textureless regions) are typically marked with a special value (e.g., $-1$ or 0).

**(5) Triangulation / Depth Computation:** For each pixel with a valid disparity, compute the depth $Z = f B / d$ and back-project to obtain 3D coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$. The collection of all such points is the 3D point cloud.

**Numerical Example:**

Consider pixel $(u, v) = (320, 240)$ in a $640 \times 480$ image with $c_x = 320, c_y = 240, f = 500$ pixels, $B = 0.1$ m, and measured disparity $d = 25$ pixels.

**Step 1:** Compute depth:

$$Z = \frac{f B}{d} = \frac{500 \times 0.1}{25} = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $X$ and $Y$:

$$X = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(320 - 320) \times 0.1}{25} = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(240 - 240) \times 0.1}{25} = 0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** The 3D coordinate is $(0, 0, 2.0)$ m, which is the principal point's line-of-sight at 2 m depth. Other pixels with non-zero $(u - c_x)$ and $(v - c_y)$ will produce non-zero $X$ and $Y$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in disparity:** $d = x_L - x_R$, NOT $x_R - x_L$. The left point is always further from the left image's principal axis than the right point, so $d > 0$ for visible points.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in the formula $Z = fB/d$:** If $f$ is in pixels and $B$ is in meters, then $Z$ comes out in meters. Mixing mm and cm is a common error.
> 3. **Forgetting the principal point $(c_x, c_y)$:** When back-projecting, many students write $X = u \cdot Z / f$ instead of $X = (u - c_x) \cdot Z / f$. This is a **3-mark deduction** in typical KTU valuation.
> 4. **Confusing $E$ and $F$:** Essential matrix works in normalized (calibrated) coordinates; fundamental matrix works in pixel (uncalibrated) coordinates. Writing the wrong one in the wrong context costs 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in derivations:** KTU examiners require explicit step-by-step algebra. Writing only the final result with no derivation yields at most **30% marks** for that sub-question.
> 6. **Forgetting to state the assumption of rectification:** The disparity formula $Z = fB/d$ is valid **only** for rectified stereo pairs. For unrectified systems, full triangulation is required.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Binocular Reconstruction**

- **Binocular reconstruction** recovers 3D structure from two 2D images captured from distinct viewpoints.
- **Baseline $B$** = physical distance between two camera centers; longer baseline = better depth accuracy.
- **Disparity $d$** = horizontal pixel difference $x_L - x_R$ between matching points in a rectified pair.
- **Master equation:** $Z = f B / d$ — memorize this symbol-by-symbol.
- **Depth error** scales as $Z^2$: $\Delta Z = Z^2 \cdot \Delta d / (f B)$ — far-field stereo is fundamentally harder.
- **Epipolar geometry** is camera-relative, not scene-dependent — it constrains the correspondence search to 1D.
- **Epipolar constraint:** $p_R^{\top} F p_L = 0$ — the cornerstone equation of two-view geometry.
- **Essential matrix $E$:** for calibrated cameras; $E = [t]_{\times} R$.
- **Fundamental matrix $F$:** for uncalibrated cameras; $F = K^{-\top} E K^{-1}$.
- **Rectification** aligns epipolar lines to image rows — required to use the simple 1D disparity formula.
- **Pipeline order:** Calibration → Rectification → Matching → Disparity Map → Triangulation → 3D Point Cloud.
- **Back-projection equations** (rectified): $X = (u - c_x) B / d$, $Y = (v - c_y) B / d$, $Z = f B / d$.
- **Limiting factors:** occlusions, textureless surfaces, repetitive patterns, reflective surfaces, narrow baseline, and large depth.
- **KTU must-derive results:** (1) disparity-depth formula, (2) epipolar constraint, (3) depth error relation, (4) back-projection to 3D.
- **Numerical sanity check:** If $f$ doubles, disparity doubles for the same depth (linear). If $B$ doubles, disparity doubles. If $Z$ doubles, disparity halves (inverse).
- **Industry standards:** KITTI benchmark uses $f \approx 720$ px, $B = 0.54$ m for automotive stereo. Middlebury stereo benchmark uses $f \approx 700-4000$ px depending on dataset.
- **Biological inspiration:** Human binocular vision has $B \approx 6.3$ cm and operates effectively from $\sim 0.5$ m to $\sim 200$ m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Methods for Binocular Fusion
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Local Methods for Binocular Fusion

# 👁️ Local Methods for Binocular Fusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Focus (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1):** This topic sits within the *Image Formation, Stereo Vision, and 3D Reconstruction* cluster. It is a high-yield area frequently tested in Part A (3 marks) as well as Part B (14 marks) long-answer problems. Mastering this section gives direct command over disparity estimation and correspondence matching — the heart of stereo vision.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Binocular Fusion** is the computational process of matching corresponding points (pixels) in a pair of stereo images — one captured by the **left camera** and the other by the **right camera** — such that the matched pairs represent the projection of the *same physical 3D point* in the scene onto the two image planes.

**Local Methods** for binocular fusion are a class of correspondence-matching algorithms that compute the disparity (and hence depth) at a given pixel by analyzing only a *small neighborhood* or *window* centered at that pixel in both the left and right images. They exploit the assumption that neighboring pixels in a real-world scene share similar intensity patterns (a direct consequence of the **brightness constancy assumption**).

Mathematically, the goal of a local binocular fusion method is to find the disparity $d$ at a pixel $(x, y)$ in the left image such that:

$$I_L(x, y) \approx I_R(x - d, y)$$

where:
- $I_L$ = left image intensity function
- $I_R$ = right image intensity function
- $d$ = horizontal disparity (pixels)
- The match is searched along the **epipolar line** (which, after rectification, is a horizontal scanline).

> [!NOTE]
> **Brightness Constancy Assumption:** The fundamental premise that the intensity of a physical point does not change between the two views. This is what makes local methods mathematically tractable but also why they fail on **specular**, **shadowed**, or **textureless** regions.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in front of a bookshelf and you close one eye at a time alternately.

- **Left eye open:** You see the red book slightly to the right of the green book.
- **Right eye open:** You see the red book *much more* to the right of the green book (the red book appears to "jump" because it is closer to you).

The brain automatically performs **binocular fusion** — it locks onto the red book in both views (the "correspondence problem") and uses the *horizontal shift* (disparity) to figure out *how close* the red book is to your face.

> **The closer an object is, the larger its disparity; the farther an object is, the smaller its disparity.**

**Local Methods** are like matching the red book by looking at a *small patch around it* (its "neighborhood texture" — the title, the spine pattern, etc.) rather than analyzing the whole bookshelf. The algorithm slides a small **matching window** over both images and asks:

> *"In which position does the right-image window look most like the left-image window?"*

The horizontal shift that gives the best match is the **disparity**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall Cue:** Local methods = **window-based neighborhood matching**. Always mention the *brightness constancy assumption* and the *epipolar constraint* in your answer opener for guaranteed marks.

---

## 1.3 Position in the KTU Syllabus

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Module** | Module 1 – Fundamentals & Image Formation |
| **Prerequisite Topics** | Camera geometry, pinhole model, epipolar geometry, image rectification |
| **Lead-in Topic** | Epipolar Geometry & Rectification |
| **Leads to** | Global methods (dynamic programming, graph cuts), 3D reconstruction |
| **Industry Application** | Autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo), robotics (Boston Dynamics), AR/VR depth sensors, industrial 3D inspection |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Binocular Fusion Methods

Binocular fusion is broadly divided into two families:

1. **Local (Window-Based / Area-Based) Methods** — *the topic of this note*
   - Use a small correlation window
   - Compute a similarity/dissimilarity metric per pixel
   - Fast, parallelizable, suitable for hardware
   - Examples: SSD, SAD, NCC

2. **Global Methods** — *contrast topic*
   - Minimize an energy function over the entire image
   - Enforce smoothness/ordering constraints globally
   - Examples: Dynamic Programming, Graph Cuts, Belief Propagation

> [!NOTE]
> Local methods produce **dense** but **noisy** disparity maps. Global methods produce **sparse-but-clean** or **smooth** disparity maps but are computationally heavy. **Semi-local (e.g., Semi-Global Matching — SGM)** methods bridge the two, but are out of scope for the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

---

## 1.5 The Correspondence Problem — The Core Challenge

The **correspondence problem** is: *Given a point in the left image, find its match in the right image.* The challenge arises because:

- **Many candidate points** in the right image could visually match (false matches).
- **Occlusions:** A point visible in the left image may not be visible in the right (or vice versa).
- **Repetitive textures:** Brick walls, fences, and windows all create ambiguity.
- **Photometric variations:** The two cameras may differ in gain, exposure, or noise.
- **Foreshortening:** A 3D patch may appear geometrically distorted in the second view.

Local methods tackle this by constraining the search to a **1D epipolar line** and using a **similarity metric over a window** to disambiguate.

---

## 1.6 The Role of the Matching Window

The choice of window size is the **single most important design decision** in any local method:

| Window Size | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Small** (3×3, 5×5) | Captures fine depth details, good at depth discontinuities (object edges) | Noisy matches, sensitive to image noise, ambiguous in textureless regions |
| **Large** (21×21, 31×31) | Robust to noise, stable in textured regions, fewer false matches | Blurs depth at object boundaries, fails on slanted surfaces |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often write that "larger windows are always better." This is **false** — large windows cause **foreground fattening** (depth edges get pushed outward). Always mention the trade-off in your answer.

---

## 1.7 Mathematical Notation Convention (Used Throughout)

For pixel $(x, y)$ in the left image and a candidate disparity $d$:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $I_L(x, y)$ | Intensity of left image at $(x, y)$ |
| $I_R(x, y)$ | Intensity of right image at $(x, y)$ |
| $W$ | A 2D window of size $(2w+1) \times (2w+1)$ centered at the pixel |
| $d$ | Disparity (scalar, in pixels) |
| $d_{\min}, d_{\max}$ | Search range of disparities |
| $D(x, y)$ | Final disparity map value at $(x, y)$ |
| $\Omega$ | Set of pixels inside the matching window $W$ |

---

## 1.8 Section 1 Quick Recap

- **Binocular fusion** = matching left & right stereo image points to recover depth.
- **Local methods** = window-based neighborhood matching along epipolar lines.
- Core assumption: **brightness constancy** $I_L(x, y) \approx I_R(x - d, y)$.
- The fundamental output: a **disparity map** $D(x, y)$.
- Window size is a critical trade-off parameter.
- Three cornerstone algorithms: **SSD**, **SAD**, **NCC** (next section).

---
