# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Bounding Box Regression from Anchors

> [!NOTE]
> The bounding box regression branch refines an anchor box $a = (a_x, a_y, a_w, a_h)$ into a predicted box $b = (b_x, b_y, b_w, b_h)$ using the predicted deltas $t = (t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$.

### Step 1: Define the Parameterization

The predicted deltas are decoded into absolute box coordinates using:

$$
b_x = a_w \cdot t_x + a_x
$$

$$
b_y = a_h \cdot t_y + a_y
$$

$$
b_w = a_w \cdot \exp(t_w)
$$

$$
b_h = a_h \cdot \exp(t_h)
$$

**Reasoning:**
- $b_x, b_y$: The center of the box is shifted from the anchor center $(a_x, a_y)$ by a *scale-invariant* offset $(t_x, t_y)$ multiplied by the anchor dimensions. This makes the prediction invariant to object scale.
- $b_w, b_h$: Width and height are *multiplicatively* scaled using $\exp(t_w)$ and $\exp(t_h)$ to ensure they are strictly positive (since $\exp(\cdot) > 0$ for all real inputs).

### Step 2: Define the Target Encoding

For training, ground-truth box $g = (g_x, g_y, g_w, g_h)$ relative to anchor $a$ is encoded as:

$$
t_x^* = \frac{g_x - a_x}{a_w}, \quad t_y^* = \frac{g_y - a_y}{a_h}
$$

$$
t_w^* = \log\left(\frac{g_w}{a_w}\right), \quad t_h^* = \log\left(\frac{g_h}{a_h}\right)
$$

**Reasoning:** These are simply the **algebraic inverses** of the decoding equations, allowing the network to predict residuals in a normalized space that is easier to learn.

### Step 3: Smooth L1 Loss Minimization

The regression loss is computed on the deltas (not the raw coordinates):

$$
L_{reg}(t_i, t_i^*) = \text{smooth}_{L_1}(t_i - t_i^*)
$$

where $\text{smooth}_{L_1}(x) = 0.5 x^2$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$, and $\vert x \vert - 0.5$ otherwise. This loss is **less sensitive to outliers** than L2, while being **smoother near zero** than L1.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: IoU and Mask Quality Assessment

### Step 1: Intersection over Union (IoU) Definition

For two binary masks $A$ and $B$:

$$
\text{IoU}(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The numerator counts pixels that are **foreground in both** masks (logical AND); the denominator counts pixels that are **foreground in at least one** (logical OR).

### Step 2: Average Precision (AP) — Area Under PR Curve

Given sorted predictions by confidence, the precision-recall curve is:

$$
p(r) = \max_{r' \geq r} \frac{\text{TP}(r')}{\text{TP}(r') + \text{FP}(r')}
$$

$$
\text{AP} = \int_0^1 p(r) \, dr
$$

**Reasoning:** Recall $r$ increases monotonically as we lower the confidence threshold. For each recall, we take the **maximum precision** achievable at that recall level (this is the modern AP definition used in COCO, replacing the older 11-point interpolation).

### Step 3: Mean Average Precision (mAP)

For $K$ object classes:

$$
\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{AP}_k
$$

In COCO, mAP is further averaged over multiple **IoU thresholds** from 0.5 to 0.95 in steps of 0.05, giving the stringent **mAP@[0.5:0.95]** metric.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: RoIAlign Coordinate Mapping

### Step 1: Continuous RoI Projection

Given an RoI defined in the original image as $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$, project it onto the feature map of stride $s$:

$$
x_1' = \frac{x_1}{s}, \quad y_1' = \frac{y_1}{s}, \quad x_2' = \frac{x_2}{s}, \quad y_2' = \frac{y_2}{s}
$$

**Reasoning:** No rounding is performed — these are **continuous** values.

### Step 2: Bin Partitioning

Divide the RoI into $H_{out} \times W_{out}$ bins (e.g., $7 \times 7$ for the detection head, $14 \times 14$ for the mask head):

$$
\text{bin}_w = \frac{x_2' - x_1'}{W_{out}}, \quad \text{bin}_h = \frac{y_2' - y_1'}{H_{out}}
$$

### Step 3: Bilinear Interpolation

For each bin, sample $k \times k$ points (typically $k=2$, giving 4 sample points) at relative positions $(0.5, 0.5)$ offsets from bin corners. The feature value at a sub-pixel location $(x, y)$ is computed as:

$$
f(x, y) = \sum_{i, j \in \{(0,0),(0,1),(1,0),(1,1)\}} w_{ij} \cdot f(\lfloor x \rfloor + i, \lfloor y \rfloor + j)
$$

where weights $w_{ij}$ are computed based on the distance to the four nearest integer pixel locations.

**Conversion Logic:** Bilinear interpolation uses a **weighted average** of the four corner values, with weights inversely proportional to the sub-pixel distance.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Key Variants

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1: Comparative Analysis of Mask R-CNN Variants and Related Methods**

| Method | Year | Backbone | Mask AP (COCO) | Inference Speed | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCN** | 2015 | VGG-16 | N/A (semantic only) | Fast | Fully convolutional for pixel labeling |
| **Mask R-CNN** | 2017 | ResNet-101 + FPN | **37.1%** | ~5 fps | RoIAlign + parallel mask branch |
| **Mask R-CNN (ResNet-50)** | 2017 | ResNet-50 + FPN | 34.7% | ~8 fps | Lighter backbone variant |
| **YOLACT** | 2019 | ResNet-101 | 29.8% | **33 fps** | Real-time, prototype-based |
| **PointRend** | 2020 | ResNet-50 | 36.2% | Variable | Point-based mask refinement |
| **Mask2Former** | 2021 | Swin-L | **57.8%** | ~4 fps | Unified mask transformer |
| **SAM (Segment Anything)** | 2023 | ViT-H | ~63% (SA-1B) | Prompt-based | Foundation model for segmentation |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Mask R-CNN Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT IMAGE HxWx3] --> B[BACKBONE CNN ResNet 101]
    B --> C[FEATURE MAP H/16 x W/16 x 2048]
    C --> D[RPN REGION PROPOSAL NETWORK]
    D --> E[ROIS REGIONS OF INTEREST]
    C --> F[ROI ALIGN LAYER]
    E --> F
    F --> G[CLASSIFICATION HEAD K plus 1 classes]
    F --> H[BOUNDING BOX REGRESSION HEAD 4 coords]
    F --> I[MASK HEAD FCN 28x28 mask]
    G --> J[FINAL PREDICTIONS class, box, mask]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 RoIAlign vs RoIPool — Precision Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CONTINUOUS ROI COORDINATES] --> B{ROIPOOL or ROIALIGN}
    B --> C[ROIPOOL]
    B --> D[ROIALIGN]
    C --> E[QUANTIZATION ROUNDING TO INTEGER]
    E --> F[MISALIGNMENT 2 to 3 PIXEL ERROR]
    F --> G[FEATURE MAP 7x7]
    D --> H[NO QUANTIZATION]
    H --> I[BILINEAR INTERPOLATION 4 SAMPLE POINTS PER BIN]
    I --> J[FEATURE MAP 7x7 PIXEL ACCURATE]
    G --> K[CLASSIFICATION OK]
    J --> L[CLASSIFICATION plus PIXEL ACCURATE MASKS]
```

## 4.3 Three-Stage Training and Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1 FEATURE EXTRACTION] --> A1[Input image]
    A1 --> A2[ResNet 101 with FPN]
    A2 --> A3[Multi scale feature pyramid P2 to P6]
    A3 --> B[STAGE 2 REGION PROPOSAL]
    B --> B1[Slide RPN over feature maps]
    B1 --> B2[Generate anchor boxes k equals 9 per location]
    B2 --> B3[Objectness scores plus box deltas]
    B3 --> B4[Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    B4 --> B5[Top 2000 RoIs]
    B5 --> C[STAGE 3 PER ROI HEADS]
    C --> C1[RoIAlign on each RoI]
    C1 --> C2[Classification softmax K plus 1]
    C1 --> C3[Bounding box regression 4 values]
    C1 --> C4[Mask prediction FCN sigmoid K x 28 x 28]
    C2 --> D[FINAL OUTPUTS]
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
```

## 4.4 The Three Segmentation Tasks — Conceptual Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT IMAGE WITH MULTIPLE OBJECTS] --> B{TASK TYPE}
    B --> C[SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION]
    B --> D[INSTANCE SEGMENTATION]
    B --> E[PANOPTIC SEGMENTATION]
    C --> C1[All car pixels labelled CAR single color]
    C --> C2[All people pixels labelled PEOPLE single color]
    C --> C3[No instance separation]
    D --> D1[Car 1 gets unique mask and ID 1]
    D --> D2[Car 2 gets unique mask and ID 2]
    D --> D3[Person 1 gets unique mask and ID 3]
    E --> E1[Stuff classes sky road labelled per pixel]
    E --> E2[Thing classes car person have instance IDs]
    E --> E3[Unified output combining both]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mask Head

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ROI FEATURE 14x14x256] --> B[CONV 3x3 256 ReLU]
    B --> C[CONV 3x3 256 ReLU]
    C --> D[DECONV 2x2 stride 2 256 ReLU]
    D --> E[CONV 1x1 K sigmoid]
    E --> F[OUTPUT K x 28 x 28 BINARY MASKS]
    F --> G[SELECT MASK FOR PREDICTED CLASS]
    G --> H[FINAL INSTANCE MASK]
```

## 4.6 Loss Function Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GROUND TRUTH class, box, mask] --> B[COMPUTE THREE LOSSES]
    B --> C[L CLASSIFICATION cross entropy]
    B --> D[L BBOX smooth L1]
    B --> E[L MASK binary cross entropy]
    C --> F[COMBINED LOSS L equals Lcls plus Lbox plus Lmask]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[BACKPROPAGATION]
    G --> H[UPDATE WEIGHTS]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Instance Segmentation. How does it differ from Semantic Segmentation?
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Instance Segmentation** is a computer vision task that detects, classifies, and segments each individual object in an image with a pixel-precise mask, assigning a **unique identifier** to every distinct object instance.

**Key differences from Semantic Segmentation:**

1. **Semantic Segmentation** assigns a class label to every pixel but does **not distinguish between separate instances** of the same class. For example, all overlapping cars in a traffic scene would be labeled "car" with a single color.
2. **Instance Segmentation** goes further — it both classifies each pixel AND separates individual object instances. Each car would get its own distinct mask, even if cars overlap.
3. Semantic segmentation is sufficient for "stuff" classes (sky, road, grass), but instance segmentation is required for "thing" classes (car, person, dog).

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks differences)*

---

### Question 2: What is RoIAlign and why was it introduced in Mask R-CNN?
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RoIAlign** is a spatial pooling operation introduced in Mask R-CNN that extracts **fixed-size feature maps from Regions of Interest (RoIs)** of arbitrary size, **without quantization** (rounding) of the continuous RoI coordinates.

**Why it was introduced:**
1. The previous **RoIPool** operation in Faster R-CNN performed two rounds of quantization (rounding) when mapping RoI coordinates to feature map bins, introducing a misalignment of up to ~2.8 pixels.
2. This misalignment was acceptable for **classification** and bounding box regression (which are coarse), but was **catastrophic for pixel-precise mask prediction**.
3. RoIAlign solves this by sampling **4 regular points per bin** and using **bilinear interpolation** to compute exact feature values, thereby preserving pixel-level spatial accuracy.

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks motivation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **DO NOT** confuse RoIAlign with RoIPool — the quantization distinction is a **favourite 2-mark question**.
> - **DO NOT** say Mask R-CNN uses "softmax" for the mask branch — the correct activation is **sigmoid per-class** (carry 1 mark if missed).
> - **DO NOT** skip drawing the architecture diagram in detection questions — 2–3 marks are reserved for the figure.
> - **DO NOT** forget the three parallel heads in the final stage (classification, box regression, mask).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Mask R-CNN with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive and explain the bounding box regression loss used in Mask R-CNN, including the parameterization of anchor-to-ground-truth transformations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Mask R-CNN is a **two-stage instance segmentation framework** extending Faster R-CNN. Its architecture consists of three sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Backbone (Feature Extraction):**
- A **ResNet-101** (or ResNet-50) CNN with an attached **Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)** processes the input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$.
- The FPN produces a multi-scale feature pyramid $\{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5\}$ with strides $\{4, 8, 16, 32\}$, enabling detection at multiple scales.

**Stage 2 — Region Proposal Network (RPN):**
- A small fully convolutional network slides over each pyramid level.
- At each spatial location, $k = 9$ anchor boxes (3 scales × 3 aspect ratios) are evaluated.
- The RPN outputs **objectness scores** and **bounding-box refinement deltas** for each anchor.
- After **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)**, the top ~2000 proposals are passed to Stage 3.

**Stage 3 — Detection + Mask Heads:**
- Each RoI is processed by **RoIAlign** to produce a fixed $7 \times 7$ feature map (for the detection heads) and a $14 \times 14$ feature map (for the mask head).
- Three parallel heads operate on each RoI:
  1. **Classification head**: Fully-connected → softmax over $K+1$ classes.
  2. **Bounding-box regression head**: Fully-connected → 4 deltas.
  3. **Mask head**: FCN with Conv → Deconv → Conv layers, producing $K$ masks of size $28 \times 28$ (one per class).

**Roles of each component:**
- **Backbone+FPN**: Extracts multi-scale, semantically rich features.
- **RPN**: Generates class-agnostic object proposals efficiently.
- **RoIAlign**: Ensures pixel-accurate feature extraction (critical for masks).
- **Three heads**: Provide classification, localization, and segmentation in parallel.

*(Marks: 3 for diagram + 4 for explanation of components)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

The bounding box regression branch refines an anchor box $a = (a_x, a_y, a_w, a_h)$ into a predicted box $b$.

**Step 1: Decoding Equations** (derived in Section 3.1):

$$
b_x = a_w \cdot t_x + a_x, \quad b_y = a_h \cdot t_y + a_y
$$

$$
b_w = a_w \cdot \exp(t_w), \quad b_h = a_h \cdot \exp(t_h)
$$

The network predicts $t = (t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$, which are then decoded into absolute box coordinates.

**Step 2: Target Encoding** (the inverse transformation):

$$
t_x^* = \frac{g_x - a_x}{a_w}, \quad t_y^* = \frac{g_y - a_y}{a_h}
$$

$$
t_w^* = \log\left(\frac{g_w}{a_w}\right), \quad t_h^* = \log\left(\frac{g_h}{a_h}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Smooth L1 Loss:**

$$
L_{reg}(t_i, t_i^*) = \text{smooth}_{L_1}(t_i - t_i^*)
$$

$$
\text{smooth}_{L_1}(x) = \begin{cases} 0.5x^2 & \text{if } \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \vert x \vert - 0.5 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Reasoning for the design:**
- The **division by anchor width/height** in the center encoding makes the prediction **scale-invariant** — a 10-pixel shift on a small object is as significant as a 100-pixel shift on a large object.
- The **log transform** for width/height ensures that predicting $t_w^* = 0$ corresponds to no change, $t_w^* = 1$ corresponds to doubling the size, and $t_w^* = -1$ corresponds to halving — a symmetric multiplicative encoding.
- The **Smooth L1** loss is **quadratic for small errors** (allowing smooth gradients) and **linear for large errors** (preventing gradient explosion from outliers).

*(Marks: 3 for equations + 2 for target encoding + 2 for Smooth L1 derivation & reasoning)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of RoIAlign. How is it different from RoIPool? Discuss why RoIAlign is essential for pixel-accurate mask prediction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the loss function used in Mask R-CNN. Why is sigmoid (not softmax) used in the mask branch, and why is the mask loss computed per-class? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working of RoIAlign (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1**: Take a continuous RoI defined by $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$ in the original image and project it to the feature map by dividing by the network stride $s$:

$$
(x_1', y_1', x_2', y_2') = \left(\frac{x_1}{s}, \frac{y_1}{s}, \frac{x_2}{s}, \frac{y_2}{s}\right)
$$

**Critical**: **No rounding** — these remain continuous (floating-point) values.

**Step 2**: Divide the projected RoI into $H_{out} \times W_{out}$ bins (e.g., $7 \times 7$ or $14 \times 14$):

$$
\text{bin width} = \frac{x_2' - x_1'}{W_{out}}, \quad \text{bin height} = \frac{y_2' - y_1'}{H_{out}}
$$

**Step 3**: In each bin, sample $k \times k$ regular points (typically $k = 2$, giving 4 points) at the centers of the 4 sub-quadrants of the bin.

**Step 4**: For each sampled sub-pixel location, compute the feature value using **bilinear interpolation** from the 4 nearest integer pixel locations on the feature map.

**Step 5**: Aggregate (max-pool or average-pool) the sampled values within each bin to produce the output feature map of size $H_{out} \times W_{out}$.

**Difference from RoIPool:**

| Aspect | RoIPool | RoIAlign |
|---|---|---|
| Coordinate handling | Quantized to integers | Continuous, no rounding |
| Sampling | 1 point per bin (rounded) | 4 regular points per bin |
| Interpolation | None | Bilinear interpolation |
| Spatial accuracy | Misaligned by ~2.8 px | Pixel-accurate |
| Speed | Slightly faster | Slightly slower |

**Why essential for mask prediction:**
- Mask prediction is a **dense, pixel-level task** requiring precise spatial alignment.
- Even small (~2.8 px) misalignments cause the predicted mask to "smear" beyond the true object boundary, especially for small objects.
- The RoIAlign authors reported a **mask AP improvement of 5.5 percentage points** (from 23.6 to 30.9) over RoIPool — a massive gain attributable entirely to the precision of the pooling operation.

*(Marks: 3 for step-by-step explanation + 2 for diagram + 2 for comparison & justification)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The Multi-Task Loss in Mask R-CNN:**

The total loss is a sum of three components:

$$
L = L_{cls} + L_{box} + L_{mask}
$$

**1. Classification Loss** ($L_{cls}$):
- Standard cross-entropy loss over $K+1$ classes (including background).
- $L_{cls} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K+1} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ where $y$ is the one-hot ground truth.

**2. Bounding Box Loss** ($L_{box}$):
- Smooth L1 loss (as derived in Section 3.1) on the box regression deltas.
- Applied only to positive (foreground) samples.

**3. Mask Loss** ($L_{mask}$):
- Per-pixel **binary cross-entropy**, computed only on the ground-truth class's mask:

$$
L_{mask} = -\frac{1}{m^2} \sum_{1 \leq i,j \leq m} \left[ y_{ij} \log \hat{y}_{ij}^k + (1-y_{ij})\log(1-\hat{y}_{ij}^k) \right]
$$

where $k$ is the ground-truth class and $\hat{y}_{ij}^k$ is the predicted probability at pixel $(i, j)$ for class $k$, with the mask resolution $m = 28$.

**Why sigmoid (not softmax) in the mask branch:**

- The mask branch outputs $K$ separate binary masks (one per class), each with its own **sigmoid** activation.
- Using softmax would force inter-class competition, requiring the network to "split" its representational capacity across all $K$ classes for every RoI — even if only one class is present.
- **Sigmoid decouples** the classes: the network can produce a high-quality mask for class $k$ without being penalized for low responses on other classes' masks.
- This **decoupling empirically yields a 5.5-point AP improvement** on COCO compared to a softmax alternative.

**Why per-class mask loss:**

- The loss is computed **only on the mask corresponding to the ground-truth class**, not on all $K$ masks.
- This avoids the network being penalized for predicting "wrong" masks for classes that aren't present in the RoI.
- It also reinforces the semantic specialization of each of the $K$ output channels.

*(Marks: 2 for the three-component loss + 2 for mask loss equation + 2 for sigmoid justification + 1 for per-class reasoning)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mask R-CNN & Instance Segmentation**

- ✅ **Instance Segmentation** = Object Detection + Semantic Segmentation, with **per-instance pixel-precise masks**.
- ✅ Mask R-CNN has **three parallel heads**: classification, box regression, **mask (FCN)**.
- ✅ **RoIAlign** (not RoIPool) is used — uses **bilinear interpolation** with **4 sample points per bin**.
- ✅ RoIAlign improves mask AP by **~5.5 points** over RoIPool by eliminating quantization.
- ✅ The mask branch outputs **$K$ separate $28 \times 28$ binary masks**, one per class.
- ✅ Final mask activation is **sigmoid per-class** (not softmax) — this is a key empirical design choice.
- ✅ Mask loss is **per-pixel binary cross-entropy**, computed **only on the ground-truth class's mask**.
- ✅ Total loss: $L = L_{cls} + L_{box} + L_{mask}$ — simple additive multi-task loss.
- ✅ Bounding box regression uses **Smooth L1 loss** on scale-invariant deltas $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$.
- ✅ **Anchors** are pre-defined boxes with 3 scales × 3 aspect ratios = 9 anchors per spatial location.
- ✅ **IoU thresholds**: $\geq 0.7$ → foreground, $\leq 0.3$ → background, between → ignored during RPN training.
- ✅ **RPN** is class-agnostic — it only proposes "object vs background" + box deltas.
- ✅ **FPN** is used as the backbone to enable multi-scale object detection (P2–P6).
- ✅ **mAP@[0.5:0.95]** is the standard COCO metric — averaged over 10 IoU thresholds.
- ✅ Mask R-CNN achieves **37.1% mask AP** on COCO test-dev with ResNet-101 + FPN at ~5 fps.
- ✅ Three segmentation paradigms: **Semantic** (per-pixel class), **Instance** (per-instance mask+ID), **Panoptic** (both combined).
- ✅ **Key advantage of decoupled mask+class**: The mask branch does not need to "win" a competition against other classes; each channel specializes.
- ✅ Modern successors: **Mask2Former** (transformer-based, 57.8% AP), **SAM** (foundation model), **YOLACT** (real-time).
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: Autonomous driving, medical imaging, AR/VR, agriculture, industrial inspection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: U-Net and Semantic Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** U-Net and Semantic Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Semantic Segmentation — Formal Academic Definition

**Semantic Segmentation** is a fundamental computer vision task that involves classifying **every individual pixel** in an image into one of several predefined categorical classes. Unlike image classification (which assigns one label to the whole image) or object detection (which draws bounding boxes), semantic segmentation produces a dense, pixel-wise prediction map of the same spatial resolution as the input image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):** Semantic segmentation is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple segments (sets of pixels), known as image segments, by assigning a class label to every pixel in the image such that pixels with the same label share certain visual characteristics.

Mathematically, given an input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ and a set of $C$ classes, semantic segmentation learns a function:

$$f: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$$

where the output is a tensor of shape $H \times W \times C$, and each spatial location $(i,j)$ contains a probability distribution over the $C$ classes. The final segmentation map is obtained via $\arg\max$ along the channel dimension.

## 1.2 Types of Pixel-Level Segmentation

| Segmentation Type | Output | Distinguishes Instances? |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Class label per pixel | ❌ No |
| **Instance Segmentation** | Class + instance ID per pixel | ✅ Yes |
| **Panoptic Segmentation** | Unified class + instance map | ✅ Yes |

## 1.3 U-Net — The Architect's Definition

**U-Net** is a symmetric **encoder–decoder Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** architecture introduced by Ronneberger et al. (2015) for biomedical image segmentation. Its name derives from the **"U" shape** of its architecture diagram. The defining feature of U-Net is the use of **skip connections** that directly concatenate feature maps from the encoder (contracting path) to the decoder (expansive path) at corresponding resolution levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "U"-shaped?** The architecture symmetrically contracts the spatial dimensions (encoder) and then expands them back (decoder), forming a U. The skip connections form the horizontal bars of the U.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### 🍕 The Pizza Map Analogy (Semantic Segmentation)

Imagine you take a photo of a city street and want a robot to understand it. Image classification is like saying *"This is a street."* Object detection is like drawing rectangles: *"There's a car here, a pedestrian there."* **Semantic segmentation** is like **coloring every single dot in the picture** — every pixel is labeled. Pixels belonging to cars are colored red, road pixels blue, sky pixels light blue, and pedestrian pixels yellow. The robot now sees a **color-coded map** of the world, pixel by pixel.

### 🏥 The Doctor's MRI Analogy (U-Net)

Imagine a radiologist examining an MRI scan looking for tumors. The brain MRI is enormous and complex. U-Net works in two phases:
- **The Contraction Path (Left side of U):** Asks *"What is this image?"* — captures the **context** (the big picture: "this looks like brain tissue") by progressively shrinking the image but extracting more abstract features.
- **The Expansion Path (Right side of U):** Asks *"Where exactly is the tumor?"* — recovers the **localization** (precise pixel coordinates) by upsampling.
- **The Skip Connections (Horizontal connections):** Like a doctor cross-referencing the original MRI scan alongside the analysis, U-Net copies fine-grained details from the encoder to the decoder so that **no small tumor is lost** during downsampling.

## 1.5 Critical Standard Metrics (KTU Board Highlights)

The following standard metrics are **bold** essentials for KTU examinations:

- **Pixel Accuracy (PA):** $\frac{\text{Correct Pixels}}{\text{Total Pixels}}$
- **Mean Intersection over Union (mIoU):** Average IoU across all classes
- **Dice Coefficient (F1-Score):** Harmonic mean of precision and recall for segmentation
- **Mean Average Precision (mAP):** Used in instance segmentation

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked *"compare semantic segmentation with image classification"*, always mention that semantic segmentation is a **dense prediction task** producing per-pixel class labels, while classification produces a single label for the whole image.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The U-Net Architecture — Component-by-Component Breakdown

U-Net consists of **three main structural blocks**:

### A. The Contracting Path (Encoder / Left Arm of U)

**Purpose:** Capture **context** and extract hierarchical features.

- Repeated application of two unpadded $3 \times 3$ convolutions
- Each followed by a **ReLU activation**
- A $2 \times 2$ **max pooling** operation with stride 2 for downsampling
- **Doubles the number of feature channels** at each downsampling step

Typical channel progression: $64 \rightarrow 128 \rightarrow 256 \rightarrow 512 \rightarrow 1024$

### B. The Bottleneck (Bottom of U)

**Purpose:** Process the most compressed, semantically rich representation.

- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions + ReLU
- Channel depth is at its maximum (typically **1024**)
- No pooling/upsampling here

### C. The Expansive Path (Decoder / Right Arm of U)

**Purpose:** Enable **precise localization** via upsampling.

- $2 \times 2$ **transposed convolution** (also called "up-convolution") that **halves** the number of feature channels
- **Concatenation** with the corresponding cropped feature map from the contracting path (skip connection)
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions + ReLU

Typical channel progression (decoder): $1024 \rightarrow 512 \rightarrow 256 \rightarrow 128 \rightarrow 64$

### D. The Skip Connections (Horizontal Arrows in U)

**Purpose:** Combine **low-level spatial details** from encoder with **high-level semantic features** from decoder.

- Concatenation (not addition) along the channel axis
- Mitigates the loss of spatial information caused by pooling
- Enables **multi-scale feature fusion**

## 2.2 Why Skip Connections Are Crucial — The "Why" and "How"

| Problem in Pure Encoder–Decoder | How Skip Connections Solve It |
|---|---|
| Pooling layers **lose spatial precision** | Skip connections **re-inject fine spatial details** from earlier layers |
| Bottleneck contains only **global context** | Skip connections provide **local detail** to balance |
| Gradient vanishing in deep networks | Skip paths create **shortcuts for gradient flow** (similar to ResNet) |

## 2.3 Pixel-wise Loss Functions for U-Net

### 1. Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy Loss

The most common loss for semantic segmentation:

$$L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \cdot \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$

where:
- $N$ = total number of pixels
- $C$ = number of classes
- $y_{i,c}$ = ground-truth one-hot label
- $\hat{y}_{i,c}$ = predicted probability

### 2. Dice Loss (Especially for Class Imbalance)

The **Dice Coefficient** measures overlap between prediction and ground truth:

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

The Dice Loss is:

$$L_{Dice} = 1 - D = 1 - \frac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert + \epsilon}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert + \epsilon}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small smoothing constant to prevent division by zero.

### 3. Combined Loss (Common in Practice)

$$L_{Total} = \alpha \cdot L_{CE} + \beta \cdot L_{Dice}$$

## 2.4 Evaluation Metrics

### Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index)

$$IoU = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$$

### Mean IoU (mIoU) — KTU Favorite Metric

$$mIoU = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}$$

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Description | Range / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $f: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ | Semantic segmentation mapping function | $H,W$ in pixels, $C$ = number of classes |
| $L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i} \sum_{c} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$ | Pixel-wise cross-entropy loss | $[0, \infty)$ |
| $D = \frac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$ | Dice coefficient (F1 for segmentation) | $[0, 1]$ |
| $IoU = \frac{TP}{TP+FP+FN}$ | Intersection over Union | $[0, 1]$ |
| $mIoU = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} IoU_c$ | Mean Intersection over Union | $[0, 1]$ |
| $PA = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Pixel accuracy | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\text{Channel}_i = 64 \times 2^{i-1}$ | U-Net channel progression (level $i$) | $i \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Always use `\vert` for absolute value in formulas to avoid breaking markdown tables. Note how the table above uses `\vert P \cap G \vert` instead of `|P ∩ G|`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor segmentation in MRI/CT scans, cell nuclei detection |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Road, lane, pedestrian, and vehicle pixel-level detection |
| **Satellite Imagery** | Land cover classification, building footprint extraction |
| **Agriculture** | Crop vs. weed segmentation, disease region detection |
| **Industrial QC** | Defect pixel detection on manufactured surfaces |
| **AR/VR** | Real-time scene understanding and object boundary detection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Softmax to Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy

### Step 1 — Raw Network Output (Logits)

The final $1 \times 1$ convolution of U-Net produces raw logits $z_i^{(c)}$ for pixel $i$ and class $c$. The **Softmax** function converts these to probabilities:

$$\hat{y}_{i,c} = \frac{e^{z_i^{(c)}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_i^{(k)}}}$$

### Step 2 — Probability of Correct Class

For a single pixel $i$ with true class $c^*$, the predicted probability of the correct class is:

$$P(\text{correct} \mid \text{pixel } i) = \hat{y}_{i,c^*} = \frac{e^{z_i^{(c^*)}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_i^{(k)}}}$$

### Step 3 — Log-Likelihood for One Pixel

Taking the negative log-likelihood:

$$\ell_i = -\log\left(\hat{y}_{i,c^*}\right) = -\log\left(\frac{e^{z_i^{(c^*)}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_i^{(k)}}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Logarithm Rules

$$\ell_i = -z_i^{(c^*)} + \log\left(\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_i^{(k)}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Average Over All Pixels

$$L_{CE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ -z_i^{(c^*)} + \log\left(\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_i^{(k)}}\right) \right]$$

> **Conversion logic explanation:** The $z_i^{(c^*)}$ term rewards correct predictions with higher scores, while the $\log \sum e^{z_i^{(k)}}$ term acts as a normalization constant (log-partition function) ensuring the loss is minimized only when the model assigns high probability to the true class.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Dice Coefficient

### Step 1 — Define the Sets

Let:
- $P$ = set of pixels predicted as positive (foreground)
- $G$ = set of ground-truth positive pixels

### Step 2 — Express in Terms of Pixel Counts

Using the standard confusion matrix:
- $\vert P \vert = TP + FP$ (all predicted positive)
- $\vert G \vert = TP + FN$ (all actually positive)
- $\vert P \cap G \vert = TP$ (correctly predicted)

### Step 3 — Substitute into Dice Formula

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{(TP+FP) + (TP+FN)}$$

### Step 4 — Final Simplification

$$\boxed{D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}$$

This shows Dice is mathematically equivalent to the **F1-score** for binary classification.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Computing IoU and Dice

**Problem:** A U-Net is evaluated on a binary segmentation task (tumor vs. background) for a single image. The following confusion matrix is obtained:

| | Predicted Tumor | Predicted Background |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Tumor** | $TP = 80$ | $FN = 20$ |
| **Actual Background** | $FP = 10$ | $TN = 890$ |

**Step 1 — Compute IoU:**

$$IoU = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 10 + 20} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.7273$$

**Step 2 — Compute Dice Coefficient:**

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 80}{2 \cdot 80 + 10 + 20} = \frac{160}{180} = 0.8889$$

**Step 3 — Compute Pixel Accuracy:**

$$PA = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 890}{80 + 890 + 10 + 20} = \frac{970}{1000} = 0.97$$

**Step 4 — Mean IoU (mIoU) for 2 classes:**

For class "Tumor":
$$IoU_{tumor} = \frac{80}{80+10+20} = 0.7273$$

For class "Background":
$$IoU_{bg} = \frac{890}{890+20+10} = 0.9457$$

$$mIoU = \frac{1}{2}(0.7273 + 0.9457) = 0.8365$$

> **Conversion logic:** Notice that pixel accuracy (97%) is misleadingly high because the background dominates. The IoU for the tumor class (72.73%) gives a more honest picture of model performance — this is exactly **why mIoU is the KTU-preferred metric** over plain accuracy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: U-Net vs. Other Segmentation Architectures

| Property | U-Net | FCN (Fully Convolutional Network) | SegNet | DeepLab |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Symmetric encoder-decoder | Encoder + 1x1 conv | Encoder-decoder (VGG-based) | Encoder + Atrous conv + ASPP |
| **Skip Connections** | Concatenation (multi-scale) | Summation (FPN-style) | Pooling indices (no copying) | Atrous spatial pyramid pooling |
| **Input Resolution** | Fixed (e.g., 512×512) | Variable | Variable | Variable |
| **Original Domain** | Biomedical imaging | Generic PASCAL VOC | Autonomous driving | Generic |
| **Parameters** | ~31M (original) | ~134M (FCN-8s) | ~29M | ~41M (DeepLabv3+) |
| **Key Strength** | Precise localization with few training samples | Simple, end-to-end trainable | Memory efficient | Multi-scale context via atrous conv |
| **Loss Function** | Pixel-wise CE / Dice | Pixel-wise CE | Pixel-wise CE | CE + CRF post-processing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 U-Net Architecture — Block-Level Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input["INPUT IMAGE\nH x W x 3"]
    
    subgraph Encoder["ENCODER (Contracting Path)"]
        direction TB
        E1["Conv 3x3 + ReLU\nConv 3x3 + ReLU\n64 channels\nH x W x 64"]
        P1["MaxPool 2x2 (stride 2)\nH/2 x W/2 x 64"]
        E2["Conv 3x3 + ReLU\nConv 3x3 + ReLU\n128 channels\nH/2 x W/2 x 128"]
        P2["MaxPool 2x2 (stride 2)\nH/4 x W/4 x 128"]
        E3["Conv 3x3 + ReLU\nConv 3x3 + ReLU\n256 channels\nH/4 x W/4 x 256"]
        P3["MaxPool 2x2 (stride 2)\nH/8 x W/8 x 256"]
        E4["Conv 3x3 + ReLU\nConv 3x3 + ReLU\n512 channels\nH/8 x W/8 x 512"]
        P4["MaxPool 2x2 (stride 2)\nH/16 x W/16 x 512"]
    end
    
    subgraph Bottleneck["BOTTLENECK"]
        B["Conv 3x3 + ReLU\nConv 3x3 + ReLU\n1024 channels\nH/16 x W/16 x 1024"]
    end
    
    subgraph Decoder["DECODER (Expansive Path)"]
        direction TB
        U1["Up-Conv 2x2\n512 channels\nH/8 x W/8 x 1024"]
        D1["Concatenate with E4\nConv 3x3 + ReLU x2\n512 channels"]
        U2["Up-Conv 2x2\n256 channels\nH/4 x W/4 x 512"]
        D2["Concatenate with E3\nConv 3x3 + ReLU x2\n256 channels"]
        U3["Up-Conv 2x2\n128 channels\nH/2 x W/2 x 256"]
        D3["Concatenate with E2\nConv 3x3 + ReLU x2\n128 channels"]
        U4["Up-Conv 2x2\n64 channels\nH x W x 128"]
        D4["Concatenate with E1\nConv 3x3 + ReLU x2\n64 channels"]
    end
    
    OutputLayer["1x1 Conv (C classes)\nSoftmax\nH x W x C"]
    Mask["OUTPUT SEGMENTATION MAP\nH x W x 1 (per class)"]
    
    Input --> E1 --> P1 --> E2 --> P2 --> E3 --> P3 --> E4 --> P4 --> B
    B --> U1
    E4 -.->|"Skip Connection\n(Concatenate)"| U1
    U1 --> D1
    E3 -.->|"Skip Connection"| D1
    D1 --> U2
    E2 -.->|"Skip Connection"| U2
    U2 --> D2
    E2 -.->|"Skip Connection"| D2
    D2 --> U3
    E1 -.->|"Skip Connection"| U3
    U3 --> D3
    E1 -.->|"Skip Connection"| D3
    D3 --> U4
    U4 --> D4
    D4 --> OutputLayer --> Mask
    
    style Input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style Mask fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style Bottleneck fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style OutputLayer fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Semantic Segmentation Pipeline — End-to-End Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Input\nImage\nH x W x 3"] --> B["Preprocessing\nResize / Normalize\nData Augmentation\nH x W x 3"]
    B --> C["Encoder Backbone\nCNN Feature Extractor\nConv + ReLU + Pool\nH/16 x W/16 x 1024"]
    C --> D["Bottleneck\nDeep Semantic Features\nH/16 x W/16 x 1024"]
    D --> E["Decoder\nTransposed Conv + Skip\nH x W x 64"]
    E --> F["Final 1x1 Conv\nClass Score per Pixel\nH x W x C"]
    F --> G["Softmax\nProbability Map\nH x W x C"]
    G --> H["Argmax\nClass Label per Pixel\nH x W x 1"]
    H --> I["Segmentation\nMask\nH x W"]
    I --> J["Post-Processing\nCRF / Morphology\nH x W"]
    J --> K["Final Output\nColor-coded Map\nH x W x 3"]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Skip Connection Mechanism — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EncoderSide["ENCODER SIDE (Low-level features)"]
        EncFeat["Feature Map\nH/8 x W/8 x 512\nContains: edges, textures, fine details"]
    end
    
    subgraph DecoderSide["DECODER SIDE (High-level features)"]
        DecFeat["Upsampled Map\nH/8 x W/8 x 512\nContains: object parts, semantics"]
    end
    
    Concat["Concatenation\nalong channel axis"]
    Merged["Merged Feature Map\nH/8 x W/8 x 1024\nContains: details + semantics"]
    ConvProcess["3x3 Conv + ReLU\nLearn how to fuse\nH/8 x W/8 x 512"]
    
    EncFeat -->|"Copy\n(identity)"| Concat
    DecFeat --> Concat
    Concat --> Merged
    Merged --> ConvProcess
    ConvProcess --> NextLayer["Next Decoder Block\nH/4 x W/4 x 256"]
    
    style EncFeat fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style DecFeat fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Merged fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style ConvProcess fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Loss Computation Flow for U-Net Training

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input["Input Image\nH x W x 3"] --> Net["U-Net\nForward Pass"]
    Net --> Pred["Predicted Logits\nH x W x C"]
    GT["Ground Truth Mask\nH x W x 1"] --> OneHot["One-Hot Encode\nH x W x C"]
    OneHot --> CEL["Cross-Entropy Loss\nL_CE"]
    Pred --> CEL
    Pred --> Softmax["Softmax"]
    Softmax --> Dice["Dice Loss\nL_Dice"]
    OneHot --> Dice
    CEL --> Combine["L_total = alpha L_CE + beta L_Dice"]
    Dice --> Combine
    Combine --> Backprop["Backpropagation\nUpdate Weights"]
    Backprop --> Net
    
    style Input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style GT fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Pred fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Combine fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style Backprop fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing U-Net in exams, always label the **left side as "Contracting Path"** and **right side as "Expansive Path"**, and explicitly **draw the skip connections as horizontal arrows** with the word "copy" or "concatenate" on them.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between semantic segmentation, instance segmentation, and image classification. Give one real-world application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Task | Output | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Image Classification** | Single class label for entire image | Spam email detection: "spam" or "not spam" |
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Class label for **each pixel** (no instance distinction) | **Autonomous driving:** labeling road, sky, pedestrian pixels |
| **Instance Segmentation** | Class label + unique instance ID for each pixel | **Medical imaging:** counting and outlining individual cell nuclei |

**Key differentiator:** Image classification gives one label per image; semantic segmentation gives one label per pixel; instance segmentation further separates objects of the same class (e.g., distinguishing car-1 from car-2).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is a skip connection in U-Net? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **skip connection** in U-Net is a direct pathway that copies (concatenates) feature maps from the encoder (contracting path) to the corresponding decoder (expansive path) at the same spatial resolution level.

**Importance:**
1. **Preserves spatial information** that would otherwise be lost during max-pooling operations
2. **Combines low-level features (edges, textures)** with **high-level semantic features (object parts)**
3. **Enables precise pixel-level localization**, which is critical for segmentation accuracy
4. **Helps gradient flow** during backpropagation, mitigating vanishing gradients

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🎯 Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of U-Net in detail with a neat labeled diagram. Describe the role of the contracting path, expansive path, bottleneck, and skip connections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A binary segmentation model produces the following confusion matrix on a test image:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | $TP = 90$ | $FN = 10$ |
| **Actual Negative** | $FP = 15$ | $TN = 185$ |

Compute the **Pixel Accuracy**, **IoU (Jaccard Index)**, and **Dice Coefficient (F1-Score)**. Comment on which metric is most reliable for imbalanced segmentation. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — U-Net Architecture (7 marks)

**1. Overview (1 mark):**
U-Net is a symmetric encoder–decoder CNN proposed by Ronneberger et al. (2015) for biomedical image segmentation. It is named for its U-shaped architecture.

**2. Contracting Path / Encoder (2 marks):**
- Repeated blocks of two $3 \times 3$ **convolutions** (unpadded), each followed by a **ReLU** activation
- A $2 \times 2$ **max-pooling** operation with stride 2 for downsampling
- After each pooling, the number of feature channels is **doubled**: $64 \rightarrow 128 \rightarrow 256 \rightarrow 512$
- **Role:** Captures the *context* (what is in the image) by progressively extracting higher-level, more abstract features while reducing spatial resolution

**3. Bottleneck (1 mark):**
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with ReLU at the bottom
- Channel depth reaches maximum (typically **1024**)
- **Role:** Processes the most compressed semantic representation

**4. Expansive Path / Decoder (2 marks):**
- $2 \times 2$ **transposed convolution (up-convolution)** that halves the number of feature channels
- **Concatenation** with the corresponding feature map from the encoder (skip connection)
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions + ReLU
- Channel progression: $1024 \rightarrow 512 \rightarrow 256 \rightarrow 128 \rightarrow 64$
- **Role:** Enables *precise localization* (where each object is) by progressively recovering spatial resolution

**5. Skip Connections (1 mark):**
- Direct feature map concatenation from encoder to decoder at the same resolution
- **Role:** Combines low-level spatial details (edges, fine textures) with high-level semantic information, preventing the loss of localization accuracy during pooling

**ASCII Diagram (1 mark, as backup):**

```
Input -> [Conv+Pool] -> [Conv+Pool] -> [Conv+Pool] -> [Conv+Pool] -> Bottleneck
            |                |                |                |
            +----------------+----------------+----------------+  (skip)
            v                v                v                v
         [UpConv]         [UpConv]         [UpConv]         [UpConv]
            ->              ->              ->              -> Output
```

---

#### Part (b) — Metric Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $TP = 90$, $FN = 10$, $FP = 15$, $TN = 185$, Total = $TP+TN+FP+FN = 300$

**Step 1 — Pixel Accuracy (2 marks):**

$$PA = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{90 + 185}{300} = \frac{275}{300} = 0.9167 = 91.67\%$$

**Step 2 — IoU / Jaccard Index (2 marks):**

$$IoU = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 15 + 10} = \frac{90}{115} = 0.7826 = 78.26\%$$

**Step 3 — Dice Coefficient (2 marks):**

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 90}{2 \cdot 90 + 15 + 10} = \frac{180}{205} = 0.8780 = 87.80\%$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Imbalance (1 mark):**

In this dataset, the negative class (background) has 200 pixels while the positive class (foreground) has only 100 pixels. Pixel accuracy (91.67%) is **misleadingly high** because the model can score well by simply predicting "background" for most pixels. **IoU and Dice** are more reliable for imbalanced segmentation as they focus on the foreground class performance.

---

### 🎯 Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` — *Alternative Choice*
**Q: (a)** What is semantic segmentation? Explain the encoder–decoder paradigm used in modern segmentation networks. How does U-Net improve upon a plain encoder–decoder without skip connections? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Dice Loss** and **Cross-Entropy Loss** for segmentation. Derive the Dice coefficient formula from first principles and explain why Dice loss is preferred over cross-entropy when there is severe class imbalance. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Semantic Segmentation and Encoder–Decoder (7 marks)

**1. Semantic Segmentation Definition (2 marks):**
Semantic segmentation is a dense prediction task in computer vision where every pixel in an input image is classified into one of $C$ predefined categories. The output is a segmentation map $S \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}^{H \times W}$ of the same spatial size as the input.

**2. Encoder–Decoder Paradigm (3 marks):**

| Stage | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoder** | Series of conv + ReLU + max-pool | Extract hierarchical semantic features, reduce spatial dimensions |
| **Bottleneck** | Deep conv layers at lowest resolution | Capture global context |
| **Decoder** | Transposed conv / upsampling | Recover spatial resolution to match input size |

The encoder progressively compresses the image into a low-resolution, high-semantic feature representation. The decoder progressively expands it back to a full-resolution prediction map. Final $1 \times 1$ convolution + softmax produces per-pixel class probabilities.

**3. U-Net's Improvement over Plain Encoder–Decoder (2 marks):**

A plain encoder–decoder loses fine spatial details during pooling operations. U-Net addresses this through **skip connections** that:
- Copy feature maps from encoder to decoder at matching resolutions
- Concatenate (not add) them, doubling the channel count
- Allow the decoder to learn how to fuse low-level spatial details with high-level semantic context

This results in **sharper boundary delineation** and **better localization of small objects**, which is critical in medical imaging and similar domains.

---

#### Part (b) — Loss Functions and Dice Derivation (7 marks)

**1. Cross-Entropy Loss (2 marks):**

$$L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$

This computes the average negative log-probability of the correct class across all $N$ pixels and $C$ classes. It is computed **pixel-wise** and treats each pixel independently.

**2. Dice Coefficient Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting with the geometric Dice-Sørensen coefficient:

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$$

Expressing in terms of the confusion matrix:
- $\vert P \cap G \vert = TP$ (intersection = true positives)
- $\vert P \vert = TP + FP$ (all predicted positive)
- $\vert G \vert = TP + FN$ (all actually positive)

Substituting:

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{(TP + FP) + (TP + FN)} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

The Dice Loss is:

$$L_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \cdot TP + \epsilon}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN + \epsilon}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small smoothing factor (e.g., $10^{-6}$).

**3. Why Dice Loss is Preferred for Imbalance (2 marks):**

When the foreground (e.g., tumor) occupies only 5% of pixels, cross-entropy is dominated by the easy-to-classify background. The model can achieve 95% CE loss reduction by predicting "background" everywhere. Dice loss, however, is a **region-based metric** that directly optimizes the overlap between prediction and ground truth, making it **invariant to class imbalance**. It gives equal weight to foreground and background regions, forcing the model to learn the small but critical structures.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Pixel Accuracy with mIoU** — Pixel accuracy is inflated on imbalanced datasets. Always report mIoU for segmentation tasks. (Loses 1–2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Drawing U-Net upside down or without skip connections** — The skip connections are the *defining* feature of U-Net. A diagram without horizontal skip arrows is incomplete. (Loses 2 marks)
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the channel doubling** in the encoder and channel halving in the decoder — This is a frequently tested detail. (Loses 1 mark)
> 
> 4. **Using `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** — Always use `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid table breaking.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Dice with IoU formulas** — They are related but different! Dice = $\frac{2 TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$ while IoU = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$. (Loses 1 mark if swapped)
> 
> 6. **Not explaining WHY skip connections exist** — Just saying "they copy features" is incomplete. You must say *they preserve spatial details lost in pooling*. (Loses 1 mark)
> 
> 7. **Forgetting the final $1 \times 1$ convolution** in U-Net that maps 64 channels to C class channels — Many students miss this final layer.

---

## 5.4 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- ☐ **Semantic Segmentation** = pixel-wise classification; output is $H \times W \times C$ tensor
- ☐ **U-Net** = symmetric encoder–decoder with skip connections, originally for biomedical imaging
- ☐ **Three paths:** Contracting (encoder), Bottleneck, Expansive (decoder)
- ☐ **Skip connections** = direct concatenation of encoder feature maps to decoder at matching resolution

### ✅ Architecture Details
- ☐ **Encoder:** Two $3 \times 3$ convs + ReLU + $2 \times 2$ max-pool (stride 2); channels **double** at each level
- ☐ **Bottleneck:** Two $3 \times 3$ convs + ReLU; maximum channels (**1024**)
- ☐ **Decoder:** $2 \times 2$ transposed conv (halves channels) + concat with skip + two $3 \times 3$ convs
- ☐ **Final layer:** $1 \times 1$ convolution mapping 64 channels to $C$ class channels, followed by softmax

### ✅ Channel Progression (Memorize!)
- ☐ $64 \rightarrow 128 \rightarrow 256 \rightarrow 512 \rightarrow 1024$ (encoder)
- ☐ $1024 \rightarrow 512 \rightarrow 256 \rightarrow 128 \rightarrow 64$ (decoder)

### ✅ Key Formulas
- ☐ **Pixel-wise CE Loss:** $L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_i \sum_c y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$
- ☐ **Dice Coefficient:** $D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$
- ☐ **IoU (Jaccard):** $IoU = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$
- ☐ **mIoU:** average of per-class IoU values
- ☐ **Pixel Accuracy:** $PA = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$

### ✅ Comparison Points
- ☐ Semantic vs. Instance vs. Panoptic segmentation
- ☐ U-Net vs. FCN vs. SegNet vs. DeepLab
- ☐ Dice Loss vs. Cross-Entropy Loss (use Dice for imbalance)
- ☐ Skip connections in U-Net vs. ResNet (concatenation vs. addition)

### ✅ Why U-Net Works
- ☐ Encoder captures **context** (what)
- ☐ Decoder captures **localization** (where)
- ☐ Skip connections fuse both → **precise boundaries**
- ☐ Works well with **few training samples** (data augmentation in original paper)

> **🎓 Final Exam Tip:** If asked *"Explain U-Net"*, always structure your answer as: (1) What is it, (2) Draw the U-diagram with skip connections, (3) Explain each path, (4) State why skip connections matter, (5) Mention loss function and metrics. This 5-point structure is the KTU board-expected format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Quality Metrics - A Case Study to Compare Performance of Various Models on a Suitable Dataset - Suggestion on Project Topics - Real Life Problems in the Domain of Computer Vision May Be Identified

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Model Quality Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Quality Metrics** (also termed **Performance Evaluation Metrics** or **Goodness-of-Fit Measures**) are quantitative mathematical instruments used to objectively measure how accurately, robustly, and reliably a trained computer vision model maps inputs to desired outputs against a ground-truth benchmark.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PBCMT504**, these metrics are the *yardsticks* that allow an engineer to numerically compare two competing vision models (e.g., a CNN vs. a Vision Transformer) on the *same* dataset, eliminating subjective bias. They are the bridge between a *trained model artifact* and a *production-ready deployment decision*.

Formally, for a trained model $f_\theta$ parameterized by weights $\theta$, evaluated on a test set $\mathcal{D}_{test} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, a metric $M$ is defined as:

$$M(f_\theta, \mathcal{D}_{test}) = \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}_i, y_i) \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{y}_i = f_\theta(x_i)$$

Here, $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss or scoring function that quantifies deviation between predicted output $\hat{y}_i$ and ground truth $y_i$.

### The Three Pillars of CV Evaluation

| Pillar | Purpose | Example Metric |
|:------:|:-------:|:--------------:|
| **Classification Quality** | Did the model label the image correctly? | **Accuracy**, **F1-Score** |
| **Localization Quality** | Did the model place the bounding box/mask in the right place? | **IoU**, **Dice Coefficient** |
| **Reconstruction Quality** | How close is the generated/denoised image to the original? | **PSNR**, **SSIM** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Inspector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Restaurant Health Inspector**
> Imagine you are a food critic who must grade 5 new restaurants. You cannot just say "Restaurant A is good." You need *measurable criteria*:
> - **Taste** (is the food delicious?) → Analogue to **Accuracy**
> - **Freshness** (is the food safe?) → Analogue to **Precision** (no false alarms)
> - **Value** (do you get what you pay for?) → Analogue to **Recall** (catching all true positives)
> - **Overall Experience** (a balanced view) → Analogue to **F1-Score**
> - **Speed of Service** (responsiveness) → Analogue to **Inference Latency (FPS)**
>
> Just like a restaurant, a CV model is judged on **multiple dimensions** — one number is never enough. A model can be 99% accurate but take 10 minutes per image, making it useless for autonomous driving.

## 1.3 Why Quality Metrics Matter in Computer Vision

1. **Model Selection**: Choosing the best model from a pool of candidates (e.g., ResNet50 vs. EfficientNet vs. ViT).
2. **Hyperparameter Tuning**: Validating whether changing learning rate, batch size, or augmentation strategies improves performance.
3. **Deployment Readiness**: Confirming a model meets production constraints (latency, memory, accuracy threshold).
4. **Regulatory Compliance**: Critical in medical imaging (FDA) and autonomous vehicles (ISO 26262) where minimum performance thresholds are legally mandated.
5. **Research Reproducibility**: Allowing peer-reviewers and the community to validate published results on standard datasets like **COCO**, **ImageNet**, **Cityscapes**, and **PASCAL VOC**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The module explicitly mandates students to *identify real-life problems* and *suggest project topics* — quality metrics are the *scientific evidence* that supports whether your identified problem has a viable CV solution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — The Atom of Classification Metrics

Every classification metric is derived from a **Confusion Matrix**, a 2×2 (binary) or N×N (multiclass) table tallying correct and incorrect predictions.

For binary classification, four outcomes exist:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| **Actual Positive** | **True Positive (TP)** | **False Negative (FN)** |
| **Actual Negative** | **False Positive (FP)** | **True Negative (TN)** |

### Operational Breakdown of Each Cell

- **TP (True Positive)**: Model says *"cat"*, ground truth is *"cat"*. ✅ *The model caught a real cat.*
- **TN (True Negative)**: Model says *"not cat"*, ground truth is *"not cat"*. ✅ *The model correctly ignored a dog.*
- **FP (False Positive)** — *Type I Error*: Model says *"cat"*, ground truth is *"not cat"*. ❌ *The model hallucinated a cat — a "false alarm."*
- **FN (False Negative)** — *Type II Error*: Model says *"not cat"*, ground truth is *"cat"*. ❌ *The model missed a real cat — a "blind spot."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Asymmetry**: In medical CV (e.g., tumor detection), missing a tumor (FN) is far worse than a false alarm (FP) — hence **Recall** is prioritized. In spam detection, the opposite is true — hence **Precision** is prioritized. This is the *engineering judgment* a CV engineer must make.

## 2.2 Classification Metrics — The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict LaTeX Isolation Rule**: All display equations below are separated by exactly one blank line from surrounding text. The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` inside any tabular content to preserve markdown integrity.

### Primary Scalar Metrics

**Accuracy** — The simplest metric, fraction of all correct predictions:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Precision** (Positive Predictive Value) — Of all *predicted positives*, how many are truly positive:

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall** (Sensitivity, True Positive Rate) — Of all *actual positives*, how many did we catch:

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall (penalizes imbalance):

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Specificity** (True Negative Rate) — Complement of FPR:

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

### Aggregate and Threshold-Based Metrics

**F-Beta Score** — Generalized F1 that weights Recall β times more than Precision:

$$F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

**ROC-AUC** — Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve; plots TPR vs. FPR at varying thresholds:

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \qquad FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

**Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** — Robust single-number summary for imbalanced data:

$$MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

**Cohen's Kappa** — Accuracy normalized by chance agreement:

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$$

where $p_o$ is observed agreement and $p_e$ is expected agreement by chance.

### KTU Cheat-Sheet Table (Markdown-Safe Pipes)

| Metric | Formula Symbol | Range | Best For | Pitfall |
|:------:|:--------------:|:-----:|:--------:|:-------:|
| Accuracy | $Acc$ | [0, 1] | Balanced datasets | Fails on imbalanced data |
| Precision | $P$ | [0, 1] | Cost of FP is high | Ignores FN |
| Recall | $R$ | [0, 1] | Cost of FN is high | Ignores FP |
| F1-Score | $F_1$ | [0, 1] | Imbalanced binary | Bias toward equal P & R |
| ROC-AUC | $A_{ROC}$ | [0, 1] | Threshold-independent comparison | Bad for heavy imbalance |
| MCC | $MCC$ | [-1, 1] | Highly imbalanced data | Less interpretable |
| Cohen's Kappa | $\kappa$ | [-1, 1] | Multi-rater agreement | Affected by prevalence |

## 2.3 Localization and Segmentation Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index)** — The single most important metric in object detection and semantic segmentation. It measures the overlap between predicted region $A$ and ground-truth region $B$:

$$IoU(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}$$

A detection is considered a **True Positive** only if $IoU \geq 0.5$ (the **PASCAL VOC threshold**). **COCO** uses averaged IoU across thresholds $mAP@[0.5:0.95]$.

**Dice Coefficient (F1 for masks)** — Sørensen–Dice index, closely related to F1:

$$DSC = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **IoU vs. Dice — Subtle but Exam-Relevant Distinction**: Dice penalizes FN more harshly than IoU because of the factor of 2 in the numerator. In medical segmentation, Dice is preferred; in general object detection, IoU is the standard.

## 2.4 Mean Average Precision (mAP) — The Detection Standard

**mAP** is the *de facto* ranking metric for object detectors (YOLO, Faster R-CNN, DETR).

**Step 1**: For each class $c$, sort predictions by descending confidence. Compute Precision and Recall at every confidence threshold.

**Step 2**: Compute the **Average Precision (AP)** as the area under the Precision-Recall curve:

$$AP_c = \int_0^1 P(R) \, dR$$

**Step 3**: Average AP across all $C$ classes:

$$mAP = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c$$

**COCO Variants**:
- $mAP@0.5$ — single IoU threshold of 0.5
- $mAP@0.75$ — stricter threshold of 0.75
- $mAP@[0.5:0.95]$ — averaged across 10 thresholds (primary COCO metric)

## 2.5 Image Quality and Reconstruction Metrics

For tasks like super-resolution, denoising, style transfer, and image generation, pixel-level fidelity matters.

**Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (I_i - \hat{I}_i)^2$$

**Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)** — log-scale version of MSE; higher is better:

$$PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{L^2}{MSE}\right)$$

where $L$ is the maximum pixel value (255 for 8-bit images). Typical good values: **PSNR > 30 dB**.

**Structural Similarity Index (SSIM)** — Perceptual metric comparing luminance, contrast, and structure:

$$SSIM(x, y) = \frac{(2\mu_x \mu_y + C_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + C_2)}$$

where $C_1 = (K_1 L)^2$ and $C_2 = (K_2 L)^2$ are stabilization constants ($K_1 = 0.01$, $K_2 = 0.03$).

**Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS)** — Uses deep features (e.g., VGG, AlexNet) to measure perceptual distance; correlates better with human judgment than PSNR/SSIM.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Table

| Metric | Used In | Why It Matters in Practice |
|:------:|:-------:|:--------------------------:|
| **Accuracy / F1** | Image classification (ImageNet, CIFAR) | Quick sanity check during training |
| **mAP@0.5:0.95** | Object detection (COCO benchmark) | Gold standard for detector comparison |
| **IoU / Dice** | Semantic segmentation (Cityscapes, medical) | Localization precision is safety-critical |
| **PSNR / SSIM** | Super-resolution, denoising | Visual fidelity for consumer media |
| **LPIPS** | GANs, image generation | Matches human perception |
| **Inference FPS** | Real-time systems (ADAS, robotics) | Latency is a hard constraint |
| **FLOPs / Params** | Edge deployment (mobile, IoT) | Memory and energy budget |

## 2.7 The Case Study Framework — A Structured Comparison Method

A rigorous KTU-style case study comparing CV models must follow this **6-step protocol**:

1. **Define the Task** (e.g., binary defect detection on PCB images).
2. **Select the Dataset** (size, class balance, domain).
3. **Choose Candidate Models** (e.g., ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, ViT-B/16).
4. **Establish Training Protocol** (same splits, augmentations, epochs).
5. **Compute Multiple Metrics** (not just accuracy — at least 4 metrics).
6. **Statistical Significance Testing** (paired t-test, McNemar's test) to confirm differences are not random.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Binary Classification Confusion Matrix Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario**: A CV system for detecting defective bolts on a manufacturing conveyor belt. The test set contains **200** images. The model's predictions versus ground truth yield the following counts: **TP = 80**, **FP = 10**, **FN = 20**, **TN = 90**.

### Step 1: Verify Matrix Consistency

The total count must equal the test-set size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP + FP + FN + TN &= 80 + 10 + 20 + 90 \\
&= 200
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ Consistent with $N = 200$.

### Step 2: Compute Accuracy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{80 + 90}{200} \\
&= \frac{170}{200} \\
&= 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The model correctly classifies 85% of all bolts.

### Step 3: Compute Precision

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 10} \\
&= \frac{80}{90} \\
&\approx 0.8889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: When the model flags a bolt as defective, it is correct **88.89%** of the time.

### Step 4: Compute Recall

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recall} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 20} \\
&= \frac{80}{100} \\
&= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The model catches **80%** of all actually defective bolts (misses 20% — dangerous in manufacturing!).

### Step 5: Compute F1-Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.8889 \cdot 0.80}{0.8889 + 0.80} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.71111}{1.68889} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.42105 \\
&\approx 0.8421
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The harmonic balance is **84.21%**, slightly lower than accuracy (85%) — a hint of P/R imbalance.

### Step 6: Compute Specificity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \\
&= \frac{90}{90 + 10} \\
&= \frac{90}{100} \\
&= 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Compute Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
MCC &= \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}} \\
&= \frac{(80)(90) - (10)(20)}{\sqrt{(90)(100)(100)(110)}} \\
&= \frac{7200 - 200}{\sqrt{99{,}000{,}000}} \\
&= \frac{7000}{9950.0} \\
&\approx 0.7035
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: Strong positive correlation; MCC < F1 confirms the class imbalance effect.

## 3.2 Worked Example — IoU Computation for Object Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario**: Ground-truth bounding box $B_{gt} = (x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (10, 10, 110, 110)$ (a 100×100 box). Predicted bounding box $B_{pred} = (30, 30, 130, 130)$ (also 100×100, but shifted by 20px).

### Step 1: Compute the Area of Each Box

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{gt} &= (110 - 10) \times (110 - 10) = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 \\
A_{pred} &= (130 - 30) \times (130 - 30) = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Intersection Coordinates

The intersection is bounded by the maximum of the top-left corners and the minimum of the bottom-right corners:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{left} &= \max(10, 30) = 30 \\
y_{top} &= \max(10, 30) = 30 \\
x_{right} &= \min(110, 130) = 110 \\
y_{bottom} &= \min(110, 130) = 110
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Intersection Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{inter} &= \max(0, x_{right} - x_{left}) \times \max(0, y_{bottom} - y_{top}) \\
&= (110 - 30) \times (110 - 30) \\
&= 80 \times 80 \\
&= 6{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Union Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{union} &= A_{gt} + A_{pred} - A_{inter} \\
&= 10{,}000 + 10{,}000 - 6{,}400 \\
&= 13{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute IoU

$$
\begin{aligned}
IoU &= \frac{A_{inter}}{A_{union}} = \frac{6{,}400}{13{,}600} \approx 0.4706
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: $IoU \approx 0.47$, which is **below the 0.5 PASCAL threshold** — this detection would be classified as a **False Positive**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — PSNR Calculation for Image Reconstruction

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario**: A super-resolution model takes a low-res image and produces a 256×256 high-res output. The ground truth is a clean 256×256 reference image. We have an MSE value of **225.0**. The maximum pixel value is **255**.

### Step 1: Recall the PSNR Formula

$$PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{L^2}{MSE}\right)$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSNR &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{225.0}\right) \\
&= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{65{,}025}{225.0}\right) \\
&= 10 \cdot \log_{10}(289.0) \\
&= 10 \times 2.4609 \\
&\approx 24.61 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: **24.61 dB** is below the 30 dB quality threshold — the reconstruction has noticeable artifacts and needs improvement.

## 3.4 Case Study — Comparing 3 Models on a Suitable Dataset

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dataset**: **CIFAR-10** (60,000 32×32 color images, 10 classes, balanced). **Task**: Image classification. **Training**: 50,000 train / 10,000 test. **Models**: ResNet-50 (transfer-learned), VGG-16 (transfer-learned), MobileNetV2 (lightweight).

| Metric | ResNet-50 | VGG-16 | MobileNetV2 |
|:------:|:---------:|:------:|:-----------:|
| **Accuracy** | 0.942 | 0.928 | 0.918 |
| **Precision (macro)** | 0.941 | 0.927 | 0.916 |
| **Recall (macro)** | 0.940 | 0.926 | 0.917 |
| **F1-Score (macro)** | 0.940 | 0.926 | 0.916 |
| **Inference Time (ms)** | 24.5 | 38.2 | **8.7** |
| **Model Size (MB)** | 102.4 | 138.6 | **14.2** |
| **FLOPs (G)** | 4.1 | 15.5 | **0.30** |

### Engineering Decision Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{ResNet} &= 0.940 \times 1.0 \text{ (accuracy weight)} - 24.5 \times 0.1 \text{ (latency weight)} = 0.940 - 0.0245 = 0.9155 \\
\text{Score}_{VGG} &= 0.926 \times 1.0 - 38.2 \times 0.1 = 0.926 - 0.0382 = 0.8878 \\
\text{Score}_{MobileNet} &= 0.916 \times 1.0 - 8.7 \times 0.1 = 0.916 - 0.0087 = 0.9073
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision**: **ResNet-50** is the best accuracy-first choice. **MobileNetV2** is the best for edge/mobile deployment where latency is paramount, despite a ~2.4% accuracy drop.

## 3.5 Real-Life CV Problems — Identification and Project Topics

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Topic Brainstorming Framework**: A great CV project must satisfy the **3S rule** — *Solvable* (annotated dataset exists), *Significant* (real-world impact), and *Scopable* (feasible in one semester).

### Tier 1 — Beginner / Reproducible Projects

| # | Project Title | Domain | Recommended Metric | Dataset |
|:-:|:--------------|:------:|:------------------:|:-------:|
| 1 | Face Mask Detection | Public Health | F1-Score | Custom / Kaggle |
| 2 | Cat vs. Dog Classifier | Education | Accuracy | Kaggle Dogs vs. Cats |
| 3 | Traffic Sign Recognition | Autonomous Vehicles | Top-5 Accuracy | GTSRB |
| 4 | Plant Disease Detection | Agriculture | F1 (per class) | PlantVillage |

### Tier 2 — Intermediate / Engineering Projects

| # | Project Title | Domain | Recommended Metric | Dataset |
|:-:|:--------------|:------:|:------------------:|:-------:|
| 5 | Pothole Detection on Roads | Smart Cities | mAP@0.5 | Custom / Roboflow |
| 6 | Brain Tumor Segmentation (MRI) | Medical CV | **Dice Coefficient** | BraTS Challenge |
| 7 | License Plate Recognition (ANPR) | Law Enforcement | CER (Character Error Rate) | UFPR-ALPR |
| 8 | Defect Detection in PCB | Manufacturing | Precision + Recall | DeepPCB |

### Tier 3 — Advanced / Research-Oriented Projects

| # | Project Title | Domain | Recommended Metric | Dataset |
|:-:|:--------------|:------:|:------------------:|:-------:|
| 9 | Crowd Density Estimation | Public Safety | MAE / RMSE | ShanghaiTech |
| 10 | Video Anomaly Detection | Surveillance | AUC-ROC | UCF-Crime |
| 11 | Visual Question Answering (VQA) | HCI / Accessibility | VQA Accuracy | VQA v2.0 |
| 12 | Underwater Image Enhancement | Marine Biology | **PSNR / SSIM / LPIPS** | UIEB |

### Identified Real-Life Problems — Industry Pain Points

1. **Healthcare**: Radiologist shortage — automated preliminary screening for X-rays/CT scans (need **high Recall**, explainable AI overlays).
2. **Agriculture**: Crop loss due to delayed pest detection — drone-based multispectral imaging (need **robust F1** under varying lighting).
3. **Retail**: Checkout-free stores (Amazon Go model) — multi-object tracking + product recognition (need **mAP + IDF1 score**).
4. **Accessibility**: Sign language translation for the deaf — pose estimation + sequence modeling (need **WER — Word Error Rate**).
5. **Disaster Response**: Search-and-rescue drone vision in rubble — thermal + RGB fusion (need **mAP under low-light**).
6. **Wildlife Conservation**: Poacher detection in thermal camera feeds — real-time alert systems (need **high Recall at low FPR**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Selection Litmus Test**: Before committing to a project, ask: (1) Is a clean, annotated dataset available? (2) Can the problem be reduced to a known CV task (classification, detection, segmentation, generation)? (3) Are the failure modes tolerable? (4) Does the model fit on a single GPU within the semester budget?

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End CV Model Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D] --> B[Train/Val/Test Split 70/15/15]
    B --> C1[Model A: ResNet50]
    B --> C2[Model B: VGG16]

    C2 --> D2[Predictions on Test Set]
    C3 --> D3[Predictions on Test Set]
    D1 --> E[Metric Computation Engine]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F1[Confusion Matrix]
    E --> F2[Classification Report]
    E --> F3[mAP / IoU / Dice]
    E --> F4[Latency / FLOPs / Params]
    F1 --> G[Comparative Performance Table]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{Decision: Best Model}

    H --> J[Iterate / Retrain]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C -->|Dog| E{Actual Class}

    D -->|Dog| F2[FP - False Positive]
    E -->|Cat| F3[FN - False Negative]
    E -->|Dog| F4[TN - True Negative]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Q1, 3 marks]**
>
> **Q1.** *Define the F1-Score in the context of computer vision model evaluation. Why is the harmonic mean preferred over the arithmetic mean when combining Precision and Recall?*
>
> **Model Answer**:
> The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision (P) and Recall (R), defined as $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$. It produces a single balanced metric on the range [0, 1], where 1 is perfect.
>
> The **harmonic mean** is preferred over the arithmetic mean because it **penalizes extreme imbalance**. For example, a model with P = 1.0 and R = 0.1 has an arithmetic mean of 0.55 (looks mediocre) but an F1 of only 0.18 (reveals catastrophic failure on recall). The harmonic mean is "honest" about poor performance on either dimension, ensuring both precision and recall are simultaneously high.

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Q2, 3 marks]**
>
> **Q2.** *What is the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric? State the standard threshold used to classify a detection as a True Positive in the PASCAL VOC benchmark.*
>
> **Model Answer**:
> **IoU** (also called the Jaccard Index) measures the overlap between a predicted bounding box/mask $A$ and the ground-truth box/mask $B$, defined as $IoU = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}$. It ranges from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).
>
> In the **PASCAL VOC** benchmark, a detection is counted as a **True Positive** only if $IoU \geq 0.5$ (i.e., 50% overlap). The **MS COCO** benchmark extends this by averaging mAP across 10 thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 in steps of 0.05, written as **mAP@[0.5:0.95]**, which is the primary COCO leaderboard metric.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Internal Choice, 14 marks]**

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **Q.A (a)** Explain the Confusion Matrix with reference to True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Answer**:
> A **Confusion Matrix** is an N×N tabular summary of classification model predictions versus ground-truth labels, where N is the number of classes. For binary classification (N=2), it is a 2×2 matrix with four cells:
>
> - **True Positive (TP)**: Model predicts positive, ground truth is positive — correct hit.
> - **True Negative (TN)**: Model predicts negative, ground truth is negative — correct rejection.
> - **False Positive (FP)**: Model predicts positive, ground truth is negative — **Type I error** (false alarm).
> - **False Negative (FN)**: Model predicts negative, ground truth is positive — **Type II error** (missed detection).
>
