> For a multi-class problem with $C$ classes, the matrix is $C \times C$ where the diagonal represents correct classifications and off-diagonal entries represent misclassifications (rows = actual, columns = predicted).
>
> Every scalar classification metric — Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Specificity, MCC — is mathematically derived from these four counts. For example, $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$.
>
> **Worked numerical example** *(for full 7 marks, present a calculation)*:
> Given TP = 90, FP = 10, FN = 20, TN = 80 (N=200):
>
> $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{90+80}{200} = 0.85, \quad P = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90, \quad R = \frac{90}{110} \approx 0.818, \quad F_1 \approx 0.857$$

> **Q.A (b)** Compare and contrast PSNR and SSIM as image reconstruction quality metrics. Why is LPIPS often preferred in modern generative CV tasks? *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Answer**:
> **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** is a pixel-fidelity metric computed as $PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{L^2}{MSE}\right)$ where $L$ is the maximum pixel value (255) and MSE is the mean squared error. Higher PSNR indicates closer pixel-wise match. **Limitations**: It assumes pixel independence, ignores structural content, and correlates poorly with human perception — a slightly blurred but noise-free image can score higher than a sharp, perceptually superior one.
>
> **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index)** improves on PSNR by comparing three local statistics — **luminance** ($\mu_x, \mu_y$), **contrast** ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y$), and **structure** ($\sigma_{xy}$) — via $SSIM = \frac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + C_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + C_2)}$. SSIM is bounded in [-1, 1] and aligns better with human judgment, but still uses hand-crafted statistical features.
>
> **LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity)** computes distance in the deep feature space of a pretrained network (e.g., VGG, AlexNet). It captures high-level semantic and perceptual differences, **correlates strongly with human opinion scores** (MOS), and is the preferred metric for **GANs, diffusion models, and super-resolution** in recent literature.
>
> **Comparative Summary**:
>
> | Property | PSNR | SSIM | LPIPS |
> |:--------:|:----:|:----:|:-----:|
> | Mathematical basis | Pixel-level (MSE) | Statistical (moments) | Deep features |
> | Range | [0, ∞] dB | [-1, 1] | [0, ∞) |
> | Perceptual fidelity | Low | Medium | **High** |
> | Computational cost | Very low | Low | High (deep inference) |
> | Best for | Quick sanity check | Classical denoising | Generative CV, SR |

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

> **Q.B (a)** Design a case study to compare the performance of three different deep learning models (ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, and MobileNetV3) on a suitable dataset for an image classification task. Specify dataset choice, metrics to be used, and the decision criteria. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Answer**:
>
> **1. Dataset Selection** *(2 marks)*:
> **CIFAR-10** is chosen — it has 60,000 32×32 RGB images across 10 balanced classes (airplane, car, bird, cat, deer, dog, frog, horse, ship, truck), with a standard 50,000/10,000 train/test split. It is small enough for fast iteration yet challenging enough to differentiate models.
>
> **2. Models** *(1 mark)*:
> - **ResNet-50**: Deep residual network (~25.6M params), accuracy-oriented.
> - **EfficientNet-B0**: Compound-scaled CNN (~5.3M params), accuracy-efficiency balance.
> - **MobileNetV3-Large**: Lightweight (~5.4M params), latency-oriented for edge deployment.
>
> **3. Training Protocol** *(1 mark)*: All models fine-tuned with the same hyperparameters — Adam optimizer, learning rate $10^{-4}$, 30 epochs, identical augmentations (random crop + horizontal flip), identical train/val/test split.
>
> **4. Metrics** *(2 marks)*:
> - **Accuracy** and **macro-F1** for classification quality.
> - **Inference latency (ms/image)** on the same hardware.
> - **Model size (MB)** and **FLOPs (G)** for deployment cost.
>
> **5. Decision Criteria** *(1 mark)*: Use a weighted score: $S = w_1 \cdot F_1 + w_2 \cdot (1/\text{latency}) + w_3 \cdot (1/\text{size})$ with weights chosen per deployment context (cloud vs. edge).
>
> **Expected Outcome**: ResNet-50 will lead in raw F1, EfficientNet-B0 will offer the best accuracy-per-FLOP trade-off, and MobileNetV3 will win on edge latency.

> **Q.B (b)** Identify and justify three real-life problems in the domain of computer vision that are suitable for a B.Tech final-year project. For each, suggest the most appropriate evaluation metric. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Model Answer**:
>
> **Problem 1: Automated Diabetic Retinopathy Detection from Retinal Fundus Images** *(2.5 marks)*
> - **Domain**: Medical CV / Public Health.
> - **Dataset**: APTOS 2019 Blindness Detection (Kaggle) or EyePACS.
> - **Task**: Multi-class classification (5 severity grades).
> - **Best Metric**: **Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK)** — penalizes misclassifications proportional to ordinal distance, recognizing that confusing "mild" with "moderate" is less harmful than "no DR" with "proliferative DR."
> - **Why**: Ordinal labels require distance-aware metrics, not flat accuracy.
>
> **Problem 2: Real-Time Pothole Detection for Smart City Road Maintenance** *(2.5 marks)*
> - **Domain**: Smart Infrastructure / Transportation.
> - **Dataset**: Roboflow pothole dataset (6,000+ annotated images).
> - **Task**: Object detection.
> - **Best Metric**: **mAP@0.5:0.95** (COCO-style) — standard for detection; combines localization precision and recall.
> - **Why**: Both false alarms (wasted maintenance crews) and missed detections (unrepaired hazards) have real costs, so a balanced mean across IoU thresholds is needed.
>
> **Problem 3: Sign Language Recognition for Hearing-Impaired Accessibility** *(2 marks)*
> - **Domain**: Accessibility / HCI.
> - **Dataset**: WLASL (Word-Level American Sign Language) video dataset.
> - **Task**: Video classification / temporal action recognition.
> - **Best Metric**: **Top-1 Accuracy** plus **Word Error Rate (WER)** if treated as a sequence prediction.
> - **Why**: End-user experience is determined by direct prediction correctness; WER captures insertion/deletion errors in continuous signing.
>
> **Justification Rationale**: All three problems satisfy the **3S rule** — *Solvable* (annotated datasets exist), *Significant* (real-world impact: preventing blindness, saving infrastructure budgets, enabling communication), and *Scopable* (achievable within a semester on a single GPU).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing IoU with Dice** — Both range [0,1] and measure overlap, but Dice is weighted by 2× the intersection; using them interchangeably will lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Citing only Accuracy** — On imbalanced data (e.g., 99% normal, 1% defective), 99% accuracy is meaningless. Always pair with F1, MCC, or ROC-AUC.
> 3. **Ignoring Computational Cost** — A model with 95% accuracy but 500ms inference is unusable in real-time systems. KTU expects you to discuss at least one efficiency metric (FPS, FLOPs, or size).
> 4. **No Justification for Metric Choice** — Just listing "I used Accuracy" without explaining *why* is incomplete. Always link metric to task type and failure costs.
> 5. **Confusing mAP with AP** — AP is per-class; mAP is the mean across classes. mAP@0.5 is NOT the same as mAP@0.5:0.95.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 Quality Metrics**

- ✅ **Confusion Matrix** is the atom: TP, FP, TN, FN — every classification metric derives from it.
- ✅ **Accuracy** alone is **dangerous on imbalanced data**; always pair with **F1, MCC, or ROC-AUC**.
- ✅ **Precision** answers *"Of those I flagged, how many are real?"*; **Recall** answers *"Of the real ones, how many did I catch?"*
- ✅ **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean — punishes imbalance between P and R.
- ✅ **mAP@0.5:0.95** is the **gold standard** for object detection (COCO benchmark).
- ✅ **IoU ≥ 0.5** is the PASCAL threshold for a True Positive detection.
- ✅ **Dice Coefficient** is preferred over IoU in **medical segmentation** (penalizes FN more).
- ✅ **PSNR > 30 dB** indicates good reconstruction; **SSIM** is perception-aware; **LPIPS** is the modern deep-feature perceptual metric.
- ✅ A rigorous **case study** requires: same dataset, same splits, same protocol, **multiple metrics** (not just one), and **statistical testing** (t-test / McNemar).
- ✅ Real-life CV project selection follows the **3S rule**: **Solvable, Significant, Scopable**.
- ✅ **Metric ↔ Task Mapping** (memorize!):
  - Classification → Accuracy, F1, MCC, ROC-AUC
  - Detection → mAP, IoU
  - Segmentation → IoU, Dice
  - Generation / Reconstruction → PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS
  - Real-time Systems → FPS, FLOPs, Model Size
- ✅ **Engineering judgment** matters: choose metrics based on **asymmetric error costs** (medical = high Recall; spam = high Precision).
- ✅ **Comparison must be multi-dimensional** — accuracy + latency + size + interpretability — never optimize a single metric blindly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solved and Implemented Using Different Algorithms - A Few Suggestions Are Given Below - Build a System Capable of Detecting and Classifying Objects (Like Pedestrians
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Solved and Implemented Using Different Algorithms - A Few Suggestions Are Given Below - Build a System Capable of Detecting and Classifying Objects (Like Pedestrians # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Object Detection and Classification** is a fundamental computer vision task that involves two integrated sub-problems: 1. **Localization** — Determining *where* an object of interest is present in an image by drawing a bounding box around it. 2. **Classification** — Determining *what* the object is by assigning a class label (e.g., *pedestrian*, *car*, *bicycle*) along with a confidence score. In the specific context of **Pedestrian Detection**, the goal is to detect human figures in still images or video streams — a critical capability for autonomous vehicles, surveillance systems, smart traffic management, and assistive robotics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes hands-on implementation. Students are expected to be familiar with at least one classical pipeline (HOG + SVM or Haar Cascades) and one modern deep-learning pipeline (YOLO / SSD / Faster R-CNN). Mathematically, an object detector models a mapping function: $$f : \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3} \rightarrow \{(b_i, c_i, s_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$ where: - $H, W$ are image height and width - $b_i = (x, y, w, h)$ is the bounding box coordinates - $c_i \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ is the class label - $s_i \in [0, 1]$ is the confidence score - $N$ is the number of detected objects --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **security guard at a busy airport entrance**. Your job is twofold: - **"Where is the person?"** → You look around and point your finger at each person (this is *localization*). - **"Is that a passenger or a staff member?"** → You mentally classify them based on uniform, ID badge, posture (this is *classification*). A computer vision system does *exactly the same thing*, but: - Instead of eyes, it uses **pixels**. - Instead of intuition, it uses **mathematical features** (gradients, edges, textures) and **learned patterns** (from thousands of training images). - Instead of pointing a finger, it draws **bounding boxes**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of object detection as a *Google search* on an image. The system "scans" the image at multiple scales, "asks" each region "is there a pedestrian here?", and "marks" the answer with a box and a label. --- ## 1.3 Why Pedestrian Detection? — Engineering Significance Pedestrian detection is one of the most socially impactful CV applications: | Application | Engineering Utility | |---|---| | **Autonomous Driving** | Collision avoidance, emergency braking (Tesla, Waymo) | | **Smart Surveillance** | Crowd counting, anomaly detection | | **Traffic Safety** | Pedestrian-crossing signals, red-light cameras | | **Assistive Tech** | Smart canes for visually impaired | | **Retail Analytics** | Footfall analysis in malls | | **Sports Analytics** | Player tracking in football, basketball | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Performance Metrics Used in Pedestrian Detection:** > - **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ > - **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ > - **mAP (mean Average Precision)** — industry standard > - **FPS (Frames Per Second)** — real-time feasibility > - **Miss Rate** at FPPI = $10^{-1}$ (INRIA benchmark standard) where $TP$ = True Positives, $FP$ = False Positives, $FN$ = False Negatives, and $FPPI$ = False Positives Per Image. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Algorithmic Families for Object Detection There are **three major families** of object detection algorithms, each with distinct trade-offs: ### Family 1: Classical Machine Learning (Pre-Deep Learning Era) - **Approach:** Hand-crafted features + Traditional classifier - **Key Algorithms:** HOG + SVM, Haar Cascades, SIFT + Bag-of-Words - **Pros:** Lightweight, interpretable, low compute - **Cons:** Poor generalization, manual feature engineering ### Family 2: Two-Stage Deep Learning Detectors - **Approach:** Region proposals first → Classification second - **Key Algorithms:** R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, **Faster R-CNN** - **Pros:** High accuracy - **Cons:** Slow (not real-time), complex pipeline ### Family 3: One-Stage Deep Learning Detectors - **Approach:** Single neural network predicts boxes + classes simultaneously - **Key Algorithms:** **YOLO (You Only Look Once)**, SSD, RetinaNet - **Pros:** Real-time (30–60+ FPS), end-to-end trainable - **Cons:** Slightly lower accuracy on tiny objects --- ## 2.2 The HOG + SVM Pipeline (Classical Pedestrian Detection) **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients)** was proposed by Dalal & Triggs (2005) and remains the gold-standard classical pedestrian detector. ### Step-by-Step Logic of HOG: 1. **Global Normalization (Gamma Correction):** Reduce lighting effects 2. **Compute Gradients:** Apply Sobel filters in $x$ and $y$ directions 3. **Cell Histograms:** Divide image into $8 \times 8$ pixel cells; bin gradients into 9 orientation bins (0°–180°) 4. **Block Normalization:** Group cells into $2 \times 2$ blocks; apply L2-normalization 5. **Feature Vector:** Concatenate all block histograms into one feature vector 6. **Classification:** Feed feature vector to a linear **SVM (Support Vector Machine)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind HOG:** Human silhouettes have *characteristic gradient patterns* — the head has a circular gradient distribution, shoulders create horizontal edges, legs create vertical edges. HOG captures these *local shape signatures* invariant to lighting and small deformations. ### Mathematical Foundation of HOG: For a pixel $I(x, y)$, gradients are computed as: $$G_x(x, y) = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$$ $$G_y(x, y) = I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)$$ The gradient magnitude and orientation: $$M(x, y) = \sqrt{G_x(x, y)^2 + G_y(x, y)^2}$$ $$\theta(x, y) = \arctan\left(\frac{G_y(x, y)}{G_x(x, y)}\right)$$ The block normalization (L2-norm variant): $$v_{\text{norm}} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{\|v\|_2^2 + \epsilon^2}}$$ where $v$ is the concatenated block feature vector and $\epsilon$ is a small constant to prevent division by zero. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Image Gradient (x) | $G_x = I(x+1,y) - I(x-1,y)$ | Detect vertical edges | | Image Gradient (y) | $G_y = I(x,y+1) - I(x,y-1)$ | Detect horizontal edges | | Gradient Magnitude | $M = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | Edge strength | | Gradient Orientation | $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$ | Edge direction | | L2 Normalization | $v_{norm} = v / \sqrt{\|v\|^2 + \epsilon^2}$ | Illumination invariance | | IoU (Intersection over Union) | $IoU = \dfrac{A \cap B}{A \cup B}$ | Box overlap measure | | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Detection accuracy | | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection coverage | | mAP | $\dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} AP_k$ | Overall detector quality | | Non-Maximum Suppression | Keep box with max score, suppress $IoU > 0.5$ | Remove duplicates | | YOLO Loss | $\lambda_{coord}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + \sum (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 + \sum \text{CE}(p_i, \hat{p}_i)$ | Multi-task loss | | HOG Descriptor Size (64×128 image) | $7 \times 15 \times 4 \times 9 = 3780$ features | Fixed-length vector | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: HOG Feature Vector Size Let's derive the size of the HOG descriptor for a standard $64 \times 128$ pedestrian window. **Step 1: Define the image size and cell parameters.** A standard INRIA pedestrian dataset uses $64 \times 128$ pixel training images. HOG cells are $8 \times 8$ pixels. Number of cells horizontally: $$n_{cx} = \frac{64}{8} = 8 \text{ cells}$$ Number of cells vertically: $$n_{cy} = \frac{128}{8} = 16 \text{ cells}$$ **Step 2: Define the block parameters.** A HOG block contains $2 \times 2$ cells (16×16 pixels). Blocks slide with **1-cell stride** to get dense features. Number of block positions horizontally: $$n_{bx} = \frac{8 - 2}{1} + 1 = 7 \text{ blocks}$$ Number of block positions vertically: $$n_{by} = \frac{16 - 2}{1} + 1 = 15 \text{ blocks}$$ **Step 3: Compute histograms per block.** Each cell contributes a 9-bin orientation histogram (unsigned gradients, 0° to 180°). A $2 \times 2$ block therefore has: $$H_{block} = 2 \times 2 \times 9 = 36 \text{ features}$$ **Step 4: Compute total descriptor length.** $$D_{total} = n_{bx} \times n_{by} \times H_{block}$$ $$D_{total} = 7 \times 15 \times 36$$ $$\boxed{D_{total} = 3780 \text{ features}}$$ This 3780-dimensional vector is then fed into a linear SVM for pedestrian vs. non-pedestrian classification. --- ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Intersection over Union (IoU) IoU is the **fundamental evaluation metric** for any object detector. Let's derive it from first principles. **Step 1: Define the two bounding boxes.** Consider a ground-truth box $B_{gt} = (x_1^{gt}, y_1^{gt}, x_2^{gt}, y_2^{gt})$ and a predicted box $B_{pr} = (x_1^{pr}, y_1^{pr}, x_2^{pr}, y_2^{pr})$. **Step 2: Compute the coordinates of the intersection rectangle.** $$x_1^{int} = \max(x_1^{gt}, x_1^{pr})$$ $$y_1^{int} = \max(y_1^{gt}, y_1^{pr})$$ $$x_2^{int} = \min(x_2^{gt}, x_2^{pr})$$ $$y_2^{int} = \min(y_2^{gt}, y_2^{pr})$$ **Step 3: Compute the intersection area.** $$A_{int} = \max(0, x_2^{int} - x_1^{int}) \times \max(0, y_2^{int} - y_1^{int})$$ The $\max(0, \cdot)$ handles the **non-overlapping case** (no intersection → area = 0). **Step 4: Compute the union area.** $$A_{union} = A_{gt} + A_{pr} - A_{int}$$ This uses the **inclusion-exclusion principle** of set theory. **Step 5: Compute IoU.** $$IoU = \frac{A_{int}}{A_{union}}$$ **Step 6: Numerical Example.** Suppose $B_{gt}$ has area $100$ and $B_{pr}$ has area $80$, and they overlap with intersection area $60$. $$A_{union} = 100 + 80 - 60 = 120$$ $$IoU = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** A detection is considered a **True Positive** if $IoU \geq 0.5$ (PASCAL VOC standard). For stricter evaluation (COCO benchmark), $IoU \geq 0.75$ is used. --- ## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) NMS is the post-processing step that **eliminates duplicate detections** of the same object. Let's derive the algorithm: **Step 1: Sort all detected boxes by confidence score in descending order.** $$S = \{b_1, b_2, \dots, b_N\} \text{ where } s_1 \geq s_2 \geq \dots \geq s_N$$ **Step 2: Initialize the keep-list as empty.** $$K = \emptyset$$ **Step 3: Iterative selection loop.** While $S$ is not empty: 1. Pick the box with the highest score: $b^* = \arg\max_{b \in S} s_b$ 2. Add it to the keep-list: $K \leftarrow K \cup \{b^*\}$ 3. Remove $b^*$ from $S$ 4. For every remaining box $b_j \in S$: - If $IoU(b^*, b_j) > \text{threshold}_{NMS}$ (typically $0.5$): - Remove $b_j$ from $S$ (suppress duplicate) 5. Repeat **Step 4: Output the final keep-list $K$.** The result is a set of **non-overlapping, high-confidence** bounding boxes. **Worked Example (3 overlapping boxes):** | Box | Score | IoU with Box A | |---|---|---| | A | 0.95 | 1.00 | | B | 0.82 | 0.72 | | C | 0.45 | 0.31 | With $NMS$ threshold = 0.5: - Keep **A** (highest score). - Suppress **B** (IoU > 0.5). - Keep **C** (IoU < 0.5, different object). Final output: $\{A, C\}$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Algorithm | Year | Type | Speed (FPS) | mAP (COCO) | Best Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **HOG + SVM** | 2005 | Classical | 5–10 | N/A | Learning fundamentals, embedded | | **Haar Cascades** | 2001 | Classical | 30+ | N/A | Face detection, real-time | | **R-CNN** | 2014 | Two-Stage | 0.05 | 66% | Research, offline | | **Faster R-CNN** | 2015 | Two-Stage | 7 | 78% | High-accuracy needs | | **YOLOv1** | 2016 | One-Stage | 45 | 64% | Real-time, simple | | **YOLOv3** | 2018 | One-Stage | 30 | 76% | Balanced speed/accuracy | | **YOLOv5** | 2020 | One-Stage | 140+ | 81% | Production deployment | | **YOLOv8** | 2023 | One-Stage | 200+ | 85% | State-of-the-art | | **SSD300** | 2016 | One-Stage | 46 | 74% | Mobile applications | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classical Pedestrian Detection Pipeline (HOG + SVM) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Image or Video Frame] --> B[Preprocessing
Resize to 64x128
Grayscale Conversion] B --> C[Gamma Normalization
sqrt compression] C --> D[Compute Gradients
Sobel Filter Gx Gy] D --> E[Cell Histograms
8x8 cells, 9 bins] E --> F[Block Normalization
L2-norm, 2x2 cells] F --> G[HOG Feature Vector
3780 dimensions] G --> H[Linear SVM Classifier] H --> I{Pedestrian?} I -- Yes --> J[Draw Bounding Box] I -- No --> K[Reject Region] J --> L[Non-Maximum Suppression] L --> M[Final Detection Output] K --> M ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: YOLO (You Only Look Once) Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Image] --> B[Resize to 416x416] B --> C[CNN Backbone
Darknet-53] C --> D[Feature Maps] D --> E[Grid Division
SxS cells] E --> F[Each cell predicts
Bounding Box B
Confidence
Class Probabilities] F --> G[Multi-task Loss
Regression + Classification] G --> H[Decoded Predictions] H --> I[Confidence Filtering
score > threshold] I --> J[Non-Maximum Suppression] J --> K[Final Detected Objects
with Bounding Boxes] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Complete End-to-End System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT A1[Image Source
Camera or Video] end subgraph PREPROCESSING B1[Resize] --> B2[Normalize] --> B3[Augmentation] end subgraph DETECTION C1[Feature Extraction] --> C2[Region Proposal] --> C3[Classification] end subgraph POSTPROCESSING D1[NMS] --> D2[Score Threshold] --> D3[Bounding Box Refinement] end subgraph OUTPUT E1[Annotated Image] --> E2[Alert System] --> E3[Database Logging] end A1 --> B1 B3 --> C1 C3 --> D1 D3 --> E1 ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Classical vs. Deep Learning Approach ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLASSICAL_APPROACH A1[Hand-crafted Features
HOG, SIFT, Haar] --> A2[Shallow Classifier
SVM, AdaBoost] A2 --> A3[Detection Output] end subgraph DEEP_LEARNING_APPROACH B1[Raw Pixels] --> B2[Deep CNN
Auto-learned Features] B2 --> B3[End-to-End Prediction] B3 --> A3 end A3 --> C[Final Result] B2 --> C ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram: Multi-Scale Detection Sliding Window ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Image] --> B[Scale 1
Original Size] A --> C[Scale 2
0.75x] A --> D[Scale 3
0.5x] A --> E[Scale 4
0.25x] B --> F[Sliding Window
at Scale 1] C --> G[Sliding Window
at Scale 2] D --> H[Sliding Window
at Scale 3] E --> I[Sliding Window
at Scale 4] F --> J[Classifier] G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[All Detections] K --> L[Non-Maximum Suppression] L --> M[Final Output] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Object Detection and Explain Its Two Sub-Tasks. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Object Detection** is a computer vision task that identifies and localizes objects of interest within an image or video. It combines two sub-tasks: 1. **Localization:** Determining the spatial position of the object by predicting a bounding box $(x, y, w, h)$ around it. 2. **Classification:** Assigning a class label (e.g., pedestrian, car) with a confidence score $s \in [0,1]$. **Example:** In a self-driving car, the system must both locate (where is the pedestrian?) and classify (is that a pedestrian?) in real time. The mathematical formulation is: $$f : \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3} \rightarrow \{(b_i, c_i, s_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$ where $b_i$ is the bounding box, $c_i$ is the class label, and $s_i$ is the confidence score. --- ### Question 2: What is HOG and Why is it Used in Pedestrian Detection? **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients)** is a feature descriptor proposed by Dalal and Triggs (2005) that captures the *distribution of gradient orientations* in localized portions of an image. **Why HOG Works for Pedestrians:** - Human silhouettes have **distinctive gradient patterns** — the head is roughly circular, shoulders are horizontal, and legs are vertical. - HOG computes gradients using Sobel filters, bins them into 9 orientation channels (0°–180°) over $8 \times 8$ pixel cells, then L2-normalizes over $2 \times 2$ cell blocks. - It is **invariant to lighting changes** and small geometric deformations. - For a standard $64 \times 128$ window, HOG produces a **3780-dimensional feature vector** that is fed to a linear SVM. **Key Equations:** $$M(x, y) = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2} \quad ; \quad \theta(x, y) = \arctan\left(\frac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: Build and Explain a Pedestrian Detection System Using HOG and SVM. **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Expected Question, Module 4]** #### Part (a): Explain the HOG Feature Extraction Process in Detail with Mathematical Foundations. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Preprocessing (1 Mark)** The input image is converted to grayscale and resized to a standard $64 \times 128$ window. Optionally, gamma correction ($\sqrt{\cdot}$ or $\log(\cdot)$) is applied for lighting normalization. **Step 2: Gradient Computation (1.5 Marks)** Horizontal and vertical gradients are computed using 1-D centered Sobel masks: $$G_x(x, y) = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$$ $$G_y(x, y) = I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)$$ Gradient magnitude and orientation: $$M(x, y) = \sqrt{G_x(x, y)^2 + G_y(x, y)^2}$$ $$\theta(x, y) = \arctan\left(\frac{G_y(x, y)}{G_x(x, y)}\right)$$ Orientation is binned into 9 channels (0°, 20°, 40°, ..., 160°) using unsigned gradients. **Step 3: Cell Histograms (1.5 Marks)** The image is divided into $8 \times 8$ pixel cells. Within each cell, the gradient orientations of all 64 pixels are weighted by their magnitudes and accumulated into a 9-bin histogram. This captures the **local shape signature**. **Step 4: Block Normalization (1.5 Marks)** Cells are grouped into $2 \times 2$ blocks (16×16 pixels). L2-normalization is applied: $$v_{\text{norm}} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{\|v\|_2^2 + \epsilon^2}}$$ This makes the descriptor **invariant to illumination and contrast** changes. **Step 5: Concatenation and SVM Classification (1.5 Marks)** For a $64 \times 128$ image: - Cells: $8 \times 16 = 128$ - Blocks (with 1-cell stride): $7 \times 15 = 105$ - Features per block: $4 \times 9 = 36$ - **Total: $7 \times 15 \times 36 = 3780$ features** This 3780-D vector is classified by a **linear SVM** trained on positive (pedestrian) and negative (non-pedestrian) samples from datasets like INRIA. --- #### Part (b): Describe the Multi-Scale Sliding Window Detection Strategy and NMS Post-Processing. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Multi-Scale Sliding Window (3 Marks)** Since pedestrians appear at **different sizes** in images, detection is performed at **multiple scales**: 1. Create an image pyramid by resizing the input at scales $s = \{1.0, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25\}$. 2. At each scale, slide a $64 \times 128$ window across the image with a fixed stride (e.g., 8 pixels). 3. For each window position, extract the HOG descriptor and run SVM inference. 4. If the SVM score exceeds threshold (e.g., 0.5), record the bounding box with the score. **Total windows processed** for a $640 \times 480$ image at 4 scales: $$N_{windows} = \sum_{s} \left(\frac{W_s}{8}\right) \times \left(\frac{H_s}{8}\right) \approx 5000 \text{ to } 50000$$ **Step 2: Non-Maximum Suppression (3 Marks)** The sliding window generates **many overlapping detections** of the same pedestrian. NMS eliminates duplicates: 1. Sort all boxes by confidence score (descending). 2. Pick the box with the highest score → add to keep list. 3. Compute $IoU$ between this box and all remaining boxes. 4. Remove any box with $IoU > 0.5$ (they likely refer to the same object). 5. Repeat until all boxes are processed. **IoU formula:** $$IoU = \frac{A_{int}}{A_{gt} + A_{pr} - A_{int}}$$ **Step 3: Final Output (1 Mark)** The output is a set of clean, non-overlapping bounding boxes with their confidence scores, ready for downstream tasks (e.g., tracking, action recognition). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Students often confuse **IoU threshold** with **score threshold** — they serve different purposes! > - **Score threshold** filters weak detections (low confidence). > - **IoU threshold** removes duplicate detections of the *same* object. > - Many students forget to apply **L2 normalization** in HOG, which is critical for illumination invariance. Loss of 1–2 marks. --- ### Question B: Explain the YOLO Algorithm for Real-Time Object Detection and Compare it with R-CNN. **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam - Expected Question, Module 4]** #### Part (a): Explain the YOLO Algorithm with its Architecture and Loss Function. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: YOLO's Core Idea (1 Mark)** YOLO (You Only Look Once), proposed by Redmon et al. (2016), reframes object detection as a **single regression problem**. The entire image is passed through a CNN **only once** to predict all bounding boxes and class probabilities simultaneously. **Step 2: Grid-Based Prediction (2 Marks)** The input image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $7 \times 7$ in YOLOv1). Each grid cell is responsible for detecting objects whose center falls within it. Each cell predicts: - $B$ bounding boxes: $(x, y, w, h)$ + confidence score - $C$ class probabilities: $P(\text{Class}_i \mid \text{Object})$ For YOLOv1: $S = 7$, $B = 2$, $C = 20$ (PASCAL VOC), giving a final tensor of $7 \times 7 \times 30$. **Step 3: YOLO Architecture (1.5 Marks)** - **Backbone:** Darknet-53 (in YOLOv3) — 53 convolutional layers. - **Neck:** Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) for multi-scale detection. - **Head:** Three detection heads at different scales (in YOLOv3 and later). **Step 4: YOLO Loss Function (2.5 Marks)** YOLO uses a **multi-part loss** combining localization, confidence, and classification: $$L = \lambda_{coord} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj} \left[ (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2 \right]$$ $$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ $$+ \lambda_{noobj} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{noobj} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$ $$+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{obj} \sum_{c \in classes} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$ where $\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}$ is 1 if the $j$-th box of cell $i$ is responsible for detecting an object, and $\lambda_{coord} = 5$, $\lambda_{noobj} = 0.5$ are weighting factors. --- #### Part (b): Compare YOLO with R-CNN in a Detailed Table. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | R-CNN | YOLO | |---|---|---| | **Year** | 2014 (Girshick et al.) | 2016 (Redmon et al.) | | **Paradigm** | Two-Stage (Region Proposal + Classification) | One-Stage (Unified Regression) | | **Pipeline** | Selective Search → CNN per region → SVM → NMS | Single CNN forward pass → NMS | | **Speed (FPS)** | ~0.05 FPS (very slow) | 45 FPS (real-time) | | **mAP (VOC 2007)** | 66% | 64% (v1), 76% (v3), 85% (v8) | | **Training** | Multi-stage, complex | End-to-end | | **Inference Time** | ~20 seconds/image | ~22 ms/image | | **Region Proposals** | 2000 per image (Selective Search) | Implicit via grid cells | | **Backbone** | AlexNet, VGG-16 | Darknet-53, CSPDarknet | | **Global Context** | Limited (per region) | Yes (full image in one pass) | | **Small Object Detection** | Better (due to region-specific features) | Initially weak, improved in v3+ | | **Hardware Requirements** | High (storage for 2000 features/image) | Moderate | | **Use Case** | Offline, accuracy-critical | Real-time, embedded systems | **Conclusion:** YOLO trades a small amount of accuracy for a **massive speed gain**, making it the preferred choice for real-time pedestrian detection in autonomous vehicles and surveillance. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Do NOT write that YOLO is "more accurate" than R-CNN. R-CNN variants (Faster R-CNN) are *more accurate*; YOLO is *faster*. > - Students often miss the **square root** in the YOLO width/height loss term $(\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i)^2$ — this is critical because it penalizes errors in *small boxes* more heavily. Loss of 1 mark. > - The $\lambda_{noobj}$ term is often forgotten. It down-weights the confidence loss for cells that contain no object, preventing the model from being overwhelmed by background. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** ### 🔑 Core Concepts - ✅ Object detection = Localization + Classification - ✅ Pedestrian detection is a **binary classification problem** (pedestrian vs. background) - ✅ HOG features are **3780-D** for a $64 \times 128$ image - ✅ SVM is used as the classifier in the classical pipeline - ✅ YOLO is a **one-stage, real-time** detector - ✅ R-CNN is a **two-stage, high-accuracy** detector ### 📐 Key Formulas to Memorize - ✅ Gradient Magnitude: $M = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ - ✅ Gradient Orientation: $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$ - ✅ L2 Normalization: $v_{norm} = v / \sqrt{\|v\|^2 + \epsilon^2}$ - ✅ IoU: $IoU = A_{int} / (A_{gt} + A_{pr} - A_{int})$ - ✅ Precision: $P = TP / (TP + FP)$ - ✅ Recall: $R = TP / (TP + FN)$ ### 🧠 Algorithms to Know Cold - ✅ **HOG + SVM** — Classical, Dalal-Triggs 2005 - ✅ **Haar Cascades** — Viola-Jones 2001 (face detection) - ✅ **YOLO** — Redmon et al. 2016 - ✅ **Faster R-CNN** — Ren et al. 2015 - ✅ **SSD** — Liu et al. 2016 ### ⚠️ Common Exam Pitfalls - ❌ Confusing **score threshold** with **IoU threshold** in NMS - ❌ Forgetting to mention **multi-scale detection** in sliding window methods - ❌ Missing the $\lambda_{coord}$ and $\lambda_{noobj}$ weights in YOLO loss - ❌ Not explaining **why** HOG works for pedestrians (shape signatures) - ❌ Writing YOLO as "more accurate" than R-CNN (it's faster, not more accurate) ### 🎯 Datasets to Mention - ✅ **INRIA Person Dataset** — Standard for pedestrian detection - ✅ **PASCAL VOC** — 20 classes, 2007/2012 versions - ✅ **MS COCO** — 80 classes, modern benchmark - ✅ **CityPersons** — Pedestrian-specific, urban scenes - ✅ **EuroCity Persons** — Day/night pedestrian dataset ### 💡 Real-World Applications to Cite - ✅ Autonomous vehicles (Tesla Autopilot, Waymo) - ✅ Smart traffic signals - ✅ Surveillance and security systems - ✅ Crowd density estimation - ✅ Sports analytics (player tracking)

topics.title: Vehicles, Traffic Signs) in Real-Time from Video Feeds in Autonomous Vehicles - You Can Explore Different Algorithms Like YOLO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Vehicles, Traffic Signs) in Real-Time from Video Feeds in Autonomous Vehicles - You Can Explore Different Algorithms Like YOLO ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Real-Time Object Detection** in the context of autonomous vehicles refers to the computational process of identifying, classifying, and localizing objects of interest (such as other vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists, and traffic signs) within a continuous stream of video frames acquired from onboard cameras, with a throughput latency typically bounded by **30 FPS (Frames Per Second)** or lower, and a decision latency not exceeding **100 ms** per frame to ensure safe vehicular navigation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):** > Object Detection in autonomous driving is the simultaneous solution of two sub-problems: > 1. **Classification** — assigning a semantic label $c \in \{ \text{Car, Pedestrian, Stop Sign, ...} \}$ > 2. **Localization** — regressing a bounding box $B = (x, y, w, h, c)$ where $(x, y)$ is the center, $(w, h)$ are dimensions, and $c$ is the confidence score. **YOLO (You Only Look Once)** is a unified, single-stage, end-to-end convolutional neural network architecture that frames object detection as a single regression problem, predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities directly from full images in one forward pass. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "One Glance" Driver > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — The Human Eye vs. YOLO** > > Imagine a seasoned taxi driver glancing at a busy intersection. In a **single 200-millisecond sweep of the eye**, the driver simultaneously: > - Notices *where* a red car is (Localization) > - Recognizes it as a *car* and not a *truck* (Classification) > - Assesses *how confident* they are it's a car (Confidence) > - Ignores the irrelevant billboard behind it (Background suppression) > > **YOLO replicates exactly this single-glance behavior.** Older two-stage detectors (R-CNN family) emulate a *student who first proposes regions, then inspects each region separately* — accurate but slow. YOLO is the *expert driver* — one neural network, one forward pass, one decision. ### 1.3 Engineering Constants & Performance Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Real-Time Detection Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite):** > - **FPS (Frames Per Second):** Throughput. Real-time threshold $\geq \mathbf{30\ \text{FPS}}$ (corresponds to $33\ \text{ms/frame}$). > - **mAP (mean Average Precision):** Accuracy. Standard benchmark is $\text{mAP}_{0.5}$ at $\text{IoU}=0.5$ and $\text{mAP}_{0.5:0.95}$ (COCO-style averaged IoU). > - **IoU (Intersection over Union):** Localization quality. Threshold for a True Positive is typically $\mathbf{0.5}$. > - **Latency:** End-to-end inference time. Safety-critical threshold for Level-3+ autonomy $\leq \mathbf{100\ \text{ms}}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The YOLO Detection Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic YOLO reframes detection as a **single regression problem** from image pixels to spatially separated bounding boxes with associated class probabilities. The pipeline operates as follows: **Step 1 — Grid Decomposition** The input image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid. Each grid cell is responsible for detecting objects whose **center falls inside that cell**. In YOLOv1, $S = 7$, giving $49$ grid cells. **Step 2 — Per-Cell Tensor Prediction** Each grid cell outputs a fixed-length tensor: $$T = (B \times 5 + C)$$ where: - $B$ = number of bounding boxes per cell (YOLOv1: $B = 2$) - $5$ = $(x, y, w, h, \text{confidence})$ per box - $C$ = number of class probabilities (PASCAL VOC: $C = 20$) For YOLOv1, the output tensor shape is therefore $7 \times 7 \times 30$. **Step 3 — Confidence Score Decomposition** The confidence $c$ in each predicted box is formally defined as: $$c = \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred}}^{\text{truth}}$$ - If no object exists in the cell: $\Pr(\text{Object}) = 0 \Rightarrow c = 0$ - If an object exists: $c$ equals the IoU between predicted and ground-truth box. **Step 4 — Class Conditioning** For evaluation and inference, YOLO multiplies the conditional class probability by the box confidence: $$\Pr(\text{Class}_i \mid \text{Object}) \times \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU} = \Pr(\text{Class}_i) \times \text{IoU}$$ This produces a single class-specific confidence score per box. **Step 5 — Thresholding and Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** Boxes with confidence below a threshold (e.g., $0.5$) are discarded. NMS then removes duplicate detections of the same object. ### 2.2 The YOLO Loss Function YOLOv1's multi-part loss function is a cornerstone of the algorithm and a **guaranteed KTU exam topic**: $$\begin{aligned} L &= \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ \left( x_i - \hat{x}_i \right)^2 + \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2 \right] \\ &\quad + \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ \left( \sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i} \right)^2 + \left( \sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i} \right)^2 \right] \\ &\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left( C_i - \hat{C}_i \right)^2 \\ &\quad + \lambda_{\text{noobj}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{noobj}} \left( C_i - \hat{C}_i \right)^2 \\ &\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{\text{obj}} \sum_{c \in \text{classes}} \left( p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c) \right)^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Loss Component Breakdown:** | # | Term | Purpose | KTU Key Insight | |---|------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | $(x, y)$ regression | Center coordinate error | Only on responsible cells (obj indicator) | | 2 | $(\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h})$ regression | Width/Height error | Square root reduces sensitivity to large boxes | | 3 | $C$ confidence (obj) | Object presence error | High weight via $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ | | 4 | $C$ confidence (no-obj) | Background suppression | Low weight via $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$ | | 5 | $p(c)$ classification | Class probability error | Only on cells containing objects | ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Design Choices > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rationale (Why these design choices?):** > - **Square root on $(w, h)$:** Small errors in large boxes should not dominate errors in small boxes. Taking $\sqrt{}$ partially equalizes the loss contribution. > - **$\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$:** Localization error is rare but critical; upweight it. > - **$\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$:** Most cells are background; downweight to prevent confidence collapse. > - **$\mathbb{1}^{\text{obj}}$ indicator:** Ensures only the *best* responsible predictor for each object is penalized. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity. | # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Units / Range | Real-World Use | |---|------------------|-------------------|---------------|----------------| | 1 | **IoU (Jaccard Index)** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_{gt})}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_{gt})}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Box accuracy thresholding | | 2 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Quality of positive predictions | | 3 | **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Coverage of true objects | | 4 | **Average Precision (AP)** | $AP = \int_0^1 p(r)\, dr$ (area under PR curve) | $[0, 1]$ | Per-class detector quality | | 5 | **mAP (mean AP)** | $mAP = \dfrac{1}{N_c} \sum_{i=1}^{N_c} AP_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Overall detector benchmark | | 6 | **YOLO Confidence** | $c = \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Box reliability score | | 7 | **Class-Specific Score** | $\text{Score} = \Pr(\text{Class}_i \vert \text{Object}) \times c$ | $[0, 1]$ | Final detection confidence | | 8 | **Throughput** | $\text{FPS} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{inference}}}$ | frames/sec | Real-time viability | | 9 | **Output Tensor Dim** | $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$ | integer | YOLO architecture sizing | | 10 | **YOLOv1 Output** | $7 \times 7 \times 30$ | integer | Anchor-free baseline | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems):** Lane-departure warning, automatic emergency braking, adaptive cruise control all consume YOLO outputs. - **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) Communication:** Detected object metadata broadcast to nearby vehicles over DSRC/C-V2X. - **HD Map Construction:** Crowd-sourced object detection builds high-definition maps for autonomous fleets. - **Traffic Analytics:** Smart-city intersection monitoring reuses YOLOv5/YOLOv8 backbones. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: IoU Computation (Box Matching) **Problem Setup:** Given a predicted bounding box $B_p = (x_p, y_p, w_p, h_p)$ and a ground-truth box $B_{gt} = (x_{gt}, y_{gt}, w_{gt}, h_{gt})$ in image coordinates, derive the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric. **Step 1 — Convert to Corner Coordinates** Convert center-format boxes to corner-format (top-left and bottom-right): $$\begin{aligned} B_p^{\text{corner}} &= \left( x_{p}^{\min}, y_{p}^{\min}, x_{p}^{\max}, y_{p}^{\max} \right) \\ &= \left( x_p - \dfrac{w_p}{2},\ y_p - \dfrac{h_p}{2},\ x_p + \dfrac{w_p}{2},\ y_p + \dfrac{h_p}{2} \right) \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} B_{gt}^{\text{corner}} &= \left( x_{gt} - \dfrac{w_{gt}}{2},\ y_{gt} - \dfrac{h_{gt}}{2},\ x_{gt} + \dfrac{w_{gt}}{2},\ y_{gt} + \dfrac{h_{gt}}{2} \right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Intersection Rectangle Coordinates** The intersection rectangle $I$ has its top-left corner at the **max of the top-lefts** and its bottom-right at the **min of the bottom-rights**: $$\begin{aligned} x_I^{\min} &= \max\left( x_{p}^{\min},\ x_{gt}^{\min} \right) \\ y_I^{\min} &= \max\left( y_{p}^{\min},\ y_{gt}^{\min} \right) \\ x_I^{\max} &= \min\left( x_{p}^{\max},\ x_{gt}^{\max} \right) \\ y_I^{\max} &= \min\left( y_{p}^{\max},\ y_{gt}^{\max} \right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Intersection Area** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Area}(I) &= \max\left(0,\ x_I^{\max} - x_I^{\min}\right) \times \max\left(0,\ y_I^{\max} - y_I^{\min}\right) \end{aligned}$$ The $\max(0, \cdot)$ handles the **no-overlap case** cleanly. If the boxes do not overlap, the intersection area is exactly $0$. **Step 4 — Union Area** By the inclusion-exclusion principle: $$\text{Area}(U) = \text{Area}(B_p) + \text{Area}(B_{gt}) - \text{Area}(I)$$ where: $$\text{Area}(B_p) = w_p \cdot h_p, \quad \text{Area}(B_{gt}) = w_{gt} \cdot h_{gt}$$ **Step 5 — Final IoU** $$\boxed{\ \text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{Area}(I)}{\text{Area}(U)} = \dfrac{\text{Area}(I)}{w_p h_p + w_{gt} h_{gt} - \text{Area}(I)}\ }$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: IoU Calculation **Given:** $B_p = (50, 50, 40, 60)$ and $B_{gt} = (60, 60, 40, 60)$ (center format). **Step 1 — Convert to corners:** $$\begin{aligned} B_p &: \left( 50 - 20,\ 50 - 30,\ 50 + 20,\ 50 + 30 \right) = (30, 20, 70, 80) \\ B_{gt} &: \left( 60 - 20,\ 60 - 30,\ 60 + 20,\ 60 + 30 \right) = (40, 30, 80, 90) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Intersection corners:** $$\begin{aligned} x_I^{\min} &= \max(30, 40) = 40 \\ y_I^{\min} &= \max(20, 30) = 30 \\ x_I^{\max} &= \min(70, 80) = 70 \\ y_I^{\max} &= \min(80, 90) = 80 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Intersection area:** $$\text{Area}(I) = (70 - 40) \times (80 - 30) = 30 \times 50 = 1500$$ **Step 4 — Individual areas:** $$\text{Area}(B_p) = 40 \times 60 = 2400,\quad \text{Area}(B_{gt}) = 40 \times 60 = 2400$$ **Step 5 — Union area:** $$\text{Area}(U) = 2400 + 2400 - 1500 = 3300$$ **Step 6 — Final IoU:** $$\text{IoU} = \dfrac{1500}{3300} = \dfrac{5}{11} \approx 0.4545$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Since $\text{IoU} = 0.4545 < 0.5$, this prediction is classified as a **False Positive** under standard PASCAL VOC thresholds. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) Algorithm NMS eliminates redundant overlapping detections of the same object. The standard greedy NMS algorithm: **Algorithm — Greedy NMS:** 1. **Input:** Set of detected boxes $\mathcal{B} = \{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ each with confidence $c_i$, IoU threshold $\epsilon$ (e.g., $0.5$). 2. **Initialize** an empty keep-list $\mathcal{D} = \emptyset$. 3. **Repeat** until $\mathcal{B} = \emptyset$: - Select $B_m$ with maximum confidence: $c_m = \max_{i} c_i$. - Move $B_m$ from $\mathcal{B}$ to $\mathcal{D}$. - For every remaining $B_i \in \mathcal{B}$: - If $\text{IoU}(B_m, B_i) \geq \epsilon$, **discard** $B_i$ (it is a duplicate). 4. **Output:** $\mathcal{D}$ — the de-duplicated detection set. **Formal NMS Suppression Rule:** $$\text{Keep}(B_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \forall B_j \in \mathcal{D}: \text{IoU}(B_i, B_j) < \epsilon \\ 0 & \text{otherwise (suppressed)} \end{cases}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation: mAP from Precision-Recall Curve **Step 1 — Sort Predictions by Confidence** Sort all detections (across all classes) in descending order of confidence score. **Step 2 — Cumulative TP/FP Counting** Walk through sorted list. For each detection: - If $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$ with a *previously unmatched* ground truth → True Positive ($TP \mathrel{+}= 1$). - Else → False Positive ($FP \mathrel{+}= 1$). **Step 3 — Compute Precision and Recall at Each Step** $$P(k) = \dfrac{TP(k)}{TP(k) + FP(k)}, \quad R(k) = \dfrac{TP(k)}{N_{gt}}$$ **Step 4 — Area Under the PR Curve** Using the interpolated 11-point AP (PASCAL VOC 2007 style): $$AP = \dfrac{1}{11} \sum_{r \in \{0, 0.1, \ldots, 1.0\}} P_{\text{interp}}(r)$$ where: $$P_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max_{\tilde{r} \geq r} P(\tilde{r})$$ **Step 5 — Mean over Classes** $$mAP = \dfrac{1}{N_c} \sum_{c=1}^{N_c} AP_c$$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: YOLO Variants Comparison > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Expectation:** Students must be able to differentiate YOLO versions. The following table is exam gold. | Variant | Backbone | Input Size | FPS (Typical) | mAP@0.5 | Year | Key Innovation | |---------|----------|------------|---------------|---------|------|----------------| | YOLOv1 | GoogLeNet-like | $448 \times 448$ | $45$ | $63.4$ | 2016 | First unified single-stage detector | | YOLOv2 | Darknet-19 | $416 \times 416$ | $67$ | $76.8$ | 2017 | Anchor boxes, multi-scale, BN | | YOLOv3 | Darknet-53 | $608 \times 608$ | $20$ | $82.3$ | 2018 | Multi-scale FPN-like predictions | | YOLOv4 | CSPDarknet53 | $640 \times 640$ | $65$ | $85.4$ | 2020 | Mish activation, PANet, mosaic aug. | | YOLOv5 | CSP-Darknet (PyTorch) | $640 \times 640$ | $140$ | $87.3$ | 2020 | AutoAnchor, Focus layer | | YOLOv8 | C2f-Darknet | $640 \times 640$ | $165$ | $89.5$ | 2023 | Anchor-free, decoupled head | | YOLOv9 | GELAN | $640 \times 640$ | $120$ | $91.1$ | 2024 | PGI, GELAN architecture | ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Real-Time Challenges in AV | Challenge | Root Cause | Engineering Solution | |-----------|------------|----------------------| | Motion blur | High vehicle speed ($\geq 100$ km/h) | Higher shutter speed, deblurring CNNs | | Occlusion | Dense traffic | Temporal smoothing, multi-frame tracking | | Adverse weather | Rain/fog/snow/glare | Sensor fusion (LiDAR + Radar + Camera) | | Small object detection | Traffic signs at distance | Multi-scale FPN, tiling inference | | Latency | GPU/CPU bottleneck | TensorRT, ONNX, INT8 quantization | | Class imbalance | Pedestrians rarer than cars | Focal loss, oversampling | | Adversarial attacks | Malicious stickers on stop signs | Defensive distillation, ensemble | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Autonomous Vehicle Perception Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Camera Sensor Array
Front Wide-Angle FOV 120 deg] --> B[Frame Preprocessing
Resize 640x640 Normalize BGR to RGB] B --> C[YOLO Backbone
CSPDarknet53 C2f Block] C --> D[Feature Pyramid Network
Multi Scale P3 P4 P5] D --> E[Decoupled Detection Head
Classification Branch] D --> F[Decoupled Detection Head
Regression Branch] E --> G[Class Probability Map
NxCxHxW Tensor] F --> H[Bounding Box Tensor
4 Coordinates per Anchor] G --> I[Confidence Threshold Filter
Score greater than 0.5] H --> I I --> J[Non Maximum Suppression
IoU Threshold 0.5] J --> K[Final Detection Set
Vehicles Pedestrians Signs] K --> L[Object Tracker
SORT or DeepSORT] L --> M[Trajectory Predictor
Kalman Filter] M --> N[Path Planning Module
Behavior Policy] N --> O[Vehicle Actuator Control
Steering Throttle Brake] ``` ### 4.2 YOLO Network Architecture — End-to-End Tensor Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR inp[Input Image
640x640x3] --> c1[Conv Block 1
320 channels stride 2] c1 --> c2[Conv Block 2
640 channels stride 2] c2 --> c3[C2f Block 3
640 channels] c3 --> c4[Conv Block 4
1280 channels stride 2] c4 --> c5[C2f Block 5
1280 channels] c5 --> c6[Conv Block 6
640 channels stride 2] c6 --> c7[C2f Block 7
640 channels] c7 --> up1[Upsample x2] c5 --> up1 up1 --> cat1[Concat with C5] cat1 --> c8[C2f Block 8
640 channels] c8 --> up2[Upsample x2] c4 --> up2 up2 --> cat2[Concat with C4] cat2 --> c9[C2f Block 9
320 channels P3 output] c9 --> d1[Detect Head P3
Small objects] c8 --> d2[Detect Head P4
Medium objects] c5 --> d3[Detect Head P5
Large objects] d1 --> out[Final Output
Detection Tensors] d2 --> out d3 --> out ``` ### 4.3 YOLO Inference Flow — Grid Cell to Bounding Box ```mermaid flowchart TD s1[Step 1 Divide image into SxS grid] --> s2[Step 2 Each cell predicts B bounding boxes] s2 --> s3[Step 3 Predict class probabilities per cell] s3 --> s4[Step 4 Multiply class prob by box confidence] s4 --> s5[Step 5 Threshold by confidence score] s5 --> s6[Step 6 Apply Non Maximum Suppression] s6 --> s7[Step 7 Output final detection list] ``` ### 4.4 Data Flow: Video Feed to Actuator Command ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Cam as Front Camera participant ECU as Onboard GPU ECU participant YOLO as YOLO Inference Engine participant Track as Multi Object Tracker participant Plan as Path Planner participant Veh as Vehicle Actuators Cam->>ECU: Raw video frame 30 FPS ECU->>YOLO: Preprocessed tensor 640x640 YOLO->>YOLO: Forward pass CNN backbone YOLO->>Track: Detection list with scores Track->>Track: Associate IDs across frames Track->>Plan: Tracked object trajectories Plan->>Plan: Compute safe trajectory Plan->>Veh: Steering angle and throttle Veh-->>Cam: Vehicle moves to new position ``` ### 4.5 Bounding Box Regression Visualization (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Grid Cell i,j
Responsible for object center] --> B[Anchor Box Prior
Pre defined WxH] B --> C[Predicted Offsets
tx ty tw th] C --> D[Decoded Box
x = sigmoid tx plus cx
y = sigmoid ty plus cy
w = pw times exp tw
h = ph times exp th] D --> E[Final Bounding Box
Pixel coordinates] E --> F{IoU with Ground Truth
greater than 0.5} F -->|Yes| G[True Positive] F -->|No| H[False Positive] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`** **Q1. Define Object Detection. Explain how it differs from Image Classification.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Image Classification (1 mark):** Assigns a single class label to an entire input image. Output: a vector of class probabilities. No spatial localization. - **Object Detection (2 marks):** Performs both classification and localization. It identifies *what* objects are present (class label) and *where* they are in the image (bounding box coordinates). The output is a variable-length set of $(c_i, x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i, s_i)$ tuples where $s_i$ is the confidence score. - **Key Distinction:** Classification answers *"What is in the image?"* Detection answers *"What is in the image AND where is it?"* --- > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q2. What is YOLO? List any two advantages of YOLO over R-CNN.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a single-stage, real-time object detection algorithm that frames detection as a single regression problem, predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities in a single neural network forward pass. - **Advantage 1 — Speed (1 mark):** YOLO processes images at 45-165 FPS because it uses a single CNN, whereas R-CNN requires $\sim 2000$ region proposals per image and runs a separate CNN for each, making it $1000\times$ slower. - **Advantage 2 — Global Context (1 mark):** YOLO sees the entire image during training and inference, encoding contextual information about classes (e.g., it rarely confuses a stop sign with other traffic signs), whereas R-CNN's region proposals operate on local patches. --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **Question Paper Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Expected Pattern]`** **(a)** Explain the YOLO algorithm in detail with its architecture, grid cell prediction mechanism, and the multi-part loss function. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Architecture (2 marks):** - YOLO uses a single CNN inspired by GoogLeNet with 24 convolutional layers followed by 2 fully connected layers. - Input image is resized to $448 \times 448$. - The final fully connected layer outputs a $7 \times 7 \times 30$ tensor. **Grid Cell Mechanism (2 marks):** - Image is divided into an $S \times S = 7 \times 7$ grid. - Each grid cell predicts $B = 2$ bounding boxes. - Each bounding box has 5 parameters: $(x, y, w, h, c)$ where $(x, y)$ is the box center relative to the cell, $(w, h)$ are normalized to image dimensions, and $c$ is the confidence score. - Each cell also predicts $C = 20$ conditional class probabilities. - Final tensor per cell: $B \times 5 + C = 2 \times 5 + 20 = 30$. **Multi-Part Loss Function (3 marks):** The total loss is a sum of five components (write the 5-term equation from Section 2.2): 1. **Localization loss** for $(x, y)$ — squared error, weighted by $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$. 2. **Localization loss** for $(w, h)$ — squared error on $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$. 3. **Object confidence loss** — squared error for cells containing objects. 4. **No-object confidence loss** — squared error, down-weighted by $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$. 5. **Classification loss** — sum of squared errors on class probabilities. Indicator function $\mathbb{1}_{i}^{\text{obj}}$ ensures only the responsible predictor is penalized. --- **(b)** With a suitable example, derive the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric step-by-step. Why is an IoU threshold of 0.5 commonly used? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** (Present the full 5-step derivation from Section 3.1, ending with the boxed IoU formula.) **Worked Example (2 marks):** Use the numerical example from Section 3.2 ($B_p = (50, 50, 40, 60)$, $B_{gt} = (60, 60, 40, 60)$) yielding $\text{IoU} = 5/11 \approx 0.4545$. **Why IoU Threshold of 0.5? (2 marks):** - **Empirical Optimality:** The PASCAL VOC challenge empirically determined 0.5 as the threshold that best balances false positives (overly strict) and false negatives (overly lenient). - **Mathematical Balance:** $\text{IoU} = 0.5$ corresponds roughly to a 41% area overlap — meaningful spatial agreement without demanding pixel-perfect alignment. - **Standardization:** Enables fair comparison across algorithms and datasets (COCO uses 0.5 as one of its primary metrics, with additional thresholds 0.75 and 0.5:0.95 averaged). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Actual Pattern]`** **(a)** Explain the Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) algorithm. Why is it essential for YOLO inference? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Why NMS is Essential (2 marks):** - YOLO predicts $7 \times 7 \times 2 = 98$ bounding boxes per image. - The same object (e.g., one car) is often detected by multiple overlapping grid cells, producing 5-10 redundant boxes per object. - Without NMS, a single car would appear as 5 separate detections, breaking downstream tracking and control. **NMS Algorithm (5 marks):** Present the 4-step greedy NMS algorithm from Section 3.3: 1. **Input:** $\mathcal{B} = \{B_1, \ldots, B_n\}$ with confidences $c_i$, threshold $\epsilon$. 2. **Initialize** $\mathcal{D} = \emptyset$. 3. **Loop:** Pick max-confidence box $B_m$, add to $\mathcal{D}$, remove all $B_i$ with $\text{IoU}(B_m, B_i) \geq \epsilon$. 4. **Output:** $\mathcal{D}$ — final non-redundant detection set. **Mathematical Formulation:** Include the keep/discard decision rule from Section 3.3. --- **(b)** Discuss the key challenges of real-time object detection in autonomous vehicles and explain how YOLO variants (v3 through v8) address them. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Challenges (3.5 marks):** 1. **Small object detection:** Traffic signs at 50 m subtend only $\sim 20 \times 20$ pixels. YOLOv1's $7 \times 7$ grid is too coarse. 2. **Latency:** Safety requires $\leq 100$ ms inference. Pure CNN forward pass must be optimized. 3. **Multi-scale objects:** Cars appear from $5$ px (distant) to $500$ px (close). Single-scale heads fail. 4. **Class imbalance:** Bicycles and pedestrians are far rarer than cars in training data. **YOLO Evolution Addressing These (3.5 marks):** | Challenge | Solution | YOLO Version | |-----------|----------|--------------| | Small objects | Multi-scale FPN predictions at 3 scales | YOLOv3 (2018) | | Latency | CSPDarknet, depth-wise separable convs | YOLOv4/v5 | | Multi-scale | PANet bidirectional feature fusion | YOLOv4 | | Class imbalance | Focal loss integration | YOLOv5 | | Anchor inefficiency | Anchor-free decoupled head | YOLOv8 | **Conclusion:** Each YOLO iteration trades off speed vs. accuracy differently, with v5/v8 optimized for edge deployment (TensorRT/ONNX compatible for in-car ECUs). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the indicator function $\mathbb{1}^{\text{obj}}$ in the loss function** — the examiner deducts 2 marks for treating all cells equally. > 2. **Confusing classification accuracy with detection accuracy** — classification is just *one* part of detection. > 3. **Not mentioning NMS** — failing to explain how duplicate boxes are eliminated is a 3-mark deduction in YOLO questions. > 4. **Confusing $\text{mAP}_{0.5}$ with $\text{mAP}_{0.5:0.95}$** — PASCAL VOC uses the former; COCO uses the latter. > 5. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ trick** — this is a classic KTU "trick question" worth 1 mark. > 6. **Writing single-stage as "one forward pass" without explaining what stage refers to** — clarify it is *one* CNN, versus R-CNN's *two* (region proposal + classification). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Real-Time Detection in AVs** - **Core Definition:** Object detection = classification + localization. Real-time means $\geq 30$ FPS or $\leq 33$ ms/frame latency. - **YOLO Core:** Single CNN, one forward pass, $S \times S$ grid, $B$ boxes/cell, output tensor $S \times S \times (B \times 5 + C)$. - **YOLOv1 Tensor:** $7 \times 7 \times 30$ (S=7, B=2, C=20). - **Confidence:** $c = \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU}$. - **Class Score:** $\Pr(\text{Class}_i \mid \text{Object}) \times c$. - **Loss has 5 terms:** center, size (with $\sqrt{}$), object conf, no-object conf, classification. - **Loss Hyperparameters:** $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$, $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$. - **NMS:** Greedy algorithm; pick max confidence, suppress IoU $\geq \epsilon$. - **IoU Formula:** $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_{gt})}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_{gt})}$. - **Standard IoU Threshold:** $0.5$ for PASCAL VOC TP classification. - **mAP:** Mean of Average Precisions across all classes; computed from Precision-Recall curve area. - **Key YOLO Variants:** v1 (basic), v3 (multi-scale FPN), v5 (PyTorch), v8 (anchor-free). - **AV Challenges:** Small objects, latency, multi-scale, class imbalance, weather. - **YOLO Advantages vs R-CNN:** $100\times$ faster, global context, end-to-end trainable. - **YOLO Disadvantages:** Lower mAP on small objects, struggles with crowded scenes, localization less precise than two-stage methods. - **Real-World Use Cases:** Tesla Autopilot, Mobileye, Waymo open dataset, KITTI benchmark. - **Dataset Benchmarks:** KITTI (AV-focused), Cityscapes (urban), BDD100K (diverse driving), COCO (general). - **Deployment:** TensorRT, ONNX Runtime, INT8 quantization for in-car ECUs like NVIDIA Orin/Driven PX.

topics.title: R-CNN etc. - Develop a Machine Learning Model to Automatically Detect and Classify Diseases from Medical Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT504 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** R-CNN etc. - Develop a Machine Learning Model to Automatically Detect and Classify Diseases from Medical Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — R-CNN Family & Automated Medical Image Disease Detection

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network)** is a family of object detection architectures that integrate **region proposal generation** with **deep convolutional feature extraction** to simultaneously perform *localization* (drawing a bounding box around the object) and *classification* (labelling the object) within an image. The R-CNN paradigm—originally proposed by Girshick et al. (2014) and progressively refined into **Fast R-CNN (2015)** and **Faster R-CNN (2015)**—forms the foundational two-stage detection framework in modern computer vision.

In the medical imaging context, R-CNN-style architectures are deployed to **automatically detect and classify pathologies** (e.g., tumours, nodules, lesions, fractures, microcalcifications) from radiological scans such as X-rays, CT slices, MRI volumes, mammograms, and histopathology slides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The course explicitly mandates understanding of the R-CNN family of detectors and their *practical engineering utility* in building **end-to-end ML pipelines for medical image analysis**, including pre-processing, region proposal, feature extraction, classification, and post-processing.

### 1.2 The R-CNN Evolution Trilogy

| Version | Year | Key Innovation | Region Proposal | Inference Speed (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R-CNN** | 2014 | CNN features + SVM classifier | Selective Search | ~47 s/image |
| **Fast R-CNN** | 2015 | Shared convolution, ROI Pooling | Selective Search | ~2 s/image |
| **Faster R-CNN** | 2015 | Region Proposal Network (RPN) | **RPN (learned)** | ~0.2 s/image |
| **Mask R-CNN** | 2017 | Adds pixel-level instance segmentation | RPN | ~0.2 s/image |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radiologist's Assistant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Specialist Doctor Workflow**
>
> Imagine a **radiologist** scanning a chest X-ray for signs of pneumonia. The radiologist's brain, *without conscious effort*, does this:
> 1. **Glance at the whole image** to spot "regions of interest" (RoI) — e.g., opaque patches in the lungs.
> 2. **Zoom in** to each region and analyze it carefully.
> 3. **Classify** what each region shows (normal, nodule, mass, infection).
> 4. **Mark the boundary** (bounding box) around the abnormality.
> 5. **Decide on a diagnosis** with a confidence score.
>
> **R-CNN does exactly this — but in silicon.** The *Selective Search / RPN* is the "glance" (region proposal), the *CNN backbone* is the "zoom in & analyze", and the *SVM/softmax head + bounding-box regressor* is the "classify and mark boundary".
>
> In the **medical domain**, the "patient" is a CT/MRI scan, the "doctor" is the trained CNN, and the "diagnosis report" is the output tensor containing bounding boxes, class labels, and confidence scores.

### 1.4 Formal Definition of the Detection Problem

Given an input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$, the detection model must produce a set of predictions:

$$\mathcal{P} = \{(b_i, c_i, s_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$

where for each detected object $i$:

- $b_i = (x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i)$ — the **bounding box** (centre coordinates, width, height)
- $c_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ — the **class label** (among $K$ disease categories, plus a "background" class)
- $s_i \in [0, 1]$ — the **confidence score** (probability of correct classification)

### 1.5 Medical Imaging Specific Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Medical Imaging Terms used in the R-CNN pipeline:**
>
> - **Region of Interest (RoI):** A candidate sub-region in the scan likely to contain pathology.
> - **Lesion:** Any abnormal tissue — a tumour, ulcer, plaque, or nodule.
> - **Nodule:** A small, rounded opacity, typically 3 mm – 30 mm in diameter (lung CT).
> - **Microcalcification:** Tiny calcium deposits in breast tissue (mammography).
> - **Dice / IoU Coefficient:** Overlap metrics between predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes / segmentation masks. A Dice score > **0.7** is conventionally accepted as a "good" detection in medical AI literature.
> - **mAP (mean Average Precision):** The gold-standard detection metric averaged over all disease classes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The R-CNN Architecture — Step-by-Step Pipeline

### Stage 1: Region Proposal Generation (Selective Search)

**Selective Search** is a *traditional, non-learned* algorithm that hierarchically merges super-pixels based on four similarity cues:

$$s(r_i, r_j) = a_1 \cdot s_{\text{colour}}(r_i, r_j) + a_2 \cdot s_{\text{texture}}(r_i, r_j) + a_3 \cdot s_{\text{size}}(r_i, r_j) + a_4 \cdot s_{\text{fill}}(r_i, r_j)$$

For each image, Selective Search produces approximately **2000 region proposals**, which are then warped (resized) to a fixed $227 \times 227$ input and passed through the CNN.

### Stage 2: Deep Feature Extraction

Each warped region is independently passed through a **pre-trained CNN backbone** (e.g., AlexNet, VGG-16, ResNet-50). The output is a **4096-dimensional feature vector** $f \in \mathbb{R}^{4096}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why fixed size?** AlexNet/VGG originally used fully-connected (FC) layers that demanded fixed input dimensions. Hence region *warping* (not preserving aspect ratio) was historically required. This bottleneck was removed in Fast R-CNN via **ROI Pooling**.

### Stage 3: Class-Specific Classification (SVM)

A **one-vs-rest linear SVM** is trained per class:

$$f_c(w) = w_c^T \phi(x) + b_c \quad \text{for each class } c \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$$

Decision function:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \; w_c^T \phi(x) + b_c$$

### Stage 4: Bounding-Box Regression

To refine the predicted box, R-CNN trains a class-specific regressor:

$$t_x = \frac{G_x - P_x}{P_w}, \quad t_y = \frac{G_y - P_y}{P_h}$$

$$t_w = \log\left(\frac{G_w}{P_w}\right), \quad t_h = \log\left(\frac{G_h}{P_h}\right)$$

where $(P_x, P_y, P_w, P_h)$ is the proposal box and $(G_x, G_y, G_w, G_h)$ is the ground-truth box. The targets $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ are predicted by ridge regression.

---

## 2.2 Fast R-CNN — The Shared-Convolution Revolution

Fast R-CNN eliminates the inefficiency of running the CNN 2000 times per image. The full image is processed **once** through the backbone, and **Region of Interest (RoI) Pooling** extracts a fixed-size feature map for each proposal.

### RoI Pooling Operation

For an RoI defined by $(r, c, h, w)$ on the feature map:

1. Divide the $h \times w$ RoI into an $H' \times W'$ grid (e.g., $7 \times 7$).
2. Apply max-pooling within each sub-window.

The output is a fixed $H' \times W'$ feature map, which is flattened and fed into FC layers.

### Multi-Task Loss in Fast R-CNN

$$L(p, u, t^u, v) = L_{\text{cls}}(p, u) + \lambda \, [u \geq 1] \, L_{\text{loc}}(t^u, v)$$

where:

- $p = (p_0, \ldots, p_K)$ — predicted class probabilities (softmax)
- $u$ — true class
- $t^u = (t_x^u, t_y^u, t_w^u, t_h^u)$ — predicted bounding-box regression offsets for class $u$
- $v = (v_x, v_y, v_w, v_h)$ — ground-truth regression targets
- $\lambda$ — balancing hyperparameter (typically 1)
- $[u \geq 1]$ — Iverson bracket (= 1 if $u$ is a foreground class, 0 if background)

#### Classification Loss (Cross-Entropy)

$$L_{\text{cls}}(p, u) = -\log p_u$$

#### Bounding-Box Regression Loss (Smooth L1)

$$L_{\text{loc}}(t^u, v) = \sum_{i \in \{x, y, w, h\}} \text{smooth}_{L_1}(t_i^u - v_i)$$

where the **Smooth L1** function is defined as:

$$\text{smooth}_{L_1}(x) = \begin{cases} 0.5 \, x^2 & \text{if } \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \vert x \vert - 0.5 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Smooth L1 over L2?** L2 loss is highly sensitive to outliers (large errors get squared and dominate training). Smooth L1 is more robust — it behaves quadratically for small errors (smooth gradients) and linearly for large errors (bounded gradient). This was a key empirical finding of the Fast R-CNN paper.

---

## 2.3 Faster R-CNN — Replacing Selective Search with RPN

The **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** is a small fully-convolutional network that slides over the CNN feature map and predicts *objectness* scores and box offsets at each spatial location.

### Anchor Boxes

At each sliding-window position, $k$ anchor boxes of different scales and aspect ratios are evaluated:

- **Scales:** $\{128^2, 256^2, 512^2\}$ pixels
- **Aspect Ratios:** $\{1:1, 1:2, 2:1\}$
- **Total anchors per position:** $k = 3 \times 3 = 9$

The RPN outputs:

- **2k scores** — objectness (foreground / background) per anchor.
- **4k coordinates** — bounding-box regression deltas per anchor.

### RPN Loss Function

$$L(\{p_i\}, \{t_i\}) = \frac{1}{N_{\text{cls}}} \sum_i L_{\text{cls}}(p_i, p_i^*) + \lambda \, \frac{1}{N_{\text{reg}}} \sum_i p_i^* \, L_{\text{reg}}(t_i, t_i^*)$$

where:

- $p_i$ — predicted probability that anchor $i$ is an object
- $p_i^*$ — ground-truth label (1 if positive anchor, 0 otherwise)
- $t_i$ — predicted box offsets
- $t_i^*$ — ground-truth box offsets
- $L_{\text{cls}}$ — binary cross-entropy
- $L_{\text{reg}}$ — Smooth L1 loss
- $N_{\text{cls}}, N_{\text{reg}}$ — normalization constants
- $\lambda$ — balancing weight (typically 10)

### Anchor-to-Box Conversion

Given an anchor with centre $(x_a, y_a)$ and dimensions $(w_a, h_a)$:

$$x = x_a + w_a \, t_x, \quad y = y_a + h_a \, t_y$$

$$w = w_a \, \exp(t_w), \quad h = h_a \, \exp(t_h)$$

---

## 2.4 Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)

After detection, multiple overlapping boxes remain. NMS prunes them:

**Algorithm:**

1. Select the box $B$ with the highest confidence score.
2. Remove all boxes $B_i$ where $\text{IoU}(B, B_i) > \text{threshold}$ (typically 0.5).
3. Repeat until no boxes remain.

### Intersection over Union (IoU)

$$\text{IoU}(B_p, B_g) = \frac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_g)}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_g)}$$

### Dice Coefficient (Medical Imaging Favourite)

$$D(A, B) = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

Dice and IoU are monotonically related:

$$D = \frac{2 \, \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$$

---

## 2.5 Mean Average Precision (mAP) — The Detection Metric

For each class $c$:

$$\text{AP}_c = \int_0^1 p_c(r) \, dr$$

where $p_c(r)$ is the precision at recall $r$. In practice, computed via the **11-point interpolated AP**:

$$\text{AP}_c = \frac{1}{11} \sum_{r \in \{0, 0.1, \ldots, 1.0\}} p_{\text{interp}}(r)$$

$$p_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max_{\tilde{r} \geq r} p(\tilde{r})$$

Then:

$$\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{c=1}^{K} \text{AP}_c$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear repeatedly in KTU questions and competitive exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IoU** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{A \cap B}{A \cup B}$ | 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect) |
| 2 | **Dice Coefficient** | $D = \dfrac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Medical AI standard; > 0.7 = good |
| 3 | **Box Regression Target (x, y)** | $t_x = \dfrac{G_x - P_x}{P_w}, \quad t_y = \dfrac{G_y - P_y}{P_h}$ | Normalized offsets |
| 4 | **Box Regression Target (w, h)** | $t_w = \log \dfrac{G_w}{P_w}, \quad t_h = \log \dfrac{G_h}{P_h}$ | Log-space |
| 5 | **Box Decoding** | $x = x_a + w_a t_x, \quad w = w_a e^{t_w}$ | Inverse of above |
| 6 | **Smooth L1 Loss** | $0.5 x^2$ if $\vert x \vert < 1$, else $\vert x \vert - 0.5$ | Robust to outliers |
| 7 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Probabilities |
| 8 | **Fast R-CNN Multi-Task Loss** | $L = L_{\text{cls}} + \lambda [u \geq 1] L_{\text{loc}}$ | $\lambda = 1$ |
| 9 | **mAP** | $\text{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_c \text{AP}_c$ | Higher is better |
| 10 | **Anchors per Position** | $k = n_{\text{scales}} \times n_{\text{ratios}}$ | Typically 9 (3 × 3) |
| 11 | **NMS Threshold** | $\text{IoU} > 0.5 \Rightarrow$ suppress | Configurable |
| 12 | **FPS (Inference)** | $\text{FPS} = \dfrac{1}{t_{\text{per-image}}}$ | Real-time $\geq$ 30 FPS |
| 13 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | 0 to 1 |
| 14 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Critical in medical AI (must be high) |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in the Real World

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Faster R-CNN dominates medical AI:**
>
> 1. **High localization accuracy** — two-stage detectors consistently outperform one-stage YOLO/SSD in medical scans where lesions are small and class-imbalanced.
> 2. **Transfer learning friendly** — pre-trained on ImageNet/COCO, then fine-tuned on small medical datasets (e.g., LIDC-IDRI for lung nodules, BreakHis for breast cancer).
> 3. **Interpretable outputs** — bounding boxes with confidence scores can be overlaid on the scan, supporting **clinician-in-the-loop** workflows.
> 4. **Extension to 3D** — used in 3D volumetric CT/MRI analysis by processing axial slices or using 3D backbones (e.g., 3D ResNet).
> 5. **Regulatory acceptance** — bounding-box outputs are easier to *explain* in FDA / CE-mark submissions than end-to-end black-box classifiers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bounding-Box Regression Transformation

We derive the parameterization that converts a proposal box into a regression target.

### Setup

Let $P = (P_x, P_y, P_w, P_h)$ be the **proposal** (centre x, centre y, width, height).  
Let $G = (G_x, G_y, G_w, G_h)$ be the **ground-truth** box.

We seek a transformation $d_\star(P) = \hat{G}$ that approximates $G$ via a learned shift and scale.

### Step 1 — Translation Normalization

To make the regression *scale-invariant*, we normalize the centre-shift by the proposal dimensions:

$$
d_x(P) = \frac{G_x - P_x}{P_w}, \quad d_y(P) = \frac{G_y - P_y}{P_h}
$$

**Reasoning:** A 5-pixel shift in a 100-pixel-wide box is less significant than a 5-pixel shift in a 20-pixel-wide box. Dividing by $P_w$ removes this scale dependence.

### Step 2 — Log-Space Scaling

For width and height, we use a *log-transform* to enforce positive predictions and convert multiplicative scaling to additive offset:

$$
d_w(P) = \log\!\left(\frac{G_w}{P_w}\right), \quad d_h(P) = \log\!\left(\frac{G_h}{P_h}\right)
$$

**Reasoning:** Width/height must remain positive. By predicting $d_w$ and decoding as $G_w = P_w \cdot \exp(d_w)$, positivity is guaranteed. Also, $\log(2) = -\log(0.5)$, so a 2× expansion and 0.5× contraction are equidistant in $d$-space — a desirable symmetry.

### Step 3 — Vectorization
